title: The Gutter and the Awakening
content:
The cobblestones were wet with night rain and something worse. Zhao Feng's blood, leaking from his shattered ribs, pooled in the cracks between stones. His breath came in rattling gasps, each one a knife twisting in his chest.

Above him, the sky of Suzhou stretched black and indifferent. Somewhere in the distance, a dog barked. The sound seemed to come from very far away, as if he were already sinking into another world.

His sons. His flesh and blood.

He had taught them everything. The art of the deal. The patience of wealth. The thousand small cruelties required to build an empire from nothing. And they had learned so well that they turned those lessons on him.

Zhao Da had been the one to speak. "Father, you've lived long enough."

Zhao Er had held the knife.

Zhao San had counted the money.

Zhao Si and Zhao Wu had argued over who would get his manor outside the city walls.

Forty years of building. Forty years of scraping and cheating and fighting. Forty years of turning copper into silver and silver into gold, until he was the richest man in the Central Plains, wealthier than some minor cultivation sects. And in the end, it meant nothing.

His hand twitched. A rat scurried past his fingers, investigating whether the meat was still warm.

Zhao Feng tried to curse them. Tried to curse his own stupidity. But his throat filled with blood and all that came out was a wet, gurgling sound.

His last thought, before the darkness took him, was not of revenge.

It was of a deal he had missed, sixty years ago. A qi stone mine in the northern hills. He had been eighteen, working as a laborer, and he had seen the signs—a certain shimmer in the rock, a certain taste to the air. He had known, with the instinct that would later make him rich, that a vein lay beneath the surface. But he had lacked the capital. Lacked the courage. So he had said nothing, and another man had claimed the discovery, and that man had become the foundation of a fortune that Zhao Feng had spent forty years chasing.

If he could go back. If he could just—

The darkness took him.

---

The cough came first. A violent, wracking thing that sent pain screaming through his chest and forced his eyes open.

Light. Too much light. The sun was pouring through a crack in a wooden ceiling, stabbing directly into his retinas. Zhao Feng blinked, tried to raise his hand to shield his eyes, and found that his arm weighed more than a mountain.

Every muscle ached. Every joint screamed. His skin felt too tight, his bones too small, his lungs too full of dust and smoke and the sharp, mineral tang of—

He froze.

The qi stone mine. He knew that smell. Had known it since he was a boy, when his father had first dragged him into the tunnels at the age of seven. It was the smell of crushed rock and released qi, of earth that had been broken open to bleed its treasures.

Zhao Feng sat up so fast his vision swam.

He was in a bunkhouse. A long, narrow room lined with wooden pallets, each one covered in a thin straw mattress. The walls were rough-cut timber, the floor packed dirt. A dozen other men lay sprawled on their beds, some snoring, some already stirring with the morning light.

His hands.

He looked down at his hands. They were not the hands of an old man—gnarled, spotted, trembling with age. These were young hands. Smooth skin, strong fingers, calluses still fresh. He turned them over, flexed them, watched the tendons move beneath the surface.

"Hey." A rough voice from across the room. "You awake? The whistle's about to blow."

Zhao Feng looked up. The man who had spoken was gangly and pock-faced, maybe twenty years old, with a scar running through one eyebrow. Something stirred in Zhao Feng's memory.

Zhang Hu. That was his name. A fellow laborer in the Northern Hills mine. They had worked together for two years, until Zhang Hu had been killed in a tunnel collapse.

Sixty years ago.

Zhao Feng's heart began to pound.

"What day is it?" The words came out rough, unfamiliar. He had to push them through a throat that felt unused to speaking.

Zhang Hu gave him a strange look. "You hit your head? It's the fifteenth of the Fire Month. Same as yesterday."

The fifteenth of the Fire Month. Fourth year of the Jade Phoenix Emperor's reign.

Zhao Feng's mind raced backward through the years, calculating, checking. If this was the fourth year of the Jade Phoenix, then he was eighteen years old. And the vein—

"Get up," Zhang Hu said, already pulling on his boots. "The foreman's in a mood. He'll dock us if we're late."

The other men were stirring now, groaning and cursing as they rolled off their pallets. Zhao Feng watched them like a man watching ghosts. He knew these men. Knew their names, their fates, the small tragedies of their lives. Old Wu would lose three fingers in a blasting accident next spring. Little Jiang would desert the mine and join a wandering bandit gang, only to be executed two years later. And Chen the Mute would die in a cave-in that would take Zhang Hu with him, the same cave-in that—

Zhao Feng stopped breathing.

He remembered now. The cave-in that killed Zhang Hu and Chen the Mute. It happened in the autumn of the fifth year. But that cave-in was caused by a poorly supported tunnel that the foreman had been too cheap to reinforce. And that tunnel had been dug in a hurry because—

Because of the vein.

The vein of high-grade qi stones. The fortune that had slipped through his fingers sixty years ago.

Zhao Feng's hands began to shake. Not with fear. With calculation.

He knew where the vein was. He had spent forty years regretting that he hadn't claimed it, studying maps, talking to geologists, tracing the underground currents of qi that led to such deposits. He knew exactly where to dig. He knew exactly how deep to go. He knew the direction the vein ran, the quality of the stones it contained, the exact volume of the deposit.

And he knew that the discovery would be made today.

A young cultivator, traveling through the hills, had stopped at the mine to rest. He had sensed the qi disturbance in the ground, asked a few questions, and then made an offer to the mine owner. A pittance, really. A fraction of what the vein was worth. But the mine owner had been greedy and stupid, had accepted the offer on the spot, and had spent the rest of his life watching the cultivator grow rich on his land.

The cultivator's name was Wang Chen. A minor disciple of the Crimson Sun Sect. He would use the wealth from this vein to buy his way into the sect's inner circle, eventually becoming an elder.

Zhao Feng had hated Wang Chen for sixty years. Not because Wang Chen had stolen the vein—Wang Chen had simply been smarter. But because Zhao Feng had been too afraid to act.

Not this time.

"Are you coming or not?" Zhang Hu was at the door, frowning at him.

Zhao Feng swung his legs off the pallet. His body moved with a youth's lithe strength, and the sensation was so foreign that he almost stumbled. He caught himself, steadied his breathing, and stood.

"I'm coming."

The bunkhouse emptied into the morning light. The Northern Hills mine sprawled across a valley that had been carved open by decades of digging. Terraces of raw earth rose in stepped layers, punctuated by the dark mouths of tunnels. Men moved across the slopes like ants, carrying picks and baskets and lengths of rope.

Zhao Feng followed the stream of workers, but his eyes were scanning. Looking for something. Someone.

There.

A figure stood apart from the workers, near the entrance of the main tunnel. He was young, maybe twenty, dressed in robes of pale blue silk that marked him as a cultivator. His stance was relaxed, his hands clasped behind his back, his gaze fixed on a particular patch of ground near the tunnel's mouth.

Wang Chen. Already here. Already sensing the qi.

Zhao Feng's mind raced. In his past life, he had watched Wang Chen make his offer to the mine owner, Old Man Liu. He had watched the deal be struck. And he had done nothing.

But he was not eighteen years old and useless anymore. He was Zhao Feng, the Merchant Prince of the Central Plains, a man who had negotiated deals that moved armies and shifted the borders of provinces. He had forty years of cunning inside this young body.

The trick was simple. He had to get to the vein first.

"Zhang Hu," Zhao Feng said, keeping his voice low. "How much does the foreman pay for a new tunnel claim?"

Zhang Hu blinked at him. "What? The standard. Three silver for a claim, plus ten percent of whatever you pull out."

"And if I find a vein? A good one?"

"Then you'd better pray the foreman doesn't decide to take it from you. You know how it works. We dig, they take."

Zhao Feng smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. It was the smile of a man who had spent forty years learning exactly how the game was played.

"Get me a pick. And a lantern. I'm going digging."

"Now? We haven't even had breakfast."

"Now."

Zhang Hu stared at him like he had lost his mind. But he shrugged and fetched the equipment, handing it over with a shake of his head.

Zhao Feng took the pick and lantern and walked toward the tunnel. He did not look at Wang Chen. He did not look at the foreman. He walked past them both, a young laborer with a plan burning in his skull, and he entered the darkness of the mine.

The tunnel sloped downward, the air growing cooler and denser with every step. Zhao Feng moved by memory, feeling the walls, listening to the subtle shifts in sound that told him where the rock was solid and where it was hollow. He had walked these tunnels sixty years ago. He had dreamed of them for sixty years.

He reached the place. A dead end, where a previous excavation had been abandoned due to poor returns. The rock here was dark and unremarkable. Any ordinary miner would have walked right past it.

Zhao Feng raised his pick.

He struck the wall once. Twice. Three times. The rhythm of the blows came back to him like an old song. His muscles remembered what his mind had never forgotten.

On the seventh strike, the pick bit deeper. Something changed in the sound. A slight ringing quality, like striking a bell buried in stone.

Zhao Feng's heart leaped.

He struck again, harder. Chips of rock flew. And then—there. A glimmer in the lamplight. A flash of deep, crystalline blue.

Qi stones. High-grade. Pure enough to make a cultivator weep.

Zhao Feng stared at the vein for a long moment. The fortune of a lifetime, glittering in the darkness of a hole in the ground.

Then he turned, wiped the sweat from his brow, and walked back toward the entrance.

He had work to do. He had a deal to make. And this time, he would not let anyone take what was his.

title: Chapter 2
content:
CHAPTER 2: The Price of Memory

The first thing Zhao Feng noticed was the smell.

Soy sauce. Ginger. The greasy tang of a wok that hadn't been cleaned properly in years.

He opened his eyes to a ceiling so low he could touch it from where he lay. Wooden beams, black with age and smoke. A single oil lamp flickered on a rickety table, casting shadows that danced like hungry ghosts.

His body ached. Not the deep, bone-weary ache of a man who'd been beaten and left to die in an alley. This was the soreness of youth. Muscles that remembered how to recover. Joints that hadn't yet learned to predict rain.

Zhao Feng sat up slowly.

The cot creaked beneath him. A thin mattress stuffed with straw. A pillow that smelled of mildew. The room was barely four meters square—a kitchen, a bedroom, and a storage space all crammed into one.

*My shop.*

The memory surfaced like a corpse rising from murky water. He'd rented this place three days after arriving in Azure Cloud City. A failed merchant had abandoned it, leaving behind a few jars of cheap tea, a rusty scale, and a debt that still haunted the address.

Zhao Feng had been eighteen. Fresh from his father's death. A sack of silver coins that would last six months if he was careful. No connections. No reputation. Just a stubborn belief that he could build something from nothing.

*And I did.*

He closed his eyes. Forty years of memories pressed against his skull, demanding attention. The first shop. The second. The warehouse district. The trade routes he'd carved through the Eastern Wastes. The mansion with its nine courtyards and three hundred servants.

The sons he'd raised. The sons who'd smiled at his birthday feast and then smiled wider when they signed his death warrant.

Zhao Feng opened his eyes.

The oil lamp flickered. A cockroach scuttled across the floor.

He was eighteen years old. He had twelve silver coins hidden in a pouch beneath the loose floorboard by the stove. He had a shop that sold tea so bitter even the city's beggars refused it.

And he had forty years of knowledge that no one in this world knew he possessed.

*Let's begin.*

---

The morning market of Azure Cloud City started before dawn.

Zhao Feng walked through the narrow streets, letting his feet carry him while his mind worked. The cobblestones were wet with dew. A vegetable seller was already arranging cabbage heads in neat rows. A fishmonger cursed as a bucket of carp splashed his trousers.

It all looked exactly as he remembered.

But memory was a strange thing. He'd walked these streets a thousand times in his first life, but he'd walked them as a poor merchant, scrambling for every copper. Later, he'd ridden through them in a sedan chair carried by eight bearers, too important to let his feet touch the ground.

Now he saw what he'd missed the first time.

The way the qi stone merchant's shop had a crack in its foundation—small, but growing. The way the apothecary's sign hung slightly crooked, suggesting a man too distracted by other matters to care about appearances. The way the crowds parted around a particular corner, where a cultivator in blue robes stood with his arms crossed, watching the flow of traffic with predatory interest.

*Patrol captain. Third rank. Probably paid by the Cai family to watch for troublemakers.*

Zhao Feng kept his head down. He wasn't ready to be noticed. Not yet.

He stopped at a food stall and bought two steamed buns for one copper. The vendor was a young woman with flour on her cheeks and a baby tied to her back. She smiled when she handed him the buns.

"New face," she said. "Just arrived?"

"Three days ago." Zhao Feng bit into the bun. Pork and chives. Hot steam escaped from the hole. It tasted like the best thing he'd ever eaten, which was ridiculous, because he'd once dined on thousand-year ginseng soup and phoenix-tail fish imported from the Southern Islands.

But that was in his other life. In this life, he had one copper in his pocket and twelve silver under his floorboard, and every meal mattered.

"Where are you staying?" the woman asked.

"The old tea shop on Rat Alley."

Her face flickered with recognition. "Old Huang's place. Heard he ran off with a dancer from the Lotus Pavilion. Left behind a lot of angry creditors."

"I paid his debts. The shop is mine now."

That wasn't entirely true. He'd paid the rent for the remaining three months on the lease, but the debts were still floating around the city, waiting to latch onto someone foolish enough to claim them.

In his first life, those debts had nearly crushed him. He'd spent a year paying them off, working sixteen-hour days, sleeping on the shop floor, eating nothing but plain rice and pickled vegetables.

This time, he had a different plan.

"The shop needs work," Zhao Feng said. "But I have a feeling business will pick up soon."

The woman tilted her head. "You sound confident for someone selling old Huang's bad tea."

"I'm not selling tea anymore."

"What are you selling, then?"

Zhao Feng finished his bun and wiped his fingers on his trousers. "Information."

---

The Cai family's compound occupied the entire eastern quarter of Azure Cloud City.

Zhao Feng stood across the street from its main gate, watching servants hurry in and out with baskets of supplies. A carriage painted with the family crest—a golden crane rising from storm clouds—waited for the gates to open.

*Cai Yuan. Third young master. The one who controls the city's qi stone trade.*

In his first life, Zhao Feng had never dealt directly with the Cai family. They were too powerful, too entrenched. He'd built his fortune in the spaces they ignored—the small trades, the minor routes, the goods that weren't worth their attention.

But that was before he understood the game.

Qi stones were the foundation of the cultivation world. Every cultivator needed them. Every breakthrough required them. The demand never stopped, and the supply was always limited.

Whoever controlled the qi stones controlled the cultivators.

And whoever controlled the cultivators controlled the world.

Zhao Feng had learned this lesson the hard way. In his first life, he'd been a merchant who sold to cultivators. He'd made them richer, stronger, more powerful. And when his sons had turned against him, the cultivators he'd helped had done nothing.

*Why would they? I was useful, but I was never essential.*

This time, he would be essential.

He turned away from the Cai compound and walked toward the southern district, where the city's second-hand market sprawled across three streets. Here, merchants sold everything from broken weapons to counterfeit pills, from servant contracts to maps of forgotten ruins.

Zhao Feng navigated the chaos with practiced ease. He ignored the hawkers who grabbed at his sleeves, promising treasures beyond imagination. He stepped over a drunk who'd passed out in the middle of the street. He dodged a cart full of screaming chickens.

At the far end of the market, tucked between a fortune teller and a stall selling mysterious powders, was a shop that looked like it had given up on life.

The sign hung by one chain. The windows were so dirty they barely let in light. A man sat on a stool by the door, whittling a piece of wood into something that might have been a dog or might have been a deformed potato.

"Old Zhang," Zhao Feng said.

The man looked up. His eyes were milky with cataracts, but they still had a sharpness that spoke of a mind that hadn't yet surrendered to age.

"I don't know you," the man said.

"You will." Zhao Feng reached into his pocket and pulled out a single silver coin. He held it up, letting the morning light catch the surface. "I need a map. The old one. The one your grandfather drew before he died."

Old Zhang's whittling stopped. "That map isn't for sale."

"Everything is for sale. You just haven't found the right buyer."

"The right buyer would need to be very rich."

"I'm not rich." Zhao Feng placed the silver coin on the stool beside the old man. "But I know what the map leads to, and I know you've been trying to find someone willing to split the profits instead of just buying it outright."

Old Zhang stared at him for a long moment. Then he laughed—a dry, rasping sound that turned into a cough.

"You're a child," he said. "What do you know about anything?"

"I know your grandfather found a vein of high-grade qi stones in the Azure Mountains. I know he marked the location on a map and then died before he could go back. I know you've spent thirty years trying to get enough money together to mount an expedition, but every cultivator you've approached has either laughed you out of the room or tried to steal the map."

Old Zhang's face had gone pale. "How do you know that?"

"Because I've seen the consequences of your failure." Zhao Feng leaned closer. "Your daughter died of a wasting disease five years ago. Your son-in-law ran off with another woman. Your grandson is working as a porter in the eastern docks, breaking his back for three copper coins a day."

"Stop."

"I can change that. But I need the map."

Old Zhang's hands trembled. The piece of wood fell from his fingers and rolled across the ground. "Even if I gave you the map, what could you do with it? You're a boy. You don't have the strength to climb the Azure Mountains, let alone fight the beasts that guard the stones."

"I don't need to fight them." Zhao Feng picked up the silver coin and pressed it into Old Zhang's palm. "I just need to know where they are. The rest is a matter of business."

Old Zhang looked at the coin. Then at Zhao Feng. Then back at the coin.

"Wait here," he said finally.

He disappeared into the shop. A minute passed. Two. Zhao Feng didn't move. He could hear the old man shuffling around inside, opening boxes, moving furniture.

When Old Zhang emerged, he was holding a rolled-up piece of leather, yellow with age.

"Take it." He thrust the map into Zhao Feng's hands. "Take it and go. I don't want to see you again."

Zhao Feng unrolled the map. The ink was faded, the lines barely visible, but he could still make out the contours of the mountains, the markings that indicated caves and streams and—

His breath caught.

There. In the corner, almost invisible, a small character written in a script so old that most scholars would have mistaken it for a brush smudge.

*To open the gate, first pay the toll.*

Zhao Feng smiled.

"Thank you, Old Zhang. You've just changed the future."

He turned to leave.

"Boy," Old Zhang called after him. "What's your name?"

Zhao Feng paused. He looked back over his shoulder at the broken-down shop, the broken-down man, the broken-down life that was about to be swept away by the tide of history.

"My name is Zhao Feng. Remember it. In ten years, everyone in this city will."

He walked away, the map pressed against his chest, his mind already calculating the next move.

He needed capital. He needed connections. He needed to move before the Cai family caught wind of what he was doing.

But more than anything, he needed to find the gate.

And pay the toll.

He was three streets away from the market when a hand grabbed his shoulder.

"Zhao Feng. I've been looking for you."

The voice was familiar. Too familiar. Zhao Feng turned slowly.

The man standing behind him was young—barely twenty—with a cruel mouth and eyes that held no warmth.

title: The Stone That Smelled of Lies
content:
The morning air carried the scent of burning joss paper and frying dough.

Zhao Feng stood at the entrance of the Immortal’s Rest teahouse, watching the street come alive. Merchants pulled down wooden shutters. Apprentices swept doorsteps with bamboo brooms. A woman with a baby tied to her back haggled over the price of freshwater prawns.

All of it felt new. All of it felt like a stage he'd already performed on.

Forty years. Forty years of climbing, falling, bleeding, and dying. And now he was here, young and empty-handed, watching his past-life competitors as they yawned and rubbed sleep from their eyes.

They didn't know yet. None of them knew.

He adjusted his cheap cotton robe—the only one he owned—and stepped inside.

The teahouse was two stories of dark wood and paper lanterns. The ground floor held twelve tables, most empty at this hour. A single old man sat in the corner, nursing a cup of osmanthus tea, his eyes half-closed like a cat pretending to sleep.

Zhao Feng ignored him. He knew the old man was a watcher. In Cloud City, every teahouse had one—someone paid to note who came, who left, who stayed too long.

He took a table near the window. Good sight lines. Two exits. No one behind him.

A serving girl approached, her face blank with the practiced neutrality of someone who'd learned not to care about customers. "Tea, young master?"

"Boiled water. No leaves."

She blinked. "Young master?"

"Water. Clean cup. Boiled three times."

She left without another word. Good. She'd remember him. That was the point.

Zhao Feng placed both hands flat on the table and let his mind drift back through the fog of memory.

The qi stone market. He'd cornered it at age forty-seven in his past life, after a decade of careful positioning. The trick wasn't in the stones themselves—they were just raw cultivation fuel, useful to everyone from Outer Disciple to Nascent Soul elder. The trick was in the timing.

Qi stones fluctuated in value based on season, sect conflicts, and breakthrough cycles. When the Azure Lotus Sect launched their annual recruitment drive, demand spiked forty percent. When the Blood Sun Palace went to war with the Southern Wilds, supply routes collapsed and prices tripled.

He'd made his first fortune buying low during peacetime and selling high during chaos.

But that was then. He had no capital now. No warehouse. No network of couriers and bribed officials.

What he had was a single advantage: he knew exactly when the next price spike would hit.

In three months, the Black Turtle Sect would discover a sealed ancient tomb beneath the Wandering Dragon Mountains. The discovery would trigger a mad rush for cultivation resources. Every sect in the eastern provinces would send teams. And every team would need qi stones.

The price would double in a week.

Then double again.

He needed to be ready.

The serving girl returned with a ceramic pot and a cup. She poured the water slowly, deliberately, letting him see the steam rise in clean white curls.

"Three times boiled," she said. "As young master requested."

He nodded, not thanking her. Thanks were currency he couldn't afford to waste on servants.

She lingered. "Young master looks familiar."

"No," he said. "I don't."

She left. Smart girl. She'd recognize him later, when his face appeared in the gossip sheets. She'd remember that he drank only water. She'd tell her friends. And those friends would tell their masters.

By the time he was ready to move, half of Cloud City would know him as the strange young man who boiled his water three times.

That was good. Mystery was a merchant's best friend.

He sipped. The water was clean, hot, tasteless. Perfect.

The front door banged open.

Three men entered, their robes the deep blue of the Vermillion Bird Trading Company. The leader was broad-shouldered, with a scar that split his left eyebrow in two. He scanned the room, let his gaze pass over Zhao Feng, and settled on the old man in the corner.

"Old Xu," the scarred man said. "You're late."

The old man didn't open his eyes. "The tea wasn't ready."

"The tea's always ready. You're always late. My master doesn't like waiting."

"Your master can wait until the tea is steeped."

The scarred man's jaw tightened. His two companions shifted their weight, hands moving toward their belts.

Zhao Feng watched. He remembered this. The Vermillion Bird Company was one of the minor trading houses—strong enough to bully street vendors, weak enough to be crushed by a real player. In his past life, he'd absorbed them after the qi stone crash. Their assets had been mediocre. Their personnel, worse.

But they had one thing he needed: access to the outer market.

The scarred man stepped closer to Old Xu. "I'll say this once. The shipment arrives at dusk. My master wants the manifests ready by noon. If they're not—"

"Then your master will have to wait until they are."

The scarred man's hand shot out, grabbing Old Xu by the collar. The old man's eyes flew open, and his tea cup clattered to the floor.

"Listen, you ancient—"

"Release him."

The words came out flat. Unbothered. Zhao Feng didn't raise his voice. Didn't stand. He simply spoke, and the room went still.

The scarred man turned. "Who the hell are you?"

"No one."

"Then shut your mouth before I shut it for you."

Zhao Feng set down his cup. "The Vermillion Bird Company controls the northern route into Cloud City. You move grain, iron, and low-grade qi stones. Your master's name is Chen Bao. He started as a mule driver fifteen years ago and bought his way into the trading guild through marriage."

The scarred man's face went pale.

"You have three warehouses," Zhao Feng continued. "One in the eastern district, two outside the city walls. The eastern warehouse is half-empty because your master's wife's brother embezzled three hundred spirit stones last spring, and your master is too afraid of his wife to confront him."

"How do you—"

"I know because I make it my business to know." Zhao Feng picked up his cup again. "Now release Old Xu, pay for his broken cup, and tell your master that if he wants his manifests by noon, he should send someone who knows how to ask politely."

The scarred man's hand trembled. For a moment, Zhao Feng saw the calculation happening behind those eyes—the weighing of options, the fear of the unknown, the instinct to strike.

Then the man let go.

Old Xu dropped back into his chair, gasping.

The scarred man reached into his pouch, pulled out three copper coins, and tossed them onto the table. "For the cup." He turned and walked out, his men scrambling behind him.

The door swung shut.

Silence.

Old Xu stared at Zhao Feng with eyes that had suddenly gone sharp. "Who are you?"

"Someone who needs information."

"About what?"

"The qi stone market. Specifically, who controls the low-grade supply coming out of the Jade Springs Mine."

Old Xu's face flickered. "That's not public knowledge."

"Nothing is public knowledge until someone pays for it."

A long pause. Then Old Xu laughed, a dry rasping sound like paper being crumpled. "You're bold, boy. Bold and stupid. The Jade Springs Mine is run by the Zhang family. They've held it for four generations. You can't touch them."

"I'm not going to touch them."

"Then what?"

Zhao Feng stood. He reached into his sleeve and pulled out a single spirit stone—the last one he'd earned from selling those storage pouches. He placed it on the table between them.

"This is for another cup of tea. And a conversation about the Zhang family's delivery schedules."

Old Xu looked at the stone. Then at Zhao Feng. Then back at the stone.

"Young master," he said slowly, "you're buying trouble."

"I'm not buying trouble." Zhao Feng smiled, and it felt like a knife being drawn. "I'm buying opportunity."

Old Xu reached for the stone.

And the teahouse door slammed open again.

This time, the man who entered wore white robes embroidered with silver clouds. His face was smooth, handsome, and cold as a winter grave. Behind him stood four attendants in matching uniforms, their qi pressure filling the room like water flooding a ship.

The man's eyes found Zhao Feng immediately.

"So," he said, his voice soft and amused. "The gutter rat has crawled out of the drain."

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

He knew that face.

In his past life, this man had been his partner. His trusted right hand. The one who'd smiled while signing the documents that stripped Zhao Feng of everything.

Wei Shan.

Alive. Young. And looking at him like he was already dead.

"I don't know you," Zhao Feng said.

Wei Shan laughed. "No. You don't. But I know you, Zhao Feng. I've been watching you for three days. Wondering when you'd show your face in this city."

"How do you know my name?"

"I know a lot of things." Wei Shan stepped closer, his smile never wavering. "I know you sold two storage pouches at the market yesterday. I know you're staying at the Drunken Crane inn. I know you have exactly seventeen spirit stones and forty-two copper coins to your name."

Zhao Feng kept his face still.

"I also know," Wei Shan continued, leaning in until his breath ghosted across Zhao Feng's ear, "that you shouldn't exist."

"What?"

"You died, Zhao Feng. I watched you die. I held your hand while the poison took you. I closed your eyes myself."

The words hit like a physical blow.

Wei Shan's smile widened. "So tell me, ghost—who sent you back? And more importantly, how much do you remember?"

title: The First Card Falls
content:
The morning sun painted Huangling Town in shades of amber and dust. Zhao Feng stood at the mouth of an alley, watching the Xuan family's medicine shop open for business. Old man Xuan shuffled to the door, his apprentice trailing behind with buckets of water for the morning cleansing.

Forty years ago, Zhao Feng had walked past this shop a hundred times without a second glance. Now he saw everything.

The faded paint on the signboard. The crack in the wooden counter. The way Xuan's hands trembled when he counted coins.

*Desperation*, Zhao Feng thought. *The smell of a business drowning slowly.*

He stepped out of the alley.

"You're open early, Master Xuan."

The old man looked up, squinting. Recognition flickered, then confusion. The Zhao family's disgraced son, dressed in patched robes, standing before his shop like he owned it.

"Zhao Feng." Xuan's voice carried the careful neutrality of a merchant who didn't want trouble. "I heard about your father's decision. Unfortunate."

"Fortunate, actually." Zhao Feng smiled. "It gave me freedom."

Xuan's apprentice snorted. "Freedom to starve in the streets."

Zhao Feng ignored him. He reached into his sleeve and pulled out a sheet of paper, yellowed and covered in dense characters. He placed it on the counter.

"I have a proposal."

Xuan glanced at the paper. His eyebrows rose slightly. "This is... a supply contract."

"For the next twelve months. I'll provide your shop with three times your current stock of common healing herbs, at seventy percent of your current wholesale price. You sell them at your standard rate. Keep the difference."

Silence.

The apprentice stopped scrubbing. Xuan's hands had stopped trembling.

"Three times the stock," Xuan repeated slowly. "At seventy percent cost. How?"

"I have connections you don't."

"Connections." Xuan's eyes narrowed. "You're eighteen years old, Zhao Feng. Your family just threw you out. What connections could you possibly have?"

Zhao Feng leaned forward. "The same connections that tell me your shop has been losing money for eight months. That your primary supplier, the Liu family, raised their prices after your daughter refused their son's marriage proposal. That you've been bleeding coin trying to keep this place open for her sake."

Xuan's face went pale.

The apprentice dropped his bucket.

"How..." Xuan's voice cracked. "How could you know that?"

*Because I watched your daughter die in my past life*, Zhao Feng thought. *Because the Liu family crushed you, and you hanged yourself in the back room three months from now. Because I remember everything.*

"I know many things," Zhao Feng said instead. "I know you have seventeen silver taels remaining in your emergency fund. I know you've been skipping meals to afford your daughter's medicine. I know you think you have no options left."

He tapped the contract.

"But you do. Sign this, and I'll have the first delivery at your door by sunset tomorrow. One month of payment upfront. If I fail to deliver, you owe me nothing."

Xuan stared at the paper. His fingers twitched toward it, then pulled back.

"Why?" he whispered. "Why help a stranger?"

"Because you're not a stranger anymore." Zhao Feng extended his hand. "You're my first business partner."

---

The afternoon passed in a blur of negotiations.

Zhao Feng walked through every street of Huangling Town, his feet carrying him to shops he'd memorized in another life. The ironmonger whose son died in a mining accident. The herbalist who'd been cheated by his brother. The innkeeper whose wife had run away with a traveling merchant.

Each had a wound. Each had a need.

And Zhao Feng offered solutions.

By evening, he had signed contracts with six businesses. Not for cash—he had barely three silver taels to his name—but for future shares. Promises of profit. A percentage of revenue in exchange for stock that would flow through his hands.

His past life had taught him one immutable truth: *money moved through trust, not through gold.*

Trust was a currency he could mint from nothing.

---

Dusk fell. Zhao Feng sat in his rented room above the noodle shop, counting the contracts spread across his thin mattress.

*Six shops. Six supply lines. A web that could grow.*

He reached for his brush to calculate the numbers, but stopped.

A knock at the door.

Three beats. Pause. Two more.

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold.

That rhythm. He knew that rhythm.

He rose slowly, sliding the contracts beneath his pillow. His hand found the knife he'd hidden under the loose floorboard.

"Who is it?"

"Open the door, brother."

The voice was smooth. Young. Familiar in a way that made Zhao Feng's stomach turn.

He opened the door.

Zhao Liang stood in the hallway, oil lamp in hand, his shadow stretching long and thin across the cracked wooden floor. He was seventeen, one year younger than Zhao Feng. Handsome in that soft way their mother had favored, with eyes that always seemed to be calculating.

In the past life, Zhao Liang had been the first to betray him.

"Father sent me," Zhao Liang said, smiling. "He wanted me to check on his disappointing son. Make sure you haven't disgraced the family name any further."

Zhao Feng didn't move. "Tell Father I'm thriving."

"Thriving?" Zhao Liang laughed. "You're renting a room above a noodle shop. I can smell the grease from here."

"Smell doesn't bother me. Neither does poverty."

"Bold words for a man with nothing."

Zhao Feng met his brother's eyes. In his past life, he'd missed the cruelty hiding behind that smile. Missed the hunger. The jealousy. The quiet satisfaction when Zhao Liang had finally driven a knife into his back.

*Not this time.*

"I have something better than money," Zhao Feng said. "I have time."

Zhao Liang's smile flickered. "Time for what?"

"To rebuild. To remember. To make sure that when I rise again, everyone who laughed at me will be the ones begging."

Silence.

The oil lamp flickered.

"You've changed," Zhao Liang said slowly, studying him with new eyes. "Exile suits you."

"It's not exile. It's opportunity."

Zhao Liang's lips curled. "Father was right. You've lost your mind."

"Perhaps." Zhao Feng stepped back, preparing to close the door. "But madmen are the only ones who change the world."

He shut the door. Locked it. Leaned against the wood, heart pounding.

*The first card has fallen*, he thought. *Now the game begins.*

---

He worked through the night.

By lamplight, Zhao Feng mapped out his plan on a scrap of parchment. The names of every merchant in Huangling Town. Their connections. Their debts. Their weaknesses.

Six contracts meant six supply lines. Six supply lines meant leverage. Leverage meant power.

But power required capital.

He had three silver taels. Enough to buy a cart and a mule. Enough to travel to the outer villages where farmers sold their herbs for pennies, then turn around and sell them in town for silver.

A simple plan. A merchant's plan.

But in this world, merchants were kings.

He dipped his brush, wrote a single line at the bottom of the page:

*The Qi Stone Exchange opens in two months. I will be there.*

Outside, a dog barked. Wind rattled the windows.

Zhao Feng looked up.

Shadows moved in the street below. Two of them. Maybe three.

*Watching.*

He extinguished the lamp. Moved to the window, pressing himself against the wall, peering through a crack in the shutters.

The figures had stopped. One pointed at his window.

*Zhao Liang*, Zhao Feng thought. *You always did move fast.*

But he'd expected this. The Zhao family wouldn't let him escape so easily. They'd want to ensure he stayed broken. They'd send watchers to track his movements, report his failures, wait for him to fall.

*Let them watch.*

He smiled in the darkness.

*Let them report how their disgraced son rises from the gutter.*

*Let them see the empire I build.*

*And when the time comes, let them realize too late that they helped me build it.*

He settled onto his mattress, the contracts tucked beneath his pillow, the knife within reach.

Tomorrow, he would buy a cart.

Tomorrow, he would visit the villages.

Tomorrow, the game continued.

But tonight, he slept with the smile of a man who had already won.

---

Dawn came gray and cold.

Zhao Feng woke to a pounding on his door.

He sat up, knife already in hand.

"Zhao Feng!" A woman's voice. Desperate. "Zhao Feng, please!"

He recognized it. The innkeeper's wife from two streets over. The one whose husband had beaten her.

He opened the door.

She stood there, face tear-streaked, clutching a crumpled piece of paper.

"They took him," she sobbed. "They took my husband."

Zhao Feng's stomach dropped.

"Who?"

"The Liu family." She thrust the paper into his hands. "They said he owes them. Said if you want him back, you need to come alone. Before noon. To the abandoned warehouse by the river."

Zhao Feng read the message. His hands didn't tremble.

*The Liu family*, he thought. *Already moving against me.*

He looked at the woman. At her tear-streaked face. At the fear in her eyes.

This was a trap. A clear, obvious trap.

But if he didn't go, he'd lose every contract he'd signed. Every merchant who'd trusted him would see him as a coward. His empire would die before it was born.

He folded the paper, tucked it into his sleeve.

"Wait here," he said.

He turned, grabbed his knife, and walked out the door.

*The first card has fallen*, he thought as he stepped into the morning light. *Now I see what the Liu family holds.*

title: The Wolf Shows His Teeth
content:
The morning air carried the scent of rain and burning incense. Zhao Feng stood at the entrance of the Cloud Terrace market, watching the merchants set up their stalls with the practiced eye of a man who had once owned half the goods they sold.

Three weeks since his rebirth. Three weeks of buying low-grade qi stones at midnight auctions, of memorizing the faces of every minor cultivator who passed through the district, of building a foundation so fragile that one wrong step would shatter everything.

He adjusted the sleeve of his cheap cotton robe. The fabric chafed against his wrists, a constant reminder of how far he'd fallen.

And how far he would climb.

"Young Master Zhao."

The voice came from behind him, smooth as polished jade. Zhao Feng didn't turn. He'd heard those footsteps approach twenty paces away, measured and deliberate. A man who wanted to be noticed.

"Brother Wu," Zhao Feng said, turning slowly. "I was wondering when you'd find me."

Wu Chen stood three steps away, his silk robes embroidered with silver thread that caught the morning light. Behind him, two guards waited with their hands tucked into their sleeves. The kind of men who kept short blades hidden there.

"I've been watching your progress," Wu Chen said, a smile playing at the corners of his mouth. "Impressive work with the spirit grass contracts. You cornered the market on low-grade restoration herbs in what, two weeks?"

"Three."

"Even better." Wu Chen stepped closer, his eyes scanning the market stalls. "But you're playing a dangerous game, young brother. The families who control these lanes don't take kindly to outsiders."

Zhao Feng kept his face neutral. In his past life, he'd crushed men like Wu Chen under his heel without a second thought. Minor nobles who thought their bloodline entitled them to everything they desired. But that was sixty years of experience talking. Right now, he was just an eighteen-year-old nobody with a few thousand spirit coins to his name.

"I'm just a merchant trying to make a living," Zhao Feng said.

Wu Chen laughed. It was a pleasant sound, the kind that made people trust him. Zhao Feng had made that sound himself, many times, right before he destroyed his competitors.

"Let's stop pretending," Wu Chen said, dropping his voice. "I know you're the one who bought the Liu family's storage debt. I know you've been buying qi stones through three different middlemen to hide your tracks. And I know you're sitting on a stockpile that could destabilize this entire district."

The words landed like stones in still water. Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. He'd been careful. Painfully, obsessively careful. He'd used false names, changed his appearance at every transaction, never visited the same broker twice.

But Wu Chen had still found him.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Zhao Feng said.

"Of course you don't." Wu Chen reached into his sleeve and pulled out a folded piece of paper. "But your father might be interested to know that his useless third son has been making moves behind his back. Or the Lin family might want to know who's been buying up all the fire-attributed qi stones before their annual tribulation preparation."

Zhao Feng's jaw tightened. The Lins were one of the most powerful cultivation families in the province. If they thought someone was manipulating their resource supply, they wouldn't ask questions. They'd just crush whoever was responsible.

"What do you want?"

Wu Chen's smile widened. "Now we're talking. I want in."

"In?"

"Your operation. Whatever you're planning, I want a piece." Wu Chen folded his arms. "I've been stuck managing this backwater market for three years while my brothers climb the ranks in the capital. I need resources. Cultivation resources. And you need protection."

Zhao Feng studied the young noble's face. Wu Chen was twenty-five, maybe twenty-six. Strong cultivation base, probably peak Qi Condensation. Ambitious eyes that couldn't quite hide their hunger. In his past life, Zhao Feng had met a thousand men like him. Useful tools, if handled carefully.

But tools could break. And broken tools could cut both ways.

"Why should I trust you?" Zhao Feng asked.

"Because I could have gone to your father. Or the Lins. Instead, I came to you." Wu Chen spread his hands. "I'm offering an alliance, young brother. My family's name and protection in exchange for a share of your profits. Thirty percent."

"Ten."

"Twenty."

"Fifteen, and you provide security for my warehouse."

Wu Chen's eyes narrowed. For a moment, Zhao Feng saw the predator beneath the pleasant mask. Then the smile returned.

"Fifteen, and first refusal on any future deals."

"Done."

They shook hands. Wu Chen's grip was firm, his palm calloused from sword practice. A man who trained as well as schemed. Dangerous.

"I'll send my man Xiao Liu to your warehouse tonight," Wu Chen said. "He knows the patrol routes and can keep the city guards away from your shipments."

"I'll be there."

Wu Chen turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing, young brother. I had you investigated after I spotted your first transaction. You're smarter than you let on. Smarter than your family gives you credit for." His voice dropped lower. "But there's something strange about you. The way you move, the way you talk. Like you've been doing this for decades, not weeks."

Zhao Feng's heart hammered against his ribs. He kept his face still, his breathing even.

"Maybe I'm just a fast learner."

Wu Chen studied him for a long moment, then nodded slowly. "Maybe you are. I'll see you tonight."

He walked away, his guards falling into step behind him. Zhao Feng watched until they disappeared around a corner, then let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding.

That was too close.

He'd been careless. In his past life, he'd had an army of accountants and spies to cover his tracks. Now he was alone, relying on tricks that were forty years out of date. The cultivation world had changed. The markets had changed. And he'd been too arrogant to realize it.

He needed help.

Not Wu Chen's kind of help, with its hidden costs and sharp edges. Real help. Loyal help.

His mind drifted to a memory. A boy with dirt on his face and fire in his eyes, reaching for something he couldn't have. A boy who would grow into a man who moved mountains.

In his past life, that boy had become Zhao Feng's most trusted enforcer. The one who stood by him when his sons turned away. The one who died protecting Zhao Feng's escape route, buying him precious minutes with his own blood.

But that was in the future. Right now, that boy was just a thirteen-year-old urchin scraping by in the lower districts.

Right now, he still had a chance to save him.

Zhao Feng turned from the market and walked toward the slums.

---

The lower districts smelled like rot and desperation.

Zhao Feng picked his way through the narrow alleys, stepping over piles of refuse and sleeping beggars. The buildings leaned against each other like drunks, their wooden walls stained with decades of grime. Somewhere nearby, a child was crying.

He found the boy exactly where he remembered.

In a corner of the abandoned temple, curled against a cracked stone pillar. His clothes were rags. His face was gaunt. And his eyes—those dark, defiant eyes—watched Zhao Feng approach with the wariness of a cornered animal.

"Go away," the boy said. His voice cracked. "I got nothing worth stealing."

"I'm not here to steal from you, Xiao Chen."

The boy's eyes widened at the sound of his name. "How do you know my name?"

Zhao Feng crouched down, bringing himself to the boy's eye level. This close, he could see the scars on Xiao Chen's arms, the malnutrition in his bones, the desperation that had already begun to harden into something colder.

"I know a lot of things about you," Zhao Feng said. "I know you've been surviving on your own for three years since your parents died. I know you pick pockets in the eastern market, and you've never been caught. I know you dream of becoming a cultivator."

Xiao Chen's hands curled into fists. "What do you want?"

"I want to offer you a deal." Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small pouch. It clinked when he set it down. "Fifty spirit coins. Enough to rent a room for a year and eat every day."

Xiao Chen's eyes locked onto the pouch. His tongue wet his lips. "What's the catch?"

"There's always a catch." Zhao Feng smiled. "I need someone I can trust. Someone who knows the streets, who can move without being seen, who can gather information without raising suspicion. You work for me for one year, and I'll not only pay you—I'll teach you how to read, how to calculate, how to survive in a world that wants to crush you."

"And if I say no?"

"Then you keep living like this, and eventually you die in this alley." Zhao Feng stood up. "I'm not going to force you, Xiao Chen. The choice is yours."

The boy stared at the pouch for a long time. His hands trembled slightly.

"Why me?" he finally asked. "There's a hundred other street kids you could pick."

Because I watched you die for me in another life. Because you were the only one who stayed.

"Because I see something in you," Zhao Feng said instead. "A spark that refuses to go out. That's rare. That's valuable."

Xiao Chen's jaw worked. Then, slowly, he reached out and took the pouch.

"When do I start?"

Zhao Feng felt something loosen in his chest. "Tonight. There's a man named Wu Chen coming to my warehouse. I need you to watch him. Learn his habits. Find out what he really wants."

"And if he tries to hurt you?"

"Then you come find me, and we decide what to do next."

The boy nodded, clutching the pouch to his chest. Zhao Feng turned and walked away, his footsteps echoing through the empty temple.

Behind him, Xiao Chen watched him go, his dark eyes unreadable.

---

The warehouse sat at the edge of the merchant district, sandwiched between a tannery and a closed-down teahouse. Zhao Feng had bought it for a fraction of its value, using money he'd scraped together from his first deals. The roof leaked. The walls had holes. But it had a locked door and space to store his growing collection of qi stones.

He arrived at dusk, carrying a lantern and a bag of supplies. The lock was intact. The seals he'd placed on the door hadn't been disturbed. Good.

Inside, he lit the lantern and surveyed his domain.

Crates of low-grade qi stones lined the walls. Bundles of spirit grass sat in neat stacks. A scale, a mortar and pestle, and a ledger book sat on a table in the center of the room. Everything he needed to begin his empire.

But it wasn't enough. Not yet.

He opened the ledger and began calculating. His current stockpile was worth roughly ten thousand spirit coins. Enough to buy a decent cultivation technique, maybe a low-grade spirit sword. But that wasn't the goal.

The goal was the Immortal Ascension Tournament, six months away.

In his past life, that tournament had changed everything. The winner had been a nobody from the outer disciples, a young woman who'd shocked everyone with her mastery of lightning techniques. The major families had scrambled to recruit her, offering fortunes for her allegiance.

But Zhao Feng knew something they didn't.

He knew where she'd gotten those techniques. He knew the hidden

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Shadow in the Night

The lantern flickered as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger's columns. Ten thousand spirit coins. A respectable start, but pitiful compared to what he'd need. The Immortal Ascension Tournament wasn't just about knowing the winner—it was about positioning himself to exploit that knowledge before anyone else caught on.

He dipped his brush in ink and wrote a single name: *Liu Mei.*

In his past life, she'd been a sweeper. A nobody. The outer disciples had laughed at her when she'd registered for the tournament. Then she'd stepped onto that platform and summoned lightning from a clear blue sky, reducing her first opponent to a smoking heap in three seconds flat.

The major families had fought over her like starving dogs over a bone. The Zhao family—*his* family, in that life—had offered her a marriage alliance with his third son, the useless one who'd later gamble away half their fortune. She'd refused. Joined the Azure Cloud Sect instead. Become an elder within a decade.

But Zhao Feng knew the secret she'd never told anyone. He knew where she'd found those lightning techniques. A tomb. An ancient cultivator's tomb, hidden beneath the Thousand Snake Mountains, accessible only during the Blood Moon Festival, three months before the tournament.

He needed to get there first.

A knock at the warehouse door pulled him from his thoughts. Three quick raps, a pause, then two more. The signal he'd arranged with Xiao Chen.

"Come in."

The door creaked open. Xiao Chen slipped through, his thin frame barely visible in the dim light. His eyes were wide, his breathing rapid.

"There's trouble," the boy said. "That man, Wu Chen. He's not alone."

Zhao Feng set down his brush. "Explain."

"I followed him like you said. He went to the Golden Lotus restaurant, met with two other men. They talked for an hour. I couldn't hear everything, but I heard your name. And I heard them mention the word 'warehouse.'"

A cold feeling settled in Zhao Feng's stomach. He'd expected Wu Chen to be suspicious. He hadn't expected him to act so quickly.

"Describe the men."

"One was old. Maybe fifty. Wore a green robe with gold trim. Had a scar across his left eye." Xiao Chen's voice dropped. "The other one was younger. Muscular. Carried a sword at his hip. He kept touching the hilt while they talked."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. The green robe with gold trim—that was the uniform of the Xu family's stewards. The Xu family. Of course. In his past life, they'd controlled the southern qi stone trade, crushing anyone who dared compete. They must have heard about his recent purchases, connected the dots, and sent Wu Chen to investigate.

"How soon until they move?"

"I don't know. But Wu Chen left the restaurant looking angry. He walked straight to the market, bought rope and a burlap sack."

Rope. A burlap sack. They weren't planning to rob him. They were planning to *take* him.

Zhao Feng looked around the warehouse. His stockpile. His future. Everything he'd built in three months. If the Xu family got their hands on him, they'd squeeze him dry, then dump his body in the river.

He had two choices: run, or fight.

Running meant losing everything. His capital. His contacts. The momentum he'd built. He'd be back to nothing, starting over in some other city, always looking over his shoulder.

Fighting meant risk. He had no cultivation base to speak of—barely at the first level of Qi Condensation. Against a trained steward and a sword-wielding thug, he'd last maybe thirty seconds.

But he had something they didn't.

He had knowledge.

"Xiao Chen," he said, his voice calm despite the pounding in his chest. "I need you to do something dangerous."

The boy straightened. "I told you. I'm not afraid."

"Good. Go to the apothecary on Silver Street. The one with the red sign. Tell the owner you need a packet of Sleeping Dream herb. Tell him Zhao Feng sent you. He'll give you credit."

Xiao Chen's brow furrowed. "Sleeping Dream? That's poison."

"It's a sedative. In the right dosage." Zhao Feng pulled a silver coin from his pouch and pressed it into the boy's hand. "If anyone asks, you're buying it for your sick mother. Can you do that?"

"Yes." The boy's voice was steady.

"Good. Bring it back here. Then I need you to deliver a message."

---

The hours crawled by.

Zhao Feng worked by lantern light, rearranging crates, setting up the warehouse for what he had in mind. He moved the scale to the back corner. Placed the spirit grass bundles near the door. Left a clear path from the entrance to his worktable.

When Xiao Chen returned with the herb, Zhao Feng ground it into a fine powder and mixed it with tea leaves. He set the pot to steep, the bitter aroma filling the warehouse.

"Now the message," Zhao Feng said, pressing a folded piece of paper into Xiao Chen's hand. "Take this to the Night Jade Pavilion. Ask for a woman named Song Lian. Give her this note and nothing else. Do you understand?"

"The Night Jade Pavilion?" Xiao Chen's eyes went wide. "That's—"

"I know what it is. Go. Don't let anyone see you."

The boy hesitated only a second, then vanished into the darkness.

Zhao Feng turned back to his preparations. The Night Jade Pavilion was a brothel, yes. But it was also the information hub of the merchant district, and Song Lian was its queen. In his past life, she'd been his most valuable ally—a woman who knew every secret, every transaction, every betrayal before it happened.

He'd saved her life once, years from now. A poisoning attempt by a rival madam. He'd identified the poison, found the antidote, and earned her eternal gratitude.

But that was in the future. In this life, she didn't know him from any other customer. The note he'd sent was a gamble—a reference to a debt she didn't know she owed, a promise he couldn't yet fulfill.

If she ignored it, he was on his own.

---

Midnight came with a knock at the door.

Not Xiao Chen's signal. Three heavy thumps, solid and demanding.

Zhao Feng took a breath. Checked his preparations one last time. Then he crossed to the door and pulled it open.

Wu Chen stood in the doorway, a lantern in one hand, a polite smile on his face. Behind him, two shadows loomed—the old man with the scar and the young thug with the sword.

"Brother Zhao," Wu Chen said, his voice dripping with false warmth. "I apologize for the late hour. I hope I'm not disturbing you."

"Not at all." Zhao Feng stepped aside. "Please, come in. I was just about to have some tea."

Wu Chen's eyes flickered to the steaming pot on the table. "Tea? At this hour?"

"It's a special blend. Helps me think." Zhao Feng smiled. "You should try some."

The three men entered. The scarred steward surveyed the warehouse with predatory interest, noting every crate, every bundle. The thug's hand never left his sword.

"My associates and I have been discussing your recent success," Wu Chen said, settling onto a crate. "Impressive growth for someone so young. The Xu family takes notice of such things."

"I'm honored." Zhao Feng poured four cups of tea. The steam carried the Sleeping Dream's scent, masked by the jasmine undertones. "But I'm afraid I don't understand. What does the Xu family want with a small-time trader?"

Wu Chen's smile didn't reach his eyes. "The Xu family believes in supporting promising talent. We'd like to offer you a position. A generous salary, protection, access to our distribution network."

"And in exchange?"

"Control of your inventory. A partnership agreement. Seventy-thirty in our favor."

Seventy-thirty. They were offering him slave terms. And from the way the thug was flexing his fingers, they weren't giving him a real choice.

"That's a generous offer." Zhao Feng lifted his cup. "Let me think about it over tea."

He raised the cup to his lips. Wu Chen watched him, waiting. The scarred steward's hand drifted toward his sleeve, where a dagger likely hid.

Zhao Feng drank.

The tea was hot, bitter, laced with enough Sleeping Dream to knock out a horse. He swallowed, felt the warmth spread through his chest, and set the cup down.

"Well?" Wu Chen said.

"It's good tea." Zhao Feng smiled. "But I have to decline your offer."

The silence stretched.

Wu Chen's smile vanished. "That's unfortunate."

"I don't think so." Zhao Feng's voice remained calm. "You see, I know what happens to people who sign agreements with the Xu family. They disappear. Their stock gets seized. Their families never hear from them again."

The scarred steward moved. His dagger was out in a flash, pressed against Zhao Feng's throat.

"Smart boy," the steward rasped. "Too smart for your own good."

Zhao Feng didn't flinch. He'd been threatened by better men than this. He'd been betrayed by his own sons. A knife at his throat was nothing.

"You should check your own pulse," he said quietly.

The steward's eyes narrowed. "What?"

"Your wrist. The vein there. Is it throbbing?"

The man's gaze dropped involuntarily. His brow furrowed. Then his eyes went wide.

"You—"

He collapsed. The dagger clattered to the floor. The thug lunged forward, hand going for his sword, but his fingers fumbled, his knees buckled, and he hit the ground beside his companion.

Wu Chen stared. His face went pale. "What did you do?"

"I told you." Zhao Feng stood up, stepping over the unconscious bodies. "It's a special blend."

"You drank it too!"

"No. I let it touch my lips, but I didn't swallow." Zhao Feng tapped his throat. "Practice. Years of practice."

Wu Chen scrambled backward, knocking over a crate. Spirit stones scattered across the floor. "You'll regret this. The Xu family will—"

"The Xu family will find two of their men dead in a warehouse, poisoned by tea they drank willingly." Zhao Feng picked up the fallen dagger, testing its weight. "And they'll have no proof of who did it. No witnesses. No evidence. Just a dead end."

"You can't—"

"I can." Zhao Feng's voice hardened. "And I will. Because I'm not the same scared trader you thought you were dealing with. I'm the man who's going to tear down your family's empire, brick by brick, starting tonight."

Wu Chen's hand darted to his waist. A hidden pouch. He threw it to the ground—a smoke bomb, black clouds billowing outward.

Zhao Feng coughed, waving the smoke away. When it cleared, Wu Chen was gone. The back door swung open, moonlight spilling through.

He let him go.

Let him run back to the Xu family with tales of a madman who poisoned tea and spoke of empire. Let them wonder. Let them fear.

Zhao Feng looked down at the two unconscious men. Then at his stockpile, his ledger, his future.

The game had begun.

He was still wiping the blood from his hands when Xiao Chen appeared in the doorway, a woman in red robes beside him.

"Zhao Feng," the woman said, her voice smooth as silk. "Your note intrigued me. Tell me what you know about the Xu family's debts

title: The Price of Ambition
content:
The night air carried the stench of fish and rot from the river. Zhao Feng stood in the shadows of a collapsed pier, watching the cargo boat drift toward the dock. Three men worked the ropes, their movements practiced and silent. They weren't merchants. They were thieves, smugglers, men who traded in things that left no paper trail.

Exactly what he needed.

The man who stepped off the boat wore a hooded cloak, but Zhao Feng recognized the walk. Confident. Leisurely. The walk of someone who owned the ground beneath his feet.

"Brother Zhao." The man pushed back his hood, revealing a face scarred by acid burns. "I heard you've been busy."

"Brother Fang." Zhao Feng stepped forward. "You heard correctly."

Fang Wu was a spirit stone dealer from the southern provinces, a man who'd built his fortune on the backs of dead cultivators. In Zhao Feng's past life, they'd done business exactly twice—both times profitable, both times without bloodshed. Fang Wu was ruthless but fair. He kept his word because breaking it cost more than keeping it.

"Your message said you had something interesting." Fang Wu gestured to the boat. "I came all this way. Don't waste my time."

Zhao Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a single qi stone. Not the low-grade stones he'd been trading—this one pulsed with a deep amber light, veins of gold running through its core like frozen lightning.

Fang Wu's eyes narrowed. "Where did you get that?"

"A mine in the eastern hills. Abandoned. The previous owner thought it was empty."

"That's impossible. The eastern hills were picked clean fifty years ago."

"The previous owner was wrong." Zhao Feng tossed the stone to Fang Wu. "Check for yourself."

Fang Wu caught it, held it up to the moonlight, and went still. His fingers traced the veins, his thumb pressing against the surface. A long silence stretched between them.

"This is mid-grade," he whispered. "Almost high-grade. A stone like this could fund a cultivation breakthrough."

"Fifty of them could fund a sect."

Fang Wu's head snapped up. "You're lying."

"I don't lie in business. It's inefficient." Zhao Feng folded his arms. "The vein runs deep. Deeper than anyone bothered to check. I've already extracted thirty stones like that one, and there's more waiting. But I need equipment. Protection. A buyer who can move product without asking questions."

"And you want me to be that buyer."

"I want you to be my partner. Sixty-forty split, my favor. You provide the capital and the connections. I provide the stones."

Fang Wu laughed. It wasn't a pleasant sound. "You're a boy who's been trading for six months. You have no reputation, no backing, no protection. If I wanted those stones, I could kill you right now and take everything."

"You could try."

The words came out flat. Unafraid. Zhao Feng met Fang Wu's gaze and held it.

For a long moment, neither of them moved.

Then Fang Wu's scarred face cracked into something that might have been a smile. "You've got balls, kid. I'll give you that." He tossed the stone back. "Fifty-fifty. I take half the risk, I take half the profit."

"Fifty-five forty-five. I'm the one putting my neck in that mine."

"Fifty-two forty-eight. And I want first refusal on any future finds."

Zhao Feng pretended to consider it. He'd already calculated this exact negotiation four different ways. "Deal."

They shook hands. Fang Wu's grip was iron, his palm calloused from decades of handling rough stones. "I'll have the equipment delivered in three days. You'll have my men at the mine by the end of the week." He paused. "One more thing. There's a man asking questions about you. Wu Chen."

Zhao Feng's expression didn't change. "I know."

"Then you know he works for the Xiao family. And the Xiao family doesn't send their dogs to sniff around unless they're planning to bite."

"What do they want?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" Fang Wu pulled his hood back up. "I'd find out fast, if I were you. The Xiao family doesn't leave loose ends."

He turned and walked back to his boat, disappearing into the cabin without another word. The ropes were cast off, and the vessel slipped back into the darkness.

Zhao Feng stood alone on the pier, the mid-grade qi stone cold in his palm.

Wu Chen.

The Xiao family.

In his past life, the Xiao family had been minor players, a third-rate clan that controlled a few spirit herb farms and a single mine. But in this life, they'd already sent someone to investigate him. That meant something had changed. Something he hadn't accounted for.

He needed information.

---

Xiao Chen found him at the warehouse an hour before dawn.

The boy moved like a ghost, slipping through the gap in the wall that Zhao Feng had deliberately left unsealed. His clothes were torn, his face streaked with dirt and what looked like dried blood.

"They know," he said, without preamble. "Wu Chen's not just watching you. He's reporting to someone higher. A woman. She came to the city three days ago."

Zhao Feng set down his ledger. "Describe her."

"Tall. Black hair with a white streak. She wears a jade ring on her left hand, and she has a scar on her jaw—like someone tried to cut her throat and failed."

The description hit Zhao Feng like a physical blow.

Lin Xue.

In his past life, she'd been his wife's closest friend, a woman who'd smiled at him at banquets and called him "Brother Zhao" while her husband plotted his destruction. She'd been there when his sons betrayed him. She'd watched him fall.

And now she was here. Six months after his rebirth. Asking questions.

"What else?"

"She's staying at the Golden Phoenix Inn. Room twelve. She meets with Wu Chen every night after midnight." Xiao Chen hesitated. "There's something else. Two nights ago, she visited the prison."

Zhao Feng's blood went cold. "The city prison?"

"Yes. She spent an hour inside. When she came out, she was smiling."

The city prison held three types of people: debtors, criminals, and cultivators who'd fallen from grace. If Lin Xue had gone there, she was looking for someone. Someone she thought could help her.

Or someone she thought could hurt him.

"How much do you need to survive?" Zhao Feng asked suddenly.

Xiao Chen blinked. "What?"

"Food. Shelter. Medicine. How much for one month?"

"I don't—maybe five spirit coins? But I can—"

Zhao Feng pulled a pouch from his belt and tossed it. "There's thirty. Take it."

"I didn't finish my job yet. I still have more to—"

"I'm not paying you for your work. I'm paying you to stay alive." Zhao Feng walked to the table and picked up a map of the city. "Lin Xue is dangerous. If she finds out you're working for me, she'll kill you. Not threaten you. Not warn you. She'll cut your throat and leave your body in the river."

"So I'll be more careful."

"No. You'll leave the city. Take the coins, go to the next province, and don't come back for at least six months."

Xiao Chen stared at him. For a moment, the boy's mask cracked, and Zhao Feng saw something raw underneath. Fear. Confusion. And something else—something that looked almost like betrayal.

"I thought you said I was valuable."

"You are. That's why I'm telling you to run."

"You said you saw a spark in me. You said you'd teach me how to survive." Xiao Chen's voice dropped. "Is this how survivors act? By running away?"

Zhao Feng's jaw tightened. In his past life, Xiao Chen had died because he'd stayed. Because he'd refused to abandon Zhao Feng when the walls closed in. The boy's loyalty had been his death sentence.

Not again.

"I'm trying to protect you."

"I didn't ask for protection." Xiao Chen stepped forward, his small hands balled into fists. "I asked for a chance. A real one. You said you'd teach me how to survive. So teach me. Don't send me away because you're scared."

The words cut deeper than they should have.

Because he was right.

Zhao Feng was scared. Not of Lin Xue. Not of Wu Chen or the Xiao family. He was scared of watching Xiao Chen die again. Of failing the only person who had ever been loyal to him.

"You don't know what you're asking."

"Then show me."

Silence stretched between them.

Zhao Feng looked at the boy—this stubborn, reckless, foolish child who had no idea what he was volunteering for. And for the first time since his rebirth, he felt something other than calculated ambition.

He felt hope.

"Fine." Zhao Feng unrolled the map. "You want to learn? Then learn this. Lin Xue is staying at the Golden Phoenix Inn. I need to know who she's meeting, what she's buying, and why she went to the prison. But I need more than observation. I need you to get inside her room."

Xiao Chen's eyes widened. "Her room?"

"There's a vent above the door. It's too small for an adult, but a child could fit. If you're quiet, if you're careful, you can listen to everything she says. Every name. Every plan. Every weakness."

"That's insane. If she catches me—"

"Then you die. And it'll be my fault." Zhao Feng met the boy's gaze. "That's the price of ambition, Xiao Chen. You don't get to be valuable without risking everything. So decide. Right now. Do you want to be a survivor, or do you want to be something more?"

Xiao Chen's hands stopped trembling.

"Something more."

"Then go. And don't get caught."

The boy slipped out into the darkness, leaving Zhao Feng alone with his map and his thoughts.

He stared at the mark he'd made on the Golden Phoenix Inn.

Lin Xue.

Why now? Why here?

In his past life, she hadn't appeared until years later, when his empire was already built and his enemies had already gathered. Her presence now meant something had shifted. A ripple he hadn't caused.

Unless—

Unless he wasn't the only one who'd been reborn.

The thought settled into his chest like a blade.

If Lin Xue remembered the past life too, then everything he'd planned, every advantage he'd calculated, every move he'd prepared—it was all worthless.

Because she already knew his playbook.

And she'd already made her countermove.

Zhao Feng looked at his hands. They were steady. Calm. The hands of a man who'd spent forty years learning to hide his fear.

But for the first time since he'd opened his eyes in this new life, he wasn't sure he was going to win.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Price of Information

The lantern flame flickered, casting dancing shadows across the ledger's yellowed pages. Zhao Feng's brush hovered over the numbers, but his mind had already moved beyond the arithmetic.

Ten thousand spirit coins. A respectable sum for a nobody who'd started with nothing three months ago. A pittance compared to what he'd once controlled.

He dipped the brush and wrote: *Projected growth at current rate: forty thousand by month six.*

Not enough. Not nearly enough.

The Immortal Ascension Tournament required sponsorship fees, bribes to officials, and the capital to acquire auction items that appeared only when the tournament approached. In his past life, he'd watched the major families fight over a single mid-grade spirit herb, driving its price to six hundred thousand spirit coins.

He had ten thousand.

A knock at the warehouse door broke his concentration. Three quick raps, a pause, then two more. The signal he'd arranged with Xiao Chen.

"Come in."

The door creaked open. Xiao Chen slipped through the gap like a shadow, his movements already more refined than they'd been a week ago. The boy had learned quickly.

"The man Wu Chen," Xiao Chen said, closing the door behind him. "He's not what he seems."

Zhao Feng set down his brush. "Explain."

"I followed him for three days like you asked. He meets with merchants during the day, talks about qi stones, negotiates prices. Normal enough." The boy's eyes glinted in the lantern light. "But at night, he goes to a different place. A teahouse in the eastern district called the Jade Lotus."

"The Jade Lotus serves cultivators of the fourth realm and above," Zhao Feng said slowly. "Wu Chen is a second realm merchant. He shouldn't be able to enter."

"He doesn't enter through the front." Xiao Chen pulled a crumpled piece of paper from his sleeve. "There's a back door. Servants' entrance. He goes in, stays for two hours, and leaves with a different expression each time."

"A different expression?"

"Sometimes he looks satisfied, like he's just made a good deal. Other times, he looks like he's been threatened. His hands shake when he lights his pipe after those visits." The boy held out the paper. "I drew the layout of the teahouse. The back entrance, the alley, the windows on the second floor."

Zhao Feng took the paper and examined it. The drawing was crude but accurate, with measurements scratched in the margins. The boy had paced the distances.

"This is good work, Xiao Chen."

The boy's cheeks flushed slightly, but he kept his voice steady. "What do you want me to do next?"

Zhao Feng studied the drawing. The second-floor windows faced the alley. If someone wanted to observe a meeting without being seen, that would be the position. But climbing the wall would require cultivation he didn't have and the boy couldn't perform.

"Stay away from the teahouse for now," Zhao Feng said. "I need you to watch Wu Chen's home instead. Learn his schedule. When he sleeps, when he eats, when he's alone."

"You think someone's controlling him?"

"I think no merchant of the second realm visits a fourth-realm establishment unless he has no choice. Someone has leverage over Wu Chen. I need to know who, and what they want."

Xiao Chen nodded and slipped back into the night.

---

Three days passed.

Zhao Feng spent them buying and selling, moving small quantities of qi stones through the market, building his reputation as a reliable dealer. He turned down several larger deals, knowing that greed would expose him before he was ready.

On the fourth day, a messenger arrived at his rented room.

The note was written on expensive paper, the characters formed with a calligrapher's precision: *The merchant Wu Chen requests the honor of your presence at the Golden Carp Restaurant, seventh hour. Matters of mutual benefit to discuss.*

Zhao Feng read the note three times. The handwriting wasn't Wu Chen's. He'd seen the man's signature on contracts—clumsy, uneven strokes that spoke of a man who'd learned to write late in life.

Someone else had written this. Someone who wanted him to think it was from Wu Chen.

He burned the note and went to find Xiao Chen.

---

The Golden Carp Restaurant occupied the corner of Merchant's Row, a three-story establishment with red lanterns hanging from every eave. Inside, the air smelled of fried fish and rice wine. Wealthy merchants and lesser cultivators filled the tables, their voices rising in heated negotiations.

Zhao Feng arrived early, taking a seat at a table near the back where he could see both entrances. He ordered a pot of tea and waited.

The seventh hour came and went.

At the eighth hour, the restaurant's owner approached his table. A heavyset man with nervous eyes and sweat beading on his forehead.

"Master Zhao," the owner said, bowing low. "I apologize for the disturbance, but a message has arrived for you."

He held out a folded piece of paper, his hands trembling.

Zhao Feng took it. The seal was broken—the owner had read it first, under threat.

The message inside was short: *You didn't think I'd be foolish enough to meet you in public, did you? Come to the teahouse. Alone. Or the boy dies.*

No signature. No explanation.

But Zhao Feng knew.

He crushed the paper in his fist and stood up so fast his chair scraped against the floor. The restaurant's conversations paused, eyes turning toward him. He ignored them.

"Where is Xiao Chen?" he asked the owner.

The man's face went pale. "I don't know any boy, Master Zhao, I swear—"

"The teahouse. Which one?"

"The... the Jade Lotus. But you can't go there, Master Zhao. The people who run that place, they're not—"

Zhao Feng was already out the door.

---

The eastern district changed character after nightfall. The respectable shops closed their shutters, and different establishments opened their doors. Places that catered to cultivators with money and no scruples. Places where a mortal who asked too many questions might disappear.

The Jade Lotus sat at the end of a narrow street, its windows glowing with soft yellow light. The music of a pipa drifted through the walls, beautiful and melancholy.

Zhao Feng approached the front entrance.
