A doorman blocked his path—a cultivator of the third realm, his aura pressing against Zhao Feng like a physical weight. "This establishment is for cultivation masters only."

"I have an appointment," Zhao Feng said.

"With whom?"

Zhao Feng met the man's eyes. "Your employer knows my name. Tell him Zhao Feng is here."

The doorman's expression flickered. He disappeared inside, returning moments later with a look of grudging respect. "Follow me."

The interior of the Jade Lotus was nothing like the rough establishments in the merchant district. Polished wood floors, silk curtains, the faint scent of incense. Cultivators sat in private booths, their conversations shielded by formation arrays that distorted sound.

The doorman led him up a flight of stairs to the second floor, then down a hallway lined with closed doors. He stopped at the last one.

"He's inside."

Zhao Feng pushed open the door.

The room was small but elegant. A low table sat in the center, bearing a tea set and a single flickering candle. Behind the table, kneeling in perfect posture, was a woman.

She was young—no older than twenty-five—with sharp features and eyes the color of amber. Her hair was pinned up with a jade hairpin, and she wore robes of pale blue silk that marked her as a cultivator of at least the fifth realm.

In the corner of the room, bound and gagged, Xiao Chen struggled against his ropes.

"Sit," the woman said, gesturing to the cushion across from her.

Zhao Feng didn't sit. "Release the boy."

"In time. First, we talk." She poured tea into two cups, her movements precise and unhurried. "I've been watching you, Zhao Feng. Three months ago, you were nothing. A gutter rat with delusions. Now you control a significant portion of the low-grade qi stone trade in the eastern district."

"I'm flattered by your attention."

"Don't be. I'm not the only one who's noticed." She pushed a cup of tea toward him. "The major families have started asking questions. Where did this merchant come from? Who backs him? How does a mortal with no cultivation knowledge navigate the spirit stone market so adeptly?"

Zhao Feng kept his face neutral. "I'm just a merchant who works hard."

The woman laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "You're a mystery, Zhao Feng. And I hate mysteries." She reached into her sleeve and produced a small jade token, placing it on the table between them. "Do you know what this is?"

He knew. It was a communication token, inscribed with a formation that allowed long-distance messaging. Only cultivators of the sixth realm and above could afford them.

"That token belongs to the Xu family," she continued. "One of the five great clans. They've offered a reward for information about you. A substantial reward."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. The Xu family. In his past life, they'd been one of his greatest rivals. If they'd already noticed him...

"Who are you?" he asked.

"My name is Lin Yue. I'm what you might call an information broker. I trade in secrets." She picked up her tea cup and took a delicate sip. "The Xu family's offer is tempting, but I prefer to collect my own debts. And you, Zhao Feng, owe me a debt."

"I've never met you before tonight."

"No. But you've met my father." Her eyes hardened. "You ruined him."

The pieces clicked into place. Lin Yue. Lin. The merchant who'd tried to cheat him on his very first qi stone deal, the one he'd turned into a fool in front of the entire market.

"Lin Hou," Zhao Feng said.

"My father was a proud man. After you humiliated him, he couldn't show his face in the merchant district. His business collapsed. His cultivation began to backslide." Her voice remained calm, but her grip on the tea cup tightened. "Last week, he drank poisoned wine and didn't wake up."

Silence filled the room. The candle flickered.

"Your father tried to destroy me," Zhao Feng said slowly. "I only defended myself."

"You destroyed him. There's a difference." Lin Yue set down her cup. "But I'm not here for revenge, Zhao Feng. I'm here for compensation."

"Compensation."

"Your business. Your contacts. Your knowledge of the qi stone market." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Give me everything you've built, and I'll let the boy go. I'll even keep your secret from the Xu family."

Zhao Feng looked at Xiao Chen. The boy's eyes were wide and frightened, but he shook his head slightly. A warning. Don't agree.

He looked back at Lin Yue.

"And if I refuse?"

"Then I take the Xu family's reward. They'll send interrogators. You'll be taken to their compound, and they'll extract every secret you have." She leaned forward. "I've seen what happens to mortals who resist Xu family interrogators. They don't die quickly."

The candle guttered, shadows dancing across her face.

Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small leather pouch. He tossed it onto the table. It landed with the clink of metal.

"What's this?"

"Payment," Zhao Feng said. "For information."

Lin Yue's eyes narrowed. She opened the pouch and spilled its contents onto the table. Silver coins. A few spirit coins. Common currency.

"Insulting me

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Hidden Technique Map

He knew the location of the Falling Thunder Sect's abandoned outpost, buried beneath the Serpent's Spine Mountains. In his past life, the young woman—Lin Xue—had stumbled upon it by accident while fleeing from bandits. Inside, she'd found a complete set of Lightning Tribulation techniques, the remnants of a sect that had been destroyed three hundred years ago.

But Zhao Feng had never told anyone about that outpost. Not his sons, not his wives, not even his most trusted advisors. He'd planned to use it himself, to transition from merchant to cultivator in his old age. Then his sons had poisoned him before he could make the journey.

Now he was eighteen, with a weak constitution and barely enough qi to light a candle. The outpost was his only path to true power.

He closed the ledger and looked at the map spread across his table. The Serpent's Spine Mountains lay three weeks' travel to the north, past the Azure Forest and through the territory of the Blood Wolf Clan. Dangerous, but not impossible. Not if he prepared properly.

A knock at the warehouse door shattered his thoughts.

Zhao Feng's hand moved instinctively to the knife hidden in his sleeve. "Who is it?"

"Wu Chen," came the muffled reply. "You said to come at dusk."

Right. Wu Chen. The merchant he'd agreed to meet about a bulk purchase of qi stones. Zhao Feng had nearly forgotten in his excitement about the map.

He crossed to the door, slid the bolt back, and opened it a crack. Wu Chen stood outside, flanked by two bodyguards. The man was in his forties, with a round face and shrewd eyes that missed nothing. He wore silk robes that marked him as wealthy, but his hands were calloused—a man who'd worked for everything he had.

"Brother Zhao," Wu Chen said, bowing slightly. "I hope I'm not too early."

"Right on time." Zhao Feng opened the door fully and gestured inside. "Please, come in. Your guards can wait outside."

Wu Chen's eyes flickered to the dim interior. "You understand my concern. This is a substantial transaction."

"And you understand mine." Zhao Feng met his gaze without flinching. "I don't know your guards. I don't know what they might see or report to others. My business is my own."

A long moment passed. Then Wu Chen chuckled and waved his guards back. "Fair enough. A cautious merchant is a living merchant." He stepped inside, and Zhao Feng closed and locked the door behind him.

The warehouse was lit by a single lantern, casting long shadows across the crates. Wu Chen walked slowly through the space, examining the stacks of qi stones with an expert eye. He picked up a stone, held it to the light, and grunted in approval.

"Good quality. Not the best I've seen, but consistent." He set the stone down. "How much are you asking?"

"Three spirit coins per stone. Bulk discount at a hundred stones or more."

Wu Chen's eyebrows rose. "That's below market rate by twenty percent. Why?"

Because I need cash quickly, and because I want you as a regular customer. Because in six months, when the tournament happens, you'll remember this favor. "Let's say I'm investing in a long-term relationship."

"I see." Wu Chen pulled a pipe from his sleeve and lit it with a flick of his finger—a small cultivation technique, barely worth noting, but it showed he had at least some training. "And what else are you selling?"

Zhao Feng stilled. "What do you mean?"

"I've heard rumors, Brother Zhao. A young merchant who appeared from nowhere, buying up spirit grass and low-grade qi stones with suspicious efficiency. Some say you're a front for a larger family. Others say you're a thief." Wu Chen took a long drag from his pipe. "I say you're someone who knows something the rest of us don't."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. He'd been too aggressive, too visible. In his past life, he'd learned to move slowly, to build connections before making big plays. But this time, he'd been impatient. Hungry.

"I know that the market is about to shift," Zhao Feng said carefully. "The Immortal Ascension Tournament is six months away. The major families are stockpiling resources for their champions. Prices will triple before the first match."

Wu Chen's eyes narrowed. "That's not a secret. Every merchant in the city knows that."

"Then you also know that most of them are selling now, trying to cash in before the prices drop after the tournament. They're thinking short-term." Zhao Feng leaned forward. "I'm thinking long-term. After the tournament, when the families have exhausted their resources, who will they come to for replenishment?"

"The ones who held back their stock."

"Exactly."

Wu Chen was silent for a long moment, smoke curling from his pipe. Then he laughed—a genuine, surprised sound. "You're either brilliant or insane. I haven't decided which yet."

"Give me six months, and I'll prove it's the former."

"Fine. I'll take two hundred stones at your discount price." Wu Chen reached into his robe and pulled out a heavy pouch. "But I have conditions. I want first refusal on anything new you acquire. And I want to know if you hear anything that might affect the market."

Zhao Feng nodded. "Agreed."

They completed the transaction quickly, counting stones and spirit coins with practiced efficiency. As Wu Chen prepared to leave, he paused at the door.

"One more thing, Brother Zhao. There's a man asking questions about you. Older, missing two fingers on his right hand. He's been going to every merchant in the district, offering money for information."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. In his past life, the man with two missing fingers was a spy for his eldest son, Zhao Wei. The man had been the one to discover Zhao Feng's secret accounts, the one who'd provided the evidence that led to his downfall.

"What did you tell him?" Zhao Feng asked, keeping his voice level.

"Nothing. I don't sell information about my business partners." Wu Chen's eyes glinted. "But others might not be so loyal. Be careful, Brother Zhao. The wolves are circling."

The door closed behind Wu Chen, leaving Zhao Feng alone with his thoughts.

The man with two missing fingers. Here. Already.

In his past life, Zhao Feng had been fifty when the man found him. Now he was eighteen, barely started, and the same hunter was already on his trail. It meant someone powerful was watching. Someone who sensed opportunity.

Zhao Feng looked at the map on his table. The outpost was his only chance. If he could reach it, claim the techniques, and advance his cultivation, he could protect himself. But the journey would take weeks, and he couldn't leave his business unattended.

He needed a plan. He needed allies.

He needed Xiao Chen.

---

The boy arrived at midnight, slipping through the warehouse window like a shadow. His clothes were still ragged, but he'd washed his face and tied his hair back. He looked less like a street rat and more like a young wolf.

"Wu Chen left two hours ago," Xiao Chen reported, his voice low. "He went straight to the Golden Lotus Tea House and met with a woman. They talked for about an hour. I couldn't hear what they said."

"A woman? Describe her."

"Tall, black hair, wearing blue silk. She had a jade hairpin shaped like a phoenix."

Zhao Feng's jaw tightened. The Golden Lotus Tea House was owned by the Zhao family—his family in his past life. The woman with the phoenix hairpin was likely his second son's wife, a cunning woman who'd helped orchestrate his downfall.

Wu Chen was already connected to them. The "friendly merchant" was a spy.

"Good work." Zhao Feng tossed Xiao Chen a small pouch of coins. "Keep watching Wu Chen. Follow him everywhere. I need to know everyone he meets, every place he goes."

Xiao Chen caught the pouch and weighed it in his hand. "And you? Where will you be?"

"Preparing for a journey." Zhao Feng rolled up the map and tucked it into his robe. "I'll be gone for a month. During that time, I need you to do something for me."

"What?"

Zhao Feng pulled out a second map—this one of the city's underground tunnels. In his past life, he'd used these tunnels to move goods without detection. They were dangerous, filled with rogue cultivators and beasts, but they were also the key to building a hidden network.

"There's a man who lives in the tunnels. He calls himself the Rat King. Find him. Tell him that Zhao Feng sends his regards and that I'll be needing his services when I return."

Xiao Chen studied the map, his brow furrowed. "And if he doesn't believe me?"

"Then show him this." Zhao Feng pulled a small jade token from his neck—a family heirloom from his past life, one of the few things he'd managed to keep. "He'll recognize it. He owes me a debt."

The boy took the token, his fingers brushing against Zhao Feng's. For a moment, something passed between them—a connection that transcended this life and the one before.

"I won't let you down," Xiao Chen said.

"I know." Zhao Feng gripped his shoulder. "That's why I chose you."

Xiao Chen slipped out the window, disappearing into the night. Zhao Feng watched him go, then turned back to his map.

The outpost. The techniques. The path to power.

But first, he had to survive the night.

Because as he stood there, planning his future, he heard it—a faint scraping sound from the roof. Someone was up there. Someone who'd been watching the warehouse.

Zhao Feng blew out the lantern and drew his knife.

The game had begun.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Snake in the Grass

The lantern flickered as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger, calculating margins that would make most merchants weep with envy. Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. In his past life, Lin Xue had emerged from obscurity, her lightning techniques tearing through the established prodigies like paper. The major families had fought over her like dogs over meat.

But none of them knew where she'd found those techniques.

Zhao Feng did.

The Falling Thunder Pavilion. A forgotten training ground in the southern hills, sealed behind a formation that had decayed over three centuries. In his past life, Lin Xue had stumbled upon it by accident, a lucky break that had launched her from outer disciple to sect elder in two years.

This time, Zhao Feng would find it first.

But first, he needed capital. More capital than ten thousand spirit coins could provide. The pavilion's entrance required a key—a jade token that was currently sitting in the private collection of Merchant Lin, the third-richest man in Greenstone City.

And Merchant Lin was not someone who sold to strangers.

A knock on the warehouse door pulled Zhao Feng from his thoughts. Three rapid taps, a pause, then two more. Xiao Chen's signal.

Zhao Feng slid the ledger into a hidden compartment beneath the table. "Enter."

The door creaked open, and Xiao Chen slipped inside like a shadow. The boy had cleaned up since their first meeting—his face washed, his hair tied back, his rags replaced with simple but intact clothing. But his eyes remained the same. Sharp. Watchful. Hungry.

"He's coming," Xiao Chen said, shutting the door behind him. "Wu Chen. He's got two men with him. One's a cultivator—Qi Condensation, third layer maybe. The other's just muscle."

Zhao Feng nodded. He'd expected as much. Wu Chen was the enforcer for the Wang family, the local power that had controlled the qi stone trade for three generations. In his past life, Zhao Feng had crushed them without breaking stride. But that was forty years from now, when he had resources and backing.

Tonight, he had a leaky warehouse and a street kid.

"Anything else?"

Xiao Chen hesitated. "There's something strange. Wu Chen met with someone before he came here. A woman. She was veiled, but she moved like... like she knew the shadows. I couldn't get close enough to hear what they said."

A woman. Veiled. Moving like shadows.

Zhao Feng's mind raced through the possibilities. The Wang family didn't employ women in their operations—too traditional, too superstitious. They believed women brought bad luck to business dealings. So who was she? A rival house? A sect representative?

Or someone from his past life, sent to check on him?

"Stay hidden," Zhao Feng said. "Watch. Listen. If things go wrong, don't interfere. Go to the South Market, find a man named Old Zhang. Tell him the jade fox sent you."

"And if you die?"

Zhao Feng almost smiled. "Then you keep the silver I gave you and find a new patron."

Xiao Chen's jaw tightened, but he said nothing. He melted back into the shadows, and a moment later, Zhao Feng was alone again.

He adjusted his robes, checked the formation talisman hidden in his sleeve, and waited.

---

Wu Chen arrived exactly at the eighth hour, as promised.

He was a thick man, built like a butcher, with a scar that ran from his temple to his jaw. His cultivation was minimal—barely Qi Condensation first layer—but his presence carried weight. Behind him, a thin man in gray robes scanned the warehouse with cold, assessing eyes. The third layer cultivator. Behind him, a brute with hands like hams cracked his knuckles.

"Zhao Feng," Wu Chen said, his voice oily. "I hear you've been making waves."

"I've been buying and selling," Zhao Feng replied, keeping his voice neutral. "That's what merchants do."

"Merchants pay their respects to the local powers. They ask permission before they set up shop." Wu Chen walked past him, running a finger along a crate. "You haven't done that."

"I wasn't aware I needed permission to trade qi stones."

"You need permission to trade in Wang territory. And this entire district is Wang territory." Wu Chen stopped at the table, picking up the mortar and pestle. He turned it over in his hands, examining it like he was appraising its value. "My employer is... concerned. You appeared out of nowhere. You have no family, no backing, no history. And yet you've accumulated enough stock to rival a small trading house."

"I'm resourceful."

"Resourceful." Wu Chen laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Resourceful doesn't buy you a warehouse full of qi stones. Resourceful doesn't get you connections to the outer sect elders. Resourceful doesn't make you a threat."

He set the mortar down with a crack that echoed through the empty space.

"So I'm going to ask you once, and I expect a truthful answer. Who do you work for?"

Zhao Feng met his gaze. "Myself."

The room went still. The third-layer cultivator's hand drifted toward his storage ring. The brute cracked his knuckles again, louder this time.

Wu Chen's smile faded. "That's unfortunate. I was hoping we could do this the easy way."

He reached into his robe and pulled out a contract, tossing it onto the table. The paper was yellowed, the characters written in blood-red ink.

A soul contract.

Zhao Feng's blood went cold. Soul contracts were rare—forbidden in most cities, punishable by death in the sect territories. They bound the signer's spirit to the holder, giving them absolute control. In his past life, Zhao Feng had seen men driven mad by soul contracts, their wills broken, their cultivation crippled.

To offer one was to declare war.

"Sign it," Wu Chen said, "and you keep your stock. You keep your business. You pay seventy percent of your profits to the Wang family for the next ten years, and at the end, we give you your freedom."

"Or?"

Wu Chen gestured to the cultivator. The man stepped forward, and the temperature in the room dropped. Frost formed on the crates, crawling across the qi stones like silver veins.

"Or we take everything. Your stock, your warehouse, your life. And we make sure no one remembers you existed."

Zhao Feng looked at the contract. At the blood-red characters that promised eternal servitude. At Wu Chen's smug face and the cultivator's cold eyes.

In his past life, he'd have signed. He'd have bowed, scraped, and spent ten years scheming his way out of the trap.

But he wasn't that man anymore.

He reached into his sleeve and activated the formation talisman.

The effect was instantaneous.

A barrier of golden light erupted around him, pushing the frost back. The cultivator's eyes widened as his technique shattered against the barrier's surface. The brute stumbled, caught off guard. Wu Chen's smug expression twisted into shock.

"What—"

Zhao Feng didn't wait for him to finish.

He grabbed the contract, tore it in half, and tossed the pieces into Wu Chen's face.

"I refuse."

The room exploded into chaos.

The cultivator recovered first, a blade of ice forming in his hand. He lunged, but Zhao Feng was already moving—not to fight, but to escape. He'd prepared for this. A secondary formation beneath the floorboards, linked to a transport talisman buried in the wall.

But as he reached for the trigger, something grabbed his ankle.

Ice. Spreading up his leg, freezing him in place.

The cultivator smiled. "Did you think a street merchant could afford a real formation? Your barrier was barely Qi Condensation level. Cute. But not enough."

Zhao Feng's heart hammered. He'd miscalculated. The formation talisman had been his insurance, but against a third-layer cultivator, it was nothing more than a party trick.

The ice climbed to his knee. His waist. His chest.

Wu Chen stepped forward, picking up the torn pieces of the contract. "Stupid boy. We were going to let you live. Now? Now I'm going to make an example of you."

He snapped his fingers, and the cultivator raised his blade of ice for the killing blow.

And then the warehouse door exploded inward.

A figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the moonlight. Tall. Cloaked. A blade in each hand, dripping with qi so dense it crackled in the air.

"I wouldn't do that if I were you," a woman's voice said. Low. Dangerous. Familiar.

The cultivator froze. Wu Chen turned, his face pale.

"Who—"

The woman stepped forward, and the lantern light caught her face.

Zhao Feng's breath caught.

It was the woman from the Jade Pavilion. The merchant's wife he'd seen at the auction. But her eyes—those soft, demure eyes—were now hard as steel, gleaming with killing intent.

"I said," she repeated, her blades rising, "I wouldn't do that."

The cultivator laughed. "And who are you to—"

He never finished the sentence.

She moved so fast that Zhao Feng's eyes couldn't track her. One moment she was at the door. The next, she was behind the cultivator, both blades buried in his back. He stared down at the steel protruding from his chest, his mouth opening and closing like a fish.

Then he crumpled.

Wu Chen screamed, scrambling backward. The brute charged, fists swinging. The woman sidestepped, caught his arm, and twisted. The crack of bone was sickening. The brute fell, howling.

In three breaths, it was over.

The woman stood in the center of the warehouse, blood dripping from her blades. She looked at Wu Chen, who was pressed against the wall, trembling.

"Tell the Wang family," she said, "that Zhao Feng is under my protection. If they touch him again, I'll burn their compound to the ground and salt the ashes."

Wu Chen nodded frantically, then bolted out the door, leaving his men behind.

The woman turned to Zhao Feng.

The ice around him had melted. He was free. But he didn't move.

"You," he said.

"Me." She sheathed her blades, a ghost of a smile on her lips. "I believe we have business to discuss, Merchant Zhao."

She reached into her robe and pulled out a jade token. The same token he'd been planning to steal from Merchant Lin.

"I believe you're looking for this."

Zhao Feng stared at the token, his mind racing.

Who was this woman? How did she know about the Falling Thunder Pavilion? And why was she helping him?

"You have questions," she said, reading his expression. "Good. That means you're smart enough to survive."

She tossed the token to him. He caught it reflexively.

"Meet me at the South Market tomorrow, at midnight. Come alone. And bring your ledger."

She turned and walked out, leaving him standing in the blood-soaked warehouse, holding the key to everything he'd been planning.

Xiao Chen emerged from the shadows, his face pale.

"Boss... who was that?"

Zhao Feng looked at the token in his hand. Then at the bodies on the floor.

"I have no idea," he said. "But I'm going to find out."

Outside, the woman vanished into the night, her cloak blending with the shadows.

But before she disappeared completely, she paused, looking back at the warehouse.

"Interesting," she murmured to herself. "Very interesting."

Then she was gone, leaving only the echo of her laughter in the empty streets.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Debt Collector's Visit

The knock came at midnight.

Zhao Feng's eyes snapped open, his hand already reaching for the dagger hidden beneath his pillow. The warehouse was dark, save for the single candle he'd left burning on the table. The flame flickered as someone pounded on the door again.

"Open up! I know you're in there!"

The voice was rough, slurred with drink. Zhao Feng rose silently, his bare feet cold against the dirt floor. He'd been sleeping in the warehouse for three weeks now, ever since his landlady had threatened to raise his rent. The leaky roof and rat droppings were worth the savings.

He approached the door and peered through a crack in the wood. A man stood outside, flanked by two others. They wore the colors of the Iron Crow sect—black robes with silver embroidery, the mark of a debt collection crew.

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

In his past life, the Iron Crow sect had been the strongest of the minor cultivation factions in Crimson Cloud City. Loan sharks. Thugs. Men who broke bones first and asked questions never. He'd dealt with them before, back when he was just starting out the first time.

But this was too early. He wasn't supposed to be on their radar yet.

He unlatched the door and stepped outside, dagger concealed in his sleeve. "Can I help you?"

The lead man was broad-shouldered, with a scar that ran from his temple to his jaw. His cultivation was at the sixth level of Qi Condensation—strong enough to crush a mortal like Zhao Feng with a flick of his wrist.

"You Zhao Feng?" the man asked, a cruel smile spreading across his face.

"That depends on who's asking."

"Name's Liu Bao. I collect debts for Elder Wu of the Iron Crow sect." He pulled a folded piece of paper from his robe and held it up. "I have a bill here, signed by one Zhao Feng of the Outer Market District. Amount due: fifteen thousand spirit coins. Due date: yesterday."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. "I've never signed anything with your sect."

"Maybe you forgot." Liu Bao shoved the paper into Zhao Feng's hands. "Take a look."

The signature was a rough approximation of his own handwriting. Close enough to pass inspection for anyone who wasn't paying close attention. The seal at the bottom was the Iron Crow sect's official mark, complete with a faint spiritual signature that verified its authenticity.

A forgery. But a good one.

"Someone's using my name," Zhao Feng said, keeping his voice neutral. "I have no business with your sect."

"Not my problem." Liu Bao cracked his knuckles. "The debt is in your name. You pay, or we collect in other ways."

Zhao Feng's mind raced through possibilities. Fifteen thousand spirit coins was more than the total value of everything he owned. Even if he liquidated all his stock, he'd come up short by at least five thousand.

And whoever had forged that document knew it.

This wasn't a simple shakedown. This was an attack. Someone was trying to destroy him before he could gain a foothold.

"I need time," Zhao Feng said.

"You need fifteen thousand spirit coins." Liu Bao stepped closer, his breath reeking of cheap wine. "You have three days. After that, we start breaking things. First your merchandise. Then your bones."

He reached out and patted Zhao Feng's cheek, a gesture of pure contempt. "Don't try to run, little merchant. We always find our debtors."

The three men turned and walked away, their laughter echoing through the empty street.

Zhao Feng stood in the doorway, his hands trembling with rage. Not fear. Rage.

He knew who had done this.

There was only one person in Crimson Cloud City who would recognize his name, who knew he'd started trading qi stones, who had the connections to forge an Iron Crow debt slip.

Wu Chen.

The man who'd come to his warehouse last week, sniffing around like a dog after a bone. Zhao Feng had been cautious, revealing nothing of his true plans. But Wu Chen was smarter than he'd appeared. He'd seen the potential in what Zhao Feng was building, and he'd decided to crush it before it could grow.

No. Before Zhao Feng could grow.

In his past life, Wu Chen had been a minor functionary in the Wang family's merchant operations. Nothing special. A man who followed orders and collected his salary. But now, with a decade of experience that shouldn't exist, Wu Chen was acting on instincts he hadn't yet developed.

The butterfly effect. Zhao Feng's rebirth was already changing the timeline, and not all those changes were beneficial.

He went back inside and sat down at his table, the candle casting long shadows across his ledger. He needed to think. Three days. He had three days to find fifteen thousand spirit coins, or to find a way to prove the debt was fraudulent.

Option one: Pay the debt. Impossible. He didn't have the money, and even if he sold everything, he'd be left with nothing. Back to square one.

Option two: Prove the forgery. Possible, but difficult. He'd need to find the person who signed his name, or get a spiritual calligrapher to authenticate the signature. Expensive, time-consuming, and he didn't have the connections yet.

Option three: Go to the city authorities. Useless. The Iron Crow sect had judges in their pocket. Any official complaint would disappear into a black hole.

Option four: Eliminate the threat.

Zhao Feng's hand stilled over the ledger.

In his past life, he'd built an empire through legitimate means. Trade agreements. Strategic partnerships. Acquisitions. He'd prided himself on never resorting to the kind of violence that lesser merchants used.

But that Zhao Feng had died betrayed and broken in a gutter.

This Zhao Feng was different.

He stood up and walked to the back of the warehouse, where he kept a small chest hidden beneath a loose floorboard. Inside was his emergency fund—five hundred spirit coins, a few pills, and a jade slip containing a technique he'd never told anyone about.

The Shadow Step technique. A mortal-level movement art he'd acquired in his past life from a dying assassin. It allowed the user to move silently, to blend into shadows, to strike from darkness.

He'd never used it. Had never needed to.

He slipped the jade into his pocket and blew out the candle.

---

The Iron Crow sect's compound sat on the eastern edge of Crimson Cloud City, a fortress of black stone and iron gates. Zhao Feng approached it from the south, moving through the beggars' quarter where the city's poorest huddled around small fires.

He found Xiao Chen exactly where he'd left him a week ago—huddled in the corner of the temple, his thin arms wrapped around his knees.

The boy looked up as Zhao Feng approached, his eyes widening. "You came back."

"I told you I would." Zhao Feng sat down across from him. "I have a job for you. Dangerous. But if you do it well, I'll double your payment."

"What kind of job?"

"I need you to find someone for me. A man named Wu Chen. He's a merchant, mid-level, works for one of the big families. I need to know where he sleeps, who he meets with, and what he does every hour of the day."

Xiao Chen's brow furrowed. "You want me to spy on him."

"I want you to watch him. There's a difference."

"For how long?"

"Three days. Starting now."

The boy stood up, brushing dirt from his ragged clothes. "I'll need money for food. And a place to stay where I can report back to you."

Zhao Feng tossed him a pouch containing fifty spirit coins. "There's an abandoned tannery two streets from here. Meet me there at midnight every night. If you see something urgent, come find me at the warehouse."

Xiao Chen caught the pouch and weighed it in his hand. "You trust me not to run?"

"I trust that you're smart enough to know fifty coins is nothing compared to what I can give you."

The boy's lips twitched into something that might have been a smile. "I'll find your man."

He slipped out of the temple and disappeared into the night, moving with the practiced silence of someone who'd learned to survive by not being seen.

Zhao Feng watched him go, then turned toward the Iron Crow compound.

---

The walls were twenty feet high, topped with iron spikes that glowed with a faint spiritual light. Traps. Anyone who tried to climb over would be electrocuted.

But Zhao Feng wasn't planning to climb.

He circled the compound until he found what he was looking for: a drainage tunnel, barely wide enough for a man to crawl through. In his past life, he'd used this same tunnel to smuggle goods past the sect's guards. He knew it led to the compound's kitchen, which was empty after midnight.

He squeezed through the narrow opening, ignoring the stench of sewage and rotting food. The tunnel was dark, cramped, and suffocating. But he'd endured worse in his first life. Much worse.

He emerged into the kitchen, brushing dirt from his clothes. The room was dark, lit only by the dying embers of the cooking fire. He moved silently through the compound, keeping to the shadows, using the Shadow Step technique to muffle his footsteps.

The compound was quiet. Most of the sect's members were asleep, their cultivation resting in their dantians. A few guards patrolled the walls, but their attention was focused outward, not inward.

Zhao Feng found what he was looking for in the main hall: a massive ledger book, bound in black leather, sitting on a desk beneath a painting of the sect's founder. The debt records.

He opened the book and flipped through the pages, searching for his name.

And there it was. Page forty-seven, entry twelve. Zhao Feng, Outer Market District. Debt of fifteen thousand spirit coins. Signed and sealed three days ago.

The signature was a forgery, but the seal was real. Someone in the Iron Crow sect had sanctioned this.

He tore the page out and slipped it into his robe, then closed the ledger. The physical evidence was the first step. Now he needed to find out who had authorized the fake debt.

He turned to leave—and froze.

A man stood in the doorway, his arms crossed, a faint smile on his lips. He was tall, lean, with sharp features and eyes that gleamed with amusement. His cultivation was at the ninth level of Qi Condensation—almost Foundation Establishment.

"Looking for something, little merchant?"

Zhao Feng's heart hammered against his ribs. He recognized that voice. Recognized that face.

In his past life, this man had been his greatest enemy.

Elder Feng of the Iron Crow sect. The man who'd orchestrated the destruction of Zhao Feng's first business empire, who'd sold his secrets to the Wang family, who'd laughed as Zhao Feng's sons turned against him.

"You're younger than I remember," Zhao Feng said, his voice remarkably steady.

Elder Feng's smile faltered. "We've met before?"

"No." Zhao Feng forced a calm he didn't feel. "But I know of you. Everyone knows of Elder Feng, the Iron Crow's most promising rising star."

Flattery. It was the only weapon he had.

Elder Feng's smile returned, though something cautious lingered in his eyes. "Flattery won't save you, boy. Breaking into a sect's compound is a capital offense."

"I wasn't breaking in. I was looking for the truth." Zhao Feng held up the torn page. "Someone forged a debt in my name. I came to find proof."

"And did you find it?"

"I found this." He tossed the page onto the desk. "A fake signature. A real seal. Someone in this sect is running a side

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Poison in the Tea

Zhao Feng closed the ledger and extinguished the lantern. The Immortal Ascension Tournament was the key, but the lock was far more complicated than he'd initially believed. In his past life, the lightning cultivator—Mei Lingxi—had emerged from nowhere, a scrawny outer disciple with dirt under her nails and fire in her meridians. Everyone assumed she'd stumbled upon a lucky inheritance.

They were wrong.

Zhao Feng had spent three years tracking her rise after the tournament, hoping to recruit her for his merchant fleet. What he'd found instead was a web of connections so deep it had made even him pause. Mei Lingxi wasn't lucky. She was planted. Her techniques came from the Shadow Crane Sect, a forbidden organization that had been supposedly destroyed fifty years before her birth.

Someone had been playing a very long game.

He stepped out of the warehouse and locked the door behind him. The night air carried the smell of frying oil and sewage, the familiar stench of the merchant district after dark. Lanterns flickered in windows, casting pools of amber light onto the muddy streets. A dog barked somewhere in the distance.

Xiao Chen should have returned by now.

Zhao Feng's hand drifted to the small knife hidden inside his sleeve. The boy had been following Wu Chen for three days, mapping his movements, his contacts, his habits. Zhao Feng had taught him the basics of shadowing: never make eye contact, vary your distance, use crowds as cover. Xiao Chen had learned fast, faster than any street kid had a right to.

But tonight, he was late.

Zhao Feng began walking toward the temple where they'd agreed to meet. The streets grew narrower as he left the merchant district behind, the buildings pressing in on both sides like rotten teeth. He passed a group of drunks singing off-key, a woman washing clothes in a public fountain, a peddler packing up his stall of counterfeit talismans.

The temple loomed ahead, its roof sagging, its walls covered in moss. Zhao Feng slipped through the gap in the eastern wall and entered the main hall. Empty. The statue of the war god stared down at him with blind stone eyes, a crack running through its face like a scar.

No Xiao Chen.

Zhao Feng waited ten minutes. Twenty. The silence grew thick, broken only by the scuttling of rats in the walls. His instincts screamed that something was wrong. Wu Chen was a minor player, a broker for the Lu family, but minor players could still be dangerous if they suspected they were being watched.

He was about to leave when he heard it: a soft scrape of cloth against stone, coming from behind the altar.

He drew his knife. "Come out slowly. I'd hate to kill someone who's only looking for a place to sleep."

A figure emerged from the shadows. It wasn't Xiao Chen.

The man was tall, lean, with the calloused hands of a cultivator who'd spent decades gripping a sword. His robes were plain gray, but the fabric was high-quality, the kind that cost more than most people made in a year. A thin scar ran from his left temple to his jaw, pulling his mouth into a permanent half-smile.

"You're harder to find than I expected, Zhao Feng," the man said. His voice was smooth, almost pleasant. "I've been watching you for three days. You don't act like a boy of eighteen."

Zhao Feng kept his knife low, his body relaxed. "Who's asking?"

"My name is Iron Blade." The man inclined his head, a gesture that wasn't quite a bow. "I represent certain interests. Interests that would like to offer you a proposition."

"Where's the boy?"

Iron Blade's smile widened. "Safe. For now. He's quite resourceful, your little shadow. He almost lost me twice. But I've been following people for thirty years, and he's only been doing it for three days."

Zhao Feng's grip tightened on the knife. The man was a professional. Not a local thug, not a hired sword from one of the merchant families. Someone with real training.

"What do you want?"

"Your cooperation." Iron Blade reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade slip, tossing it to Zhao Feng's feet. "That contains a list of names. People who will be attending the Immortal Ascension Tournament. People who need to have... accidents before the competition begins."

Zhao Feng didn't pick it up. "You want me to kill people."

"I want you to arrange their deaths. You're a merchant. You have access to resources. Poison, faulty equipment, contaminated qi stones. The means don't matter, only the results." Iron Blade's eyes glittered in the dim light. "In exchange, you'll receive enough spirit coins to buy half the merchant district. And more importantly—you'll receive protection."

"Protection from what?"

"From the people who are already hunting you."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Zhao Feng's mind raced. He'd been careful. He'd covered his tracks, used false names, never made direct contact with anyone who could trace him back to his past life. But someone had found him anyway.

"Who's hunting me?"

Iron Blade tilted his head. "You really don't know, do you? That's almost impressive. You've been operating in this city for months, building your little empire, and you never once wondered why the Lu family hasn't crushed you? Why the Zhao family hasn't come looking for their disgraced son?"

The blood drained from Zhao Feng's face. He'd assumed his disguise was holding. He'd changed his name, altered his appearance, kept his distance from everyone who'd known him in his past life.

But someone had recognized him anyway.

"The Zhao family doesn't know I'm alive," he said slowly. "They think I died in the gutter three years ago."

"They do," Iron Blade agreed. "But someone else knows. Someone who's been watching the Zhao family's movements, waiting for you to resurface. They've been tracking every disgraced son, every runaway, every merchant who appears out of nowhere with suspiciously good instincts."

Zhao Feng's stomach turned to ice. There was only one person who would be watching the Zhao family that closely. Only one person who would recognize the patterns of his business tactics.

His fifth son. The one who'd orchestrated his betrayal. The one who'd smiled as he signed the documents that stripped Zhao Feng of everything.

"Who do you work for?" Zhao Feng demanded.

Iron Blade shook his head. "That's not part of the deal. Accept the contract, and I'll tell you everything I know. Refuse, and I walk away, and you can face whatever's coming alone."

"And Xiao Chen?"

"He'll be released once you accept. I give you my word."

Zhao Feng laughed, a harsh sound that echoed through the empty temple. "Your word? You're an assassin, not a noble. Your word is worth less than the dirt under my boots."

Iron Blade's smile didn't waver. "I'm a professional. My reputation is my currency. If I break my word, no one will hire me. So yes, you can trust my word, even if you can't trust anything else about me."

Zhao Feng looked at the jade slip on the ground. It glowed faintly, pulsing with a soft green light. All he had to do was pick it up. Accept the contract. Become a killer for hire.

In his past life, he'd never dirtied his own hands. He'd paid others to do the killing. But this wasn't his past life. This was a second chance, and he'd sworn he wouldn't make the same mistakes.

"Tell me one thing," Zhao Feng said. "The lightning cultivator. Mei Lingxi. Does she work for the same people who sent you?"

Iron Blade's expression flickered. It was barely a moment, barely a crack in his professional mask, but Zhao Feng caught it.

"No," Iron Blade said. But his voice was too careful, too measured. "She has nothing to do with this."

You're lying, Zhao Feng thought. He stored the information away, another piece of the puzzle clicking into place. The Shadow Crane Sect. The Immortal Ascension Tournament. The assassination contracts. They were all connected.

"I need time to think about it," Zhao Feng said.

"You have until dawn tomorrow." Iron Blade turned and walked toward the gap in the wall. "Don't be late. And don't try to run. I'll find you."

He vanished into the night, leaving Zhao Feng alone in the temple with the jade slip and the weight of a decision that could destroy everything he'd built.

---

Zhao Feng didn't sleep that night. He sat in his warehouse, the jade slip on the table before him, and he calculated.

If he accepted the contract, he'd gain resources, protection, and information. But he'd also become a puppet, dancing on strings held by an unknown master. If he refused, he'd face an enemy who knew his identity, who'd been tracking him since his rebirth.

Either path was dangerous. But one path offered a chance to learn who was pulling the strings.

He picked up the jade slip.

The list of names was short. Seven people, all of them minor cultivators scheduled to participate in the tournament. No one important. No one whose death would cause a major investigation.

Except the last name.

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold.

Mei Lingxi.

The lightning cultivator. The woman who'd won the tournament in his past life. The one who'd been planted by the Shadow Crane Sect.

Someone wanted her dead before she could compete.

And Zhao Feng was supposed to be the one to kill her.

He set the jade slip down, his hands shaking. This was no ordinary assassination contract. This was a move in a game he'd only just begun to understand. The Shadow Crane Sect had planted Mei Lingxi, and now someone was trying to remove her from the board.

But who? And why?

The answer came to him like a blade sliding between his ribs.

The same person who'd recognized him. The same person who'd been watching the Zhao family.

His fifth son.

But in this timeline, his fifth son was only thirteen years old. A child. There was no way a thirteen-year-old could orchestrate this level of conspiracy.

Unless his son had also been reborn.

Zhao Feng's breath caught in his throat. He'd assumed he was the only one who'd been given a second chance. But what if he was wrong? What if his betrayal hadn't been the end of his life, but the beginning of a war that would span lifetimes?

He looked at the jade slip again, his reflection staring back at him from its polished surface.

Dawn was coming. He had a choice to make.

And for the first time since his rebirth, Zhao Feng didn't know which path would lead to victory—and which would lead to another death.

The lantern sputtered and died, plunging the warehouse into darkness.

Outside, footsteps approached. Slow. Deliberate.

Xiao Chen's voice came through the door, barely a whisper.

"Boss. I got free. But they know about the warehouse. They're coming."

Zhao Feng's hand closed around the jade slip.

The trap had already closed.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Price of Secrets

The night air carried the stench of rotting fish and cheap wine. Zhao Feng stood in the shadow of a broken awning, watching the back entrance of the Golden Carp Pavilion. Moonlight caught the edge of his jade pendant as he adjusted his collar.

Three days had passed since Xiao Chen had agreed to work for him. Three days of watching, waiting, gathering threads that would eventually weave into a net.

The boy had proven himself faster than expected.

He'd followed Wu Chen to five different teahouses, two brothels, and a gambling den. He'd memorized the names of every person the man spoke to, every handshake exchanged, every envelope that changed palms. Most importantly, he'd discovered where Wu Chen went when he thought no one was watching.

The Eastern Market Vault.

A storage facility owned by the Lin family, one of the three major merchant clans in Greenstone City. The same Lin family that had crushed Zhao Feng's first business venture in his past life, absorbing his assets when he'd been too weak to resist.

*Not this time.*

A soft scrape of fabric behind him. Zhao Feng didn't turn.

"You're late," he said quietly.

Xiao Chen slipped out of the darkness, his movements barely stirring the air. The boy had learned to move like a ghost in just three days. Either he had natural talent, or he'd been practicing survival long before Zhao Feng found him.

"Wu Chen left the vault an hour ago," Xiao Chen said, his voice barely above a whisper. "He was carrying a box. Iron-bound, about this big." He held his hands apart the width of his shoulders. "He took it to the Xu mansion."

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

The Xu family. The weakest of the three major clans, perpetually struggling against the Lin and Song families for market dominance. In his past life, they'd been the first to fall, their assets divided like carrion between the vultures.

"Did he go inside?"

"No. He handed the box to a servant at the side gate. Then he went to the Drunken Immortal Tavern and ordered three bottles of their best wine."

*Celebrating,* Zhao Feng thought. *Or drowning his nerves.*

"What else?"

Xiao Chen hesitated. It was the first time the boy had shown uncertainty since their arrangement began.

"There's something else. Something I don't understand."

"Speak."

"I followed Wu Chen yesterday to a meeting at the Jade Lotus Temple. He met a woman there. Veiled. Expensive robes. They spoke for an hour in the garden. I couldn't get close enough to hear, but..." Xiao Chen's brow furrowed. "She gave him something. A token. Black jade, with a symbol carved into it. I've never seen the symbol before."

Zhao Feng's hands tightened at his sides.

*Black jade. A single symbol.*

He knew that token. He'd seen it in his past life, pressed into the palm of a dying man, carried by messengers who moved through the shadows of the cultivation world. It was the mark of the Shadow Market—the underground network that traded in forbidden techniques, stolen spirit tools, and information that could topple empires.

Wu Chen wasn't just selling information to the Xu family.

He was connected to something much larger.

"How far away is the Xu mansion from here?"

"Twenty minutes if you run. Thirty if you walk."

"Take me there."

---

The Xu mansion squatted at the edge of the merchant district, a sprawling compound of weathered stone and cracked tiles. Once, it had been grand. Now it looked like a dying beast, its bones showing through thin flesh.

Zhao Feng circled to the servant's entrance, the same side gate Xiao Chen had described. A single lantern hung above the door, casting weak light onto the cobblestones.

"Wait here," he told Xiao Chen. "If I'm not out in an hour, go to the warehouse and burn everything."

The boy's eyes widened. "Burn—"

"Do it. Don't hesitate. Don't try to save anything. Just burn it all."

Zhao Feng didn't wait for a response. He walked to the gate and knocked three times.

Silence. Then footsteps, heavy and slow. The gate creaked open to reveal a middle-aged servant with a face like weathered leather.

"The Xu family isn't receiving guests at this hour."

"I'm not a guest. I'm a merchant with a proposition." Zhao Feng pulled a single qi stone from his sleeve, not the low-grade ones he traded in, but a mid-grade stone worth a hundred times more. The servant's eyes locked onto it. "Tell your master that I have information about the Lin family's supply routes. Information that could save his clan."

The servant's hand trembled as he reached for the stone. Zhao Feng let him take it.

"Wait here."

The gate closed. Zhao Feng counted his breaths. Eighty-seven of them passed before the gate opened again.

"Follow me. Don't touch anything."

The interior of the Xu mansion was a study in decay. Faded tapestries hung on walls that hadn't seen fresh paint in years. Dust gathered in corners where servants no longer bothered to clean. The furniture was old, expensive once, but now showing the wear of generations.

*The Xu family is bleeding money,* Zhao Feng observed. *Their cultivation resources are depleted. Their trade routes are compromised. They're desperate.*

And desperate people made mistakes.

The servant led him to a study on the second floor. A man sat behind a large desk, his face half-hidden in shadow. He was younger than Zhao Feng had expected—maybe thirty, with sharp features and eyes that held the haunted look of someone who'd seen too many failures.

"Leave us," the man said. The servant bowed and retreated, closing the door behind him.

"I am Xu Ming, head of the Xu family." The man's voice was tired, but there was an edge to it. "You have information about the Lin supply routes. Speak quickly, before I decide you're wasting my time."

Zhao Feng took a seat without being invited. The gesture made Xu Ming's eyes narrow.

"The Lin family receives their spirit herbs from the Southern Wetlands. They use three different caravans, rotating schedules to avoid bandits. Their next shipment arrives in five days, traveling through the Mountain Pass of Falling Leaves." Zhao Feng paused. "But you already know that."

Xu Ming's face went still. "What are you playing at?"

"I'm not playing at anything, clan head Xu. I'm proving that I have access to information your spies have missed. For example, did you know that the Lin family has been using a hidden fourth caravan? Smaller, faster, carrying their most valuable goods?"

The silence stretched. Xu Ming's fingers drummed against his desk.

"How do you know this?"

"Because I saw the manifest. I saw the seals." Zhao Feng leaned forward. "And I know that the Lin family is planning to cut off your supply of qi stones entirely within the next two months. They've already secured agreements with the Song family to divide your territory."

It was a lie. For now. But Zhao Feng had been planting seeds of discord between the families for weeks, small trades that favored one over the other, rumors spread through carefully chosen channels. The Lin family *was* planning something. He just didn't know exactly what yet.

But Xu Ming didn't know that.

The clan head's face drained of color. "That's impossible. The Song family has been our ally for—"

"They've been your ally as long as you were useful." Zhao Feng's voice was cold. "Now that you're dying, they're looking for a new partner. One with more to offer."

Xu Ming stood abruptly, his chair scraping against the floor. He paced to the window, staring out at the dark street below.

"Why are you telling me this? What do you want?"

"Access."

"To what?"

"Your family's storage vaults. Specifically, the section where you keep your sealed cultivation manuals."

Xu Ming spun around, his eyes blazing. "Absolutely not. Those are family heirlooms, priceless treasures passed down for—"

"For three generations," Zhao Feng interrupted. "And none of them have been opened in fifty years because your family no longer has cultivators strong enough to break the seals. They're gathering dust while your clan crumbles."

The words hit like physical blows. Xu Ming's hands clenched into fists.

"Even if I wanted to help you, I couldn't. The seals are set by bloodline. Only a Xu with sufficient cultivation can open them."

"I know." Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small vial. Dark liquid swirled inside, shot through with veins of silver light. "That's why I brought this."

Xu Ming stared at the vial. His breath caught.

"That's... that's Blood Essence Elixir. Grade three. Worth more than this entire mansion."

"Correct. One dose will temporarily boost your cultivation by an entire realm. Enough to break those seals." Zhao Feng set the vial on the desk between them. "In exchange, I want to copy three manuals. No originals leave your possession. I'll bring my own scribes, my own materials. You'll have a guard present the entire time."

"Why three? Why not one?"

"Because I'm building an empire, clan head Xu. And empires aren't built on single bricks."

Xu Ming stared at the vial for a long time. His hand reached out, hovering over it, then pulled back.

"If I accept, and the other families find out—"

"They won't. Not from me." Zhao Feng stood. "You have until dawn to decide. After that, I make other arrangements."

He turned and walked to the door. His hand was on the handle when Xu Ming's voice stopped him.

"Wait."

Zhao Feng didn't turn around.

"The woman. The one with the black jade token." Xu Ming's voice was barely a whisper. "She came to me too. Three weeks ago. Offered to sell me information about the Lin family's weaknesses."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. He turned slowly.

"What did you tell her?"

"I told her no." Xu Ming's face was pale. "Because I recognized the symbol. My father used to trade with the Shadow Market, back when our family still had power. He said they never give anything for free. They always collect. Eventually."

"And?"

"And I think she's still in the city. I think she's watching. I think..." Xu Ming swallowed hard. "I think she's already collected from someone. I just don't know who."

The words hung in the air like poison.

Zhao Feng thought of Wu Chen, celebrating at the Drunken Immortal Tavern. Thought of the iron-bound box delivered to the Xu mansion. Thought of the black jade token pressing into someone's palm.

*The Shadow Market doesn't make small moves,* he reminded himself. *Every transaction is a thread in a larger web.*

"Keep the vial," Zhao Feng said. "Consider it a down payment on our partnership. I'll return at dawn for your answer."

He left without waiting for a response.

Outside, Xiao Chen materialized from the shadows. The boy's face was drawn tight with tension.

"Did you get what you wanted?"

"Part of it." Zhao Feng walked quickly, his mind racing. "I need you to find out everything you can about a woman. Veiled, expensive robes, carries a black jade token with an unknown symbol. She's connected to the Shadow Market."

Xiao Chen's steps faltered. "The Shadow Market? I've heard stories. They say the people who trade there never live to spend their profits."

"The stories are true." Zhao Feng stopped at the corner, looking back toward the Xu mansion. Its windows glowed with dim light, like dying embers. "And I think she's already made her first move in Greenstone City."

"Who's her target?"

Zhao Feng

title: The Price of Shadows
content:
The moon hung low over the merchant district, casting long shadows between the crumbling buildings. Zhao Feng worked by lantern light, his brush moving across the ledger in precise strokes. The numbers told a story no one else could read—a map of power disguised as account balances.

Three weeks had passed since Xiao Chen joined him. The boy had proven himself faster than expected, his street instincts translating into a network of informants that spanned half the city. Every night, he slipped through the cracks of the Eastern Wastes, listening, watching, reporting.

But tonight, he was late.

Zhao Feng set down his brush and rubbed his eyes. The qi stones in their crates pulsed with a faint, ambient energy, a constant reminder of how far he'd come and how far he still had to go. The Immortal Ascension Tournament loomed like a storm on the horizon, and he needed more. More stock. More leverage. More information.

The door creaked.

"You're late," Zhao Feng said without turning around.

Xiao Chen's voice came from the shadows, breathless. "Had to take the long way. Someone's watching the warehouse."

Zhao Feng's hand moved instinctively to the dagger hidden beneath his sleeve. "Who?"

"Don't know yet. Two men, dressed like porters, but they don't move like porters. Too still. Too patient." The boy stepped into the lantern light, his face smudged with dirt, a fresh cut across his cheekbone. "They've been there since sundown."

"Did they see you?"

"No. But they saw someone else."

Zhao Feng turned fully, his eyes narrowing. "Explain."

Xiao Chen pulled a folded piece of paper from his shirt. The edges were singed, the paper brittle. "One of my boys found this stuffed in a crack near the north gate. It's addressed to you."

Zhao Feng took the paper and unfolded it. The handwriting was rough, almost illegible, but the message was clear:

*The wolf knows the sheep's pen. Move your flock or be eaten.*

No signature. No seal.

He read it twice, then held it to the lantern flame. The paper curled and blackened, turning to ash in his palm.

"Who delivered it?" he asked.

"Don't know. My boy found it already there. Could've been there for days."

Zhao Feng paced the length of the warehouse, his mind racing. The message was a warning, but from whom? And who was the wolf? In his past life, he'd made enemies of everyone who mattered. But in this life, he'd been careful. Deliberate. He'd stepped lightly, never leaving footprints.

Except for Wu Chen.

The man had come to the warehouse three times now, each visit more probing than the last. He asked about Zhao Feng's suppliers, his customers, his plans. Zhao Feng had answered with vague pleasantries and deflections, but Wu Chen wasn't stupid. He knew something was happening beneath the surface.

"Xiao Chen, what did you learn about Wu Chen?"

The boy's eyes flickered. "He's not just a merchant. He has connections to the Black Wind Sect."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. The Black Wind Sect. One of the minor cultivation sects that operated in the gray space between legitimacy and crime. They dealt in stolen goods, forbidden techniques, and the occasional assassination. In his past life, he'd used them twice. Both times, he'd regretted it.

"How do you know?"

"I followed one of his men to a meeting. Three men in black robes, faces hidden. They exchanged a pouch—looked like qi stones, but darker. Tainted, maybe." Xiao Chen's voice dropped. "And then they talked about you."

"What did they say?"

"They said you're a problem that needs solving. That you're moving too fast, buying too much. That someone wants to know who's backing you."

Zhao Feng stopped pacing. "Someone wants to know if I'm working for a larger power."

"Yes."

"And what did they conclude?"

Xiao Chen hesitated. "They concluded you're alone. Which makes you either very brave or very stupid."

Zhao Feng allowed himself a thin smile. "Let them think what they want. Have you found the woman yet?"

"The lightning user? No. But I've heard rumors. A girl matching her description was seen near the southern markets three days ago. She bought spirit herbs—the kind used for lightning-attributed cultivation."

"Which herbs?"

"Thunder Lotus root. Stormvine leaves. And a single Azure Lightning Pearl."

Zhao Feng's heart quickened. The Azure Lightning Pearl was rare. Expensive. In his past life, it had been the key component in the technique that won the tournament. But acquiring one required either immense wealth or inside knowledge of the black market.

"Find her," he said. "Before the tournament. Before anyone else does."

Xiao Chen nodded, but his expression remained troubled. "There's something else."

"There's always something else."

"I heard a name tonight. One I didn't expect." The boy's hands clenched at his sides. "Zhao Ming."

The name hit Zhao Feng like a physical blow. His eldest son. In his past life, the first to betray him, the one who'd smiled as he signed the documents that stripped Zhao Feng of everything. The one who'd stood over his father's dying body and said, *"You were always too trusting, Father. Business is war. And in war, everyone dies."*

Zhao Feng's vision swam. The warehouse walls seemed to close in, the lantern light flickering like a dying star.

"What about Zhao Ming?" His voice came out flat, controlled, but inside he was screaming.

"I heard he's in the city. Arrived three days ago. He's been asking questions about a young merchant who's been buying up qi stones. Someone matching your description."

Zhao Feng gripped the edge of the table. The wood groaned under his fingers. *Impossible.* In his past life, Zhao Ming hadn't risen to power for another decade. He'd been a minor figure, a second-rate cultivator living off his father's wealth. How could he be here now, in this life, asking questions about a man who shouldn't exist?

Unless. Unless the butterfly wings of Zhao Feng's actions had already begun to stir the winds of fate.

"Does he know it's me?"

"No. But he's getting close. He's offering rewards for information about the 'mysterious qi stone buyer.'" Xiao Chen's voice hardened. "And he's not the only one. I've seen at least three other factions poking around. The Black Wind Sect. A family named Lin. And someone from the city guard."

Zhao Feng forced himself to breathe. To think. In his past life, he'd built an empire over forty years. He'd learned patience, strategy, the art of the long game. But this was different. This was a race he hadn't anticipated, against opponents who shouldn't exist yet.

"I need to accelerate the timeline," he muttered.

"Boss?"

"Xiao Chen, I need you to take a message to the lightning woman. Tell her I have information about the Immortal Ascension Tournament. Tell her I know the secrets of the lightning technique. Tell her I can help her win."

The boy's eyes widened. "You know where she lives?"

"No. But I know where she'll be. In three days, there's a gathering of rogue cultivators at the Jade Pavilion. The tournament scouts will be there, watching for talent. She'll be there too, trying to find backing." Zhao Feng reached into his pouch and pulled out a small jade token, carved with the image of a thunderbird. "Give her this. Tell her the token is a promise. If she comes to me, I'll give her the method to master the Azure Lightning Pearl."

Xiao Chen took the token, his fingers trembling slightly. "And if she doesn't come?"

"Then we move to plan B. But she'll come. She has to."

The boy tucked the token into his shirt and turned to leave, but Zhao Feng caught his arm.

"One more thing. Watch your back. If Zhao Ming is here, he's not alone. He always traveled with a shadow guard—a cultivator named Yan Huo. Third-grade Qi Condensation, specializes in fire techniques. If you see a man with a burn scar on his left hand, run. Don't fight. Just run."

Xiao Chen's jaw tightened. "I can handle myself."

"I know you can. But Yan Huo kills first and asks questions never. He's not a threat you can fight with cleverness. He's a threat you survive by not being there."

The boy nodded, his dark eyes meeting Zhao Feng's. "I understand."

"Good. Now go. And be careful."

Xiao Chen slipped out into the night, disappearing into the shadows like he'd been born to them. Zhao Feng stood alone in the warehouse, the lantern casting his shadow across the stacked crates of qi stones.

He walked to the far corner, where a smaller crate sat separate from the others. Inside was a single object, wrapped in silk and sealed with wax: the fragment of a jade slip he'd found in a ruin outside the city. The slip contained a cultivation technique, ancient and incomplete, but powerful beyond measure.

In his past life, he'd never had the talent to use it. But he'd known someone who did.

The lightning woman.

He touched the silk wrapping, feeling the faint pulse of energy beneath. The technique was called Stormbreaker's Legacy. It was the key to winning the tournament, to becoming someone worth recruiting, to building the foundation of an empire that would rival the great families.

But it was also a death sentence if the wrong people knew he had it.

Zhao Feng closed the crate and turned back to his ledger. The numbers blurred before his eyes, replaced by the memory of Zhao Ming's face, smiling as he signed away his father's life.

*Not this time,* Zhao Feng thought. *This time, I see the knife before it cuts.*

He picked up his brush and began writing a new letter—not to Xiao Chen, not to the lightning woman, but to an old name he hadn't spoken in decades. A name from his past life, someone who owed him a debt that had never been repaid.

*Brother Chang,* he wrote. *I know you're alive. I know you're in the city. And I know you remember what I did for you in the Eastern Wastes. I need your help. One last time. Come to the old warehouse by the tannery. Bring your knives. Bring your silence. And bring your memory of a man named Zhao Ming.*

He sealed the letter with wax, pressing his thumbprint into the soft surface.

Then he blew out the lantern and waited in the dark.

Somewhere in the city, his son was hunting him. Somewhere, a woman was preparing to change her fate. And somewhere, the first threads of a war were being woven.

Zhao Feng closed his eyes and listened to the silence.

It would not last.

---

The knock came three hours before dawn.

Zhao Feng opened the door to find a figure wrapped in a tattered cloak, face hidden beneath a wide hood. The figure held the jade token in one hand and a lightning-wreathed dagger in the other.

"You're the one who sent this?" The voice was female, sharp, crackling with barely contained energy.

"I am."

"Why should I trust you?"

Zhao Feng stepped aside, revealing the crate behind him. "Because I have something you need. And because I know what you're running from."

The woman's hand tightened on the dagger. "You don't know anything about me."

"I know you stole the Azure Lightning Pearl from the Lin family vault. I know they've been hunting you for six months. And I know you're terrified that if you use

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER_THIRTEEN: A Crack in the Mask

The morning air carried the scent of rain and burning incense.

Zhao Feng stood at the entrance of the Golden Lotus Auction House, watching the wealthy and powerful file past him. Silk robes. Jade ornaments. The subtle glow of cultivation bases that marked men who had never known real hunger.

Three days since the warehouse. Three days since Wu Chen's first visit.

The man had been exactly what Zhao Feng expected—a merchant with hungry eyes and cautious hands. He'd inspected the qi stones, tested their purity with a small talisman, and nodded with grudging approval.

"Acceptable quality," Wu Chen had said. "But your prices are too high. The market standard is fifteen spirit coins per stone. You're asking twenty."

"I'm not selling to the market," Zhao Feng had replied. "I'm selling to you. And you're not a regular buyer—you're a man who needs bulk shipments without questions."

Wu Chen's eyes had narrowed. "That's a dangerous accusation."

"It's not an accusation. It's an observation." Zhao Feng had spread his hands. "You came alone. No guards. No apprentices. You paid in unmarked coins. You're not with any of the major families. That means you're either a rogue cultivator with too much money, or you're someone who doesn't want to be traced."

"And if I'm neither?"

"Then you're wasting both our time."

Wu Chen had laughed then—a dry, humorless sound. "You're young. Too young to be this sharp." He'd reached into his sleeve and produced a list. "I need three hundred stones. Can you deliver?"

Zhao Feng had taken the list without looking at it. "In one week."

"Impossible. No one in this city has that kind of stock."

"I don't have it yet. But I will."

Wu Chen had studied him for a long moment. "You're either brilliant or a fool. I'll find out which in seven days." Then he'd left, his footsteps echoing through the empty warehouse.

That was three days ago.

Now, Zhao Feng stood outside the Golden Lotus, watching the auction house prepare for its monthly event. Inside, cultivators would bid on techniques, weapons, and rare ingredients. The prices would be astronomical. The competition would be fierce.

And Zhao Feng would walk away with a fortune.

But first, he needed to find her.

---

The outer disciple quarters were a collection of rundown buildings on the eastern edge of the sect grounds. Zhao Feng had never been here in his past life—he'd been too important, too busy building his empire to notice the nobodies who scraped by on scraps.

Now, he walked through the muddy paths, ignoring the curious glances from the disciples who recognized him as the merchant who'd caused such trouble in the market square.

They whispered as he passed. *That's him. The one who humiliated the Lin family. They say he's nothing but a nobody, but he fights like a demon.*

Let them talk.

He found her on the training grounds, practicing alone.

She was young—maybe seventeen—with calloused hands and a determined set to her jaw. Her robes were patched. Her sword was chipped. But when she moved, lightning crackled around her fingers.

*Lin Xue.*

In his past life, she'd been the champion of the Immortal Ascension Tournament. The nobody who'd shocked the world. The woman the major families had fought over like dogs over a bone.

And then she'd disappeared.

Zhao Feng had never learned what happened to her. The rumors said she'd been recruited by a hidden sect. Others said she'd been killed for refusing to share her techniques. But in the end, she'd vanished from history like smoke in the wind.

This time, he would make sure she didn't vanish alone.

He approached slowly, keeping his hands visible. "Impressive."

Lin Xue spun, her sword raised. Lightning danced along the blade. "Who are you? What do you want?"

"I'm a merchant. I saw your technique and wanted to compliment you."

"Merchants don't come to outer disciple training grounds." Her eyes narrowed. "They send servants if they want to buy something."

"Normally, yes. But I'm not a normal merchant." Zhao Feng stopped ten feet away. "I saw you at the tournament last month. You placed third in the lightning division."

"Fourth."

"My mistake. Fourth." He smiled. "But you should have placed first."

Lin Xue's grip on her sword tightened. "What do you want?"

"I want to offer you a deal."

"I don't make deals with strangers."

"Then let me introduce myself properly." Zhao Feng bowed, formal and respectful. "My name is Zhao Feng. I'm a merchant, as I said. But I'm also someone who recognizes talent when he sees it."

"And what talent do you think you see?"

"The talent to become the strongest cultivator in this generation." He held her gaze. "If you know where to find the right techniques."

Lin Xue's expression flickered. For just a moment, he saw the hunger beneath her calm exterior. The desperation of someone who knew they had potential but couldn't afford to unlock it.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Of course you don't." Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and produced a small jade slip. "But this might help. It's a map. To a place where the old lightning techniques are kept."

Lin Xue stared at the slip. "Why would you give me this?"

"Because I need you to trust me. And because when you reach that place, you'll find something else—a sealed box. Inside is a technique manual. I want you to bring it to me."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you keep training with your chipped sword and patched robes, and you stay in the outer disciples until you're too old to matter." Zhao Feng tossed the slip to her feet. "Or you take a chance. The choice is yours."

He turned and walked away, leaving her standing in the mud with the jade slip at her feet.

She would take it. He knew she would. Because she was exactly like him—someone who would never accept the life she'd been given.

---

The auction began at dusk.

The Golden Lotus Auction House was packed with cultivators from all over the province. Major families. Independent cultivators. Sect representatives in their distinctive robes.

Zhao Feng sat in the back, watching.

The first few items were unremarkable—low-grade weapons, common elixirs, a few spirit beasts that had been captured alive. The bidding was fierce but predictable.

Then came the main event.

"Ladies and gentlemen," the auctioneer announced, his voice ringing through the hall, "we have a special item tonight. A technique manual from the lost era of the Thunder Emperor. Grade: Heaven."

The crowd erupted.

Zhao Feng leaned forward, his heart beating faster. This was it. The technique that had made Lin Xue a legend. The technique that would change everything.

But as the auctioneer unveiled the manual, a voice cut through the noise.

"Stop the auction."

A man stood up from the front row. He was tall, handsome, dressed in robes of pure white silk. His cultivation base radiated power—late Foundation Establishment, at least.

And Zhao Feng recognized him.

*Lin Hao.*

His second son. The one who had stolen his shipping empire. The one who had smiled as he watched Zhao Feng bleed out in the gutter.

"You can't sell this manual," Lin Hao said, his voice carrying through the silent hall. "It belongs to my family. It was stolen from our vaults three days ago."

The auctioneer's face went pale. "Young Master Lin, I assure you, we verified the provenance—"

"You verified nothing." Lin Hao turned, scanning the crowd. "The thief is here tonight. I can feel his qi on the manual."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold.

He hadn't touched the manual. He'd never even seen it in person. But somehow, impossibly, Lin Hao had tracked it here.

And if Lin Hao could sense the thief's qi, he could sense anyone who had been near the thief.

Anyone who had touched a jade slip connected to the manual.

*Lin Xue.*

Zhao Feng stood up slowly, keeping his face neutral. He needed to get out. He needed to warn her.

But Lin Hao's eyes found him.

"You." The young master's voice was sharp. "I know you. You're the merchant who's been causing trouble in the market."

The crowd turned to stare at Zhao Feng.

"I'm just a spectator," Zhao Feng said calmly. "I have no interest in your family's quarrels."

"No?" Lin Hao smiled, cold and cruel. "Then why does my technique manual smell of your qi?"

The words hit like a thunderbolt.

Zhao Feng's mind raced. It was impossible. He'd never touched the manual. He'd never even been near it.

Unless...

*The jade slip.*

He'd handled the jade slip before giving it to Lin Xue. And if the slip had come from the same vault as the manual, if the thief had stored them together...

Then Lin Hao wasn't tracking the manual's qi.

He was tracking the slip's.

And Zhao Feng had just given it to the one person who could lead him to the truth.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Zhao Feng said, keeping his voice steady. "I've never seen that manual in my life."

"Then you won't mind if I search your storage rings."

The crowd murmured. Searching a cultivator's storage ring was the highest insult. It implied theft, dishonor, a complete lack of trust.

But Zhao Feng had no choice.

He held out his hand, palm open. "Search all you want. You'll find nothing."

Lin Hao approached, his eyes gleaming with triumph. He reached out, his fingers brushing against Zhao Feng's ring—

And then the world exploded.

A bolt of lightning crashed through the roof of the auction house, striking the stage where the manual sat. The auctioneer screamed. The crowd scattered.

And in the chaos, a figure descended from the hole in the ceiling.

Lin Xue.
