Her eyes blazed with lightning. Her sword crackled with power.

"Zhao Feng!" she shouted. "Run!"

And then she threw something at him—a small, sealed box.

He caught it instinctively.

The moment his fingers touched the box, Lin Hao's head snapped toward him. The young master's face twisted with fury.

"*Thief!*"

The guards surged forward.

Zhao Feng didn't wait.

He turned and ran, the box clutched to his chest, as Lin Xue's lightning lit up the auction house behind him.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Debt Collector's Secret

The night air carried the stench of dying fish and cheap wine. Zhao Feng stood in the shadows of an abandoned pier, watching the man who would change everything.

Wu Chen arrived exactly at midnight, as Zhao Feng had expected. The merchant moved with the practiced caution of someone who knew the docks after dark—quick steps, darting eyes, one hand never leaving the pouch at his belt. He was alone, which meant he either trusted Zhao Feng completely or planned to kill him.

Neither option was true.

Zhao Feng stepped out of the shadows. "You're early."

Wu Chen startled, then forced a laugh. "And you're late, young Zhao. I was beginning to think you'd changed your mind about our arrangement."

"I don't change my mind." Zhao Feng gestured toward the warehouse. "Shall we?"

They walked in silence. The moon hung low and yellow, casting sickly light across the cobblestones. Wu Chen's footsteps were too loud, his breathing too quick. He was nervous. Good.

Inside the warehouse, Zhao Feng lit three lamps, bathing the room in warm light. His qi stones sat in neat stacks, polished and sorted by quality. The spirit grass hung from the rafters, drying in bundles. Everything looked legitimate, professional, above board.

Wu Chen whistled low. "You've been busy, young Zhao. Last I heard, you were selling trinkets out of a cart."

"People underestimate what a cart can do." Zhao Feng poured two cups of tea from a pot he'd prepared earlier. "You wanted to discuss a partnership."

"I did." Wu Chen accepted the cup but didn't drink. "My sources tell me you're stockpiling for the Immortal Ascension Tournament. Smart move. Prices will triple once the outer disciples start panicking."

"The tournament is six months away. I'm planning for the long term."

"Long term." Wu Chen's smile thinned. "Funny you should say that. I've been thinking about the long term too. Specifically, about who's going to win."

Zhao Feng kept his face neutral. "Anyone's game."

"Anyone except the favorites." Wu Chen set down his cup without drinking. "The major families have already chosen their champions. The Lius are backing a second-stage Qi Condensation cultivator. The Wangs have a spear prodigy. And the Zhangs..." He paused. "The Zhangs have found someone interesting. A girl, barely seventeen, with lightning techniques no one's ever seen."

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

He remembered that girl. In his past life, she'd emerged from nowhere, a nobody from the outer disciples who'd shocked the entire sect. The major families had fought over her, offering fortunes and marriage alliances. She'd chosen the Zhangs in the end, and they'd used her to dominate the spirit stone market for a decade.

But that was supposed to happen three months from now. Not six.

"How do you know about her?" Zhao Feng asked.

Wu Chen's smile widened. "Because I'm the one who found her."

The words hit like a physical blow. Zhao Feng gripped his teacup, forcing his hands to stay still. In his past life, no one had known where the lightning girl came from. She'd simply appeared, won the tournament, and disappeared into the Zhang family's compound. The mystery of her origins had been a topic of speculation for years.

If Wu Chen had found her first, then everything Zhao Feng knew about the timeline was wrong.

"You're lying," he said flatly.

"I never lie about money, young Zhao. It's bad for business." Wu Chen reached into his robe and pulled out a folded piece of paper. "This is a contract. I've already secured exclusive rights to her cultivation materials. The girl needs high-grade lightning-attuned spirit stones to advance. Lots of them."

Zhao Feng took the paper. His eyes scanned the characters, his heart sinking with each line. It was real. Notarized. Signed with the girl's blood seal.

"Where did you find her?"

"Does it matter? What matters is that I have a cultivator who's going to win the tournament, and she needs supplies. You have supplies. I'm offering you first refusal on all her purchases, in exchange for thirty percent of your profits for the next five years."

Thirty percent. The bastard was trying to own him.

"Thirty percent is too high," Zhao Feng said, buying time while his mind raced. "Fifteen, and I'll match whatever price the other merchants offer."

"Twenty-five."

"Twenty, and you give me exclusive rights to supply her until the tournament."

Wu Chen's eyes glittered. "Deal."

They shook hands. Zhao Feng felt the grip of a man who thought he'd won. And maybe he had, on the surface. Twenty percent of his profits for five years was steep. But it gave him access to the lightning girl, and access meant information. Information meant leverage.

Wu Chen left with a copy of the contract and a promise to return next week with the first order. Zhao Feng watched him disappear into the night, then turned and punched the wall.

The wood splintered. Blood dripped from his knuckles.

Something was wrong. In his past life, the lightning girl hadn't been discovered until three months before the tournament. Wu Chen had been a minor merchant who'd died in a plague two years from now. None of this matched.

Which meant either his memories were flawed, or someone else had changed the past.

Zhao Feng forced himself to breathe. Panic was a luxury he couldn't afford. He needed information, and he needed it now.

He cleaned his wound, wrapped it in cloth, and left the warehouse. The streets were empty, the moon hidden behind clouds. He walked quickly, his mind turning over possibilities.

The lightning girl's early discovery could be coincidence. Butterfly effects from his own actions. But Wu Chen's involvement didn't fit. The man was a third-rate merchant who dealt in stolen goods and loan sharking. He didn't have the connections to find a hidden cultivation prodigy.

Unless someone had pointed him in the right direction.

The temple loomed ahead, its broken roof silhouetted against the night sky. Xiao Chen was supposed to be there, watching Wu Chen's movements. But as Zhao Feng approached, he saw the boy wasn't alone.

A figure stood in the temple doorway, tall and thin, wrapped in a grey cloak. Xiao Chen was beside them, his posture tense, one hand gripping a knife he'd pulled from somewhere.

Zhao Feng slowed his pace. "Xiao Chen. Who's your friend?"

The figure turned. A woman's face emerged from the shadows—sharp cheekbones, dark eyes, a scar running from her temple to her jaw. She looked young, maybe twenty-five, but her gaze held the weight of someone who'd seen too much.

"You must be Zhao Feng," she said. Her voice was rough, like stones grinding together. "I've been looking for you."

"Have you now." Zhao Feng stopped ten feet away, keeping his hands visible. "And why is that?"

"I have information about Wu Chen." She stepped forward, and Xiao Chen moved to block her. She smiled, a cold expression that didn't reach her eyes. "Information he'd kill to keep hidden."

"I'm listening."

"Not here. Too many ears." She glanced at Xiao Chen. "Your watchdog is loyal. Keep him. But we talk alone."

Zhao Feng considered his options. She could be a trap, sent by Wu Chen to test him. She could be a rival merchant trying to poison his partnership. She could be anyone.

But she knew Wu Chen's name, and she'd found him at the temple—a location he'd only visited twice. That took resources. Resources meant she was either very powerful or very desperate.

"I'll meet you at the Serpent's Tail," he said. "Tomorrow, an hour before sunset."

"Make it midnight. I don't like sunlight."

"Midnight, then."

She nodded once, then melted back into the shadows. Xiao Chen waited until she was gone before lowering his knife.

"Who was that?" the boy asked, his voice tight.

"I don't know yet." Zhao Feng stared at the empty doorway. "But I'm going to find out."

---

The Serpent's Tail was a teahouse that served as neutral ground for the city's underground. Criminals and merchants alike gathered there to trade information, settle disputes, and drink tea that cost more than most people made in a month. Zhao Feng had used it in his past life, back when he was building his first empire.

He arrived at midnight, dressed in plain robes, his face partially hidden by a hood. The woman was already there, sitting in a corner booth, a pot of tea steaming before her.

She didn't look up as he sat down. "You came alone."

"So did you."

"I always do." She poured him a cup. "Drink. It's not poisoned."

Zhao Feng didn't drink. "You said you had information about Wu Chen."

"I do." She leaned back, studying him with those dark eyes. "But before I give it to you, I need to know something. Why are you really in this city?"

"Business."

"Everyone's in this city for business. But most people don't buy warehouses and stockpile qi stones six months before a tournament they have no chance of winning." She smiled again, that cold expression. "Unless they know something they shouldn't."

Zhao Feng's pulse quickened. "What are you implying?"

"I'm not implying anything. I'm stating a fact." She reached into her cloak and pulled out a small jade token, placing it on the table between them. "Do you know what this is?"

He did. He'd seen it before, in his past life, when he'd been powerful enough to move among the city's true rulers. It was a merchant guild token, but not an ordinary one. The symbol carved into its surface—a coiled serpent eating its own tail—marked it as belonging to the Black Scale Society.

The underground organization that controlled every major trade route in the eastern provinces.

"Where did you get that?" he asked, keeping his voice level.

"Does it matter?" She picked up the token, turning it over in her fingers. "What matters is that Wu Chen stole it. Stole it from the Society's vault, along with a list of every merchant who owes them favors. He's been using that list to blackmail people, build his own power base."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I want it back." Her eyes locked onto his. "And I think you're the only one who can get it for me."

Zhao Feng laughed. It was a harsh sound, bitter. "You want me to steal from a man I just signed a partnership with?"

"I want you to destroy him." She set the token down again. "The Society has been looking for Wu Chen for months. He's been careful, covering his tracks. But he made a mistake when he approached you."

"What mistake?"

"He doesn't know who you really are." She leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. "But I do, Zhao Feng. I know you were reborn. I know you remember your past life. And I know you're planning to destroy the Zhang family."

The world stopped.

No one knew that. No one could know that. He'd told no one, left no clues, changed nothing that would reveal his secret.

"How—"

"I was there when you died." Her voice cracked, just slightly. "I was the one who held your hand as you took your last breath. You don't remember me, because you were half-dead by the time I found you, but I remember you."

Zhao Feng stared at her, his mind blank. The scar on her face. The rough voice. The way she moved, like someone who'd survived things no one should survive.

"I'm sorry I didn't

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Serpent in the Garden

The knock came at midnight.

Zhao Feng looked up from his ledger, a faint smile playing at his lips. He'd been expecting this. The lantern had burned low, casting dancing shadows across the crates of qi stones. Outside, the streets were silent except for the occasional dog barking in the distance.

He set down his brush and walked to the door. Three locks disengaged in sequence, each one a simple mechanism he'd installed himself. Not enough to stop a determined cultivator, but enough to buy him time.

The door swung open to reveal a man in dark robes. Mid-thirties. Lean face. Eyes that moved constantly, never settling on anything for more than a heartbeat. A scar ran from his left temple to his jaw, pulling the corner of his mouth into a permanent sneer.

"Wu Chen," Zhao Feng said. "Right on time."

Wu Chen didn't enter immediately. His gaze swept the warehouse interior, cataloging every crate, every bundle, every possible escape route. Professional habit. Zhao Feng recognized it because he'd cultivated the same instinct over forty years of business negotiations.

"You know who I am," Wu Chen said. It wasn't a question.

"I know you work for the Black Stone Consortium. I know you've been watching my warehouse for three days. I know you reported to your superiors yesterday afternoon that I was a small-time operator not worth their attention."

Wu Chen's eyes finally stopped moving. They fixed on Zhao Feng with a coldness that would have made lesser men step back.

"You knew I was watching."

"I counted on it."

A long silence stretched between them. Then Wu Chen laughed—a dry, rasping sound that held no humor.

"Interesting. Most merchants don't welcome watchers from the Consortium into their warehouses."

"Most merchants don't have anything worth protecting." Zhao Feng stepped aside, gesturing toward the interior. "Come in. We have things to discuss."

Wu Chen didn't move. "I prefer to conduct business in open spaces. Easier to see threats coming."

"Then you've been dealing with the wrong people." Zhao Feng walked back to his table and sat down. "The best deals happen in private, where neither party has an audience to perform for. But suit yourself. We can shout across the street if you prefer."

Another long pause. Then Wu Chen stepped through the doorway, closing the door behind him.

He didn't sit. He stood near the entrance, one hand resting casually on his belt where a dagger hung. His eyes continued to move, now cataloging the interior at close range.

"I've been sent to deliver a message," Wu Chen said. "The Black Stone Consortium has noticed your recent activities. The qi stone purchases. The spirit grass acquisitions. The discreet inquiries about storage space in the inner city."

"I'm flattered the Consortium pays attention to small-time operators."

"We pay attention to anything that might affect the market." Wu Chen's scarred face tightened. "You've bought seventeen thousand spirit coins worth of qi stones in the past month. That's not small-time."

Close. He'd bought nineteen thousand, but he'd deliberately let three thousand slip through unofficial channels to muddy the numbers. Good. His deception was working.

"Seventeen thousand," Zhao Feng repeated, as if considering the number. "That's less than what the Consortium moves in a single day."

"Correct. But you're not the Consortium. You're a nobody from the outer district who appeared six months ago with nothing and has somehow accumulated enough capital to start cornering the low-grade market."

"Cornering is a strong word."

"It's the word my superiors used." Wu Chen took a step closer. "They want to know what your angle is. No merchant operates like this without a patron. Who's backing you?"

Now we come to it.

Zhao Feng leaned back in his chair, folding his hands. "No one. I'm self-funded."

"Impossible. You'd need at least fifty thousand spirit coins to establish the supply chain you're building. No one accumulates that kind of capital without connections."

"Then perhaps I'm simply better at business than your superiors."

Wu Chen's hand tightened on his dagger. "You're playing a dangerous game, merchant."

"I'm playing the only game that matters." Zhao Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a folded piece of paper. He slid it across the table. "Take this to your superiors. Tell them it's a proposal."

Wu Chen didn't touch the paper. "What kind of proposal?"

"The kind that makes everyone rich." Zhao Feng stood up, moving around the table. "I know the Consortium controls sixty percent of the qi stone trade in the capital. I know you've been losing market share to the Azure Lotus Sect's merchant arm. I know your profit margins have dropped twelve percent in the last quarter."

Wu Chen's face betrayed nothing, but his eyes flickered.

"My proposal is simple," Zhao Feng continued. "I want to partner with the Consortium. Not as a subordinate. As an equal partner. I bring the strategy, the supply chain innovations, and access to markets you can't reach. You bring distribution, protection, and the political cover I need to operate."

"You're insane."

"Am I?" Zhao Feng stopped three feet from Wu Chen, close enough to see the micro-expressions flickering across the scarred face. "Tell me—how much does the Consortium spend on tribute to the city guard every month?"

"That's internal information."

"Thirty thousand spirit coins. Minimum. More during festival seasons. And how much do you lose to bandit attacks on your supply routes?"

Silence.

"Eighteen percent of your annual shipments," Zhao Feng answered for him. "I know because I've been studying your operations for two months. I know where your weaknesses are. I know how to fix them. And I know your competitors are about to make a move that will cost you a third of your market share within the year."

Wu Chen's hand finally left his dagger. He picked up the paper, unfolded it, and began to read.

Zhao Feng watched his eyes move across the characters. He'd written the proposal carefully, laying out his analysis of the Consortium's vulnerabilities without revealing his own sources. He'd included a timeline for the Azure Lotus Sect's planned expansion. He'd outlined a counter-strategy that would not only protect the Consortium's market share but increase it.

And hidden in the fine print, buried deep in the appendices, was a clause that would give him control of the Consortium's low-grade qi stone supply within eighteen months.

Wu Chen read for a long time. When he finished, he folded the paper and tucked it into his robe.

"I'll deliver this to my superiors."

"I know you will."

"But I should tell you something." Wu Chen's scarred face twisted into something that might have been a smile. "The Consortium doesn't trust outsiders. They'll study your proposal, pick it apart, and use your ideas without giving you credit. You'll get nothing for your trouble."

"I'm aware."

"Then why bother?"

Zhao Feng smiled. "Because I'm not giving them anything they couldn't figure out on their own. The real value isn't in the proposal. The real value is in me."

Wu Chen studied him for a moment, then shook his head. "You're either the most confident merchant I've ever met, or the most foolish."

"Both. It's the only way to survive in this city."

Wu Chen turned and walked to the door. He paused with his hand on the latch. "One more thing. There's a rumor going around the underworld. Someone's been asking questions about the Immortal Ascension Tournament. Specifically about the lightning technique that appeared three years ago."

Zhao Feng's heart rate didn't change. His expression remained calm. But something cold settled in his chest.

"Is that so?"

"Word is, someone's trying to find the cultivator who developed that technique. The one who disappeared after the tournament." Wu Chen looked back over his shoulder. "If I were you, I'd stay away from that line of inquiry. Some secrets are worth dying for."

"I'll keep that in mind."

Wu Chen left. The door closed, and the locks engaged one by one.

Zhao Feng stood in the darkness, his mind racing.

Someone else was looking for the lightning cultivator.

In his past life, she had remained hidden for another eight years before emerging to become the strongest cultivator of her generation. But now, someone was searching for her. Someone who knew she existed.

He'd thought he had time. Six months until the tournament, plenty of time to build his wealth, establish his network, and find her before the major families did.

But if someone else was already searching—

A shadow moved at the edge of his vision.

Zhao Feng spun, his hand reaching for the dagger hidden in his sleeve.

Xiao Chen stood in the corner of the warehouse, barely visible in the darkness. The boy's face was pale, his eyes wide.

"How long have you been there?" Zhao Feng demanded.

"Since before he arrived." Xiao Chen's voice was barely a whisper. "I followed him from the consortium headquarters. Watched him approach the warehouse. Climbed in through the roof hole before he knocked."

Zhao Feng lowered his hand. "You should have announced yourself."

"I wanted to see what he'd do. If he tried to hurt you, I was going to drop on him from above." The boy's face hardened. "He's dangerous. I watched him break a man's arm earlier today just for looking at him wrong."

"I know."

"You still let him in here."

"I needed him to deliver that proposal. It's the first step in a much larger plan." Zhao Feng walked back to his table and sat down. "Did you hear everything?"

"Yes."

"Good. Then you know someone else is looking for the lightning cultivator."

Xiao Chen nodded slowly. "What are you going to do?"

Zhao Feng picked up his brush and dipped it in ink. "I'm going to find her first."

"How? If the consortium is already searching—"

"The consortium is searching blindly. They know she exists, but they don't know where she came from. I do." Zhao Feng began writing in his ledger, his brush moving with practiced precision. "In three days, I'm going to the outer village where she grew up. I'm going to find her family. I'm going to make them an offer they can't refuse."

"And if someone else gets there first?"

Zhao Feng's brush paused. He looked up at the boy, and for a moment, the mask slipped. Xiao Chen saw something in those eyes that made him take a step back.

"Then I'll find out who they are," Zhao Feng said, his voice flat. "And I'll make sure they never search for anything again."

Xiao Chen swallowed. "You're not like other merchants."

"No," Zhao Feng agreed, returning to his writing. "I'm not."

The lantern flickered, casting long shadows across the warehouse walls. Outside, the night deepened, and somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled.

The hunt had begun.

title: The Looming Shadow
content:
The candle sputtered, casting dancing shadows across the warehouse walls. Zhao Feng set down his brush, the ink still wet on the ledger page.

*Mount Hua Sect. Outer Disciple Recruitment. Three months.*

He'd almost missed it. In his past life, the Immortal Ascension Tournament had consumed his attention so completely that he'd overlooked the smaller opportunities sprouting around it. But now, with fresh eyes and a clearer purpose, he saw the pattern beneath the surface.

The Mount Hua Sect didn't recruit often. Once every decade, maybe twice if they lost disciples to the wilderness or demon beasts. Their standards were brutal—strength, talent, and lineage all mattered. But they also valued something else: resources.

Every new disciple needed qi stones, spirit herbs, and cultivation aids. The sect provided the basics, but the truly ambitious bought their advantages. And where there was buying, there was selling.

Zhao Feng dipped his brush again. *Supply chain. Low-grade qi stones at market price, medium-grade at a ten percent premium. Spirit grass bundles, bulk discount for orders over fifty.*

The numbers danced in his head, familiar as old friends. If he could secure the contract to supply the new disciples, he'd triple his capital in two months. More importantly, he'd gain access to the sect's inner marketplace—a closed economy where cultivators traded secrets alongside spirit coins.

A knock at the door broke his concentration.

Three raps. A pause. Two more.

Xiao Chen's signal.

"Come in."

The boy slipped through the crack, moving with the practiced silence of someone who'd spent years avoiding notice. His eyes darted around the warehouse, cataloging exits and hiding spots before settling on Zhao Feng.

"He's meeting someone tomorrow night," Xiao Chen said without preamble. "Wu Chen. At the Drunken Crane Tavern, back room, midnight."

Zhao Feng set down his brush. "Who's the someone?"

"Don't know yet. But he's been nervous all week. Checking over his shoulder. Buying better wine than usual." The boy's thin fingers twitched at his sides. "Bad nervous, not good nervous."

*Bad nervous.* That meant fear, not anticipation. Someone had put pressure on Wu Chen, and pressure made men desperate. Desperate men made mistakes.

"Can you get inside the tavern?"

Xiao Chen's lip curled. "I can get inside anything that's not nailed shut."

"Then be there. Watch through the crack in the door. I want to know everything—who he meets, what they say, how they look at each other."

The boy nodded and turned to leave.

"Xiao Chen."

He stopped.

"Be careful. If you get caught, I can't protect you. Not yet."

For a moment, something flickered in the boy's dark eyes. Gratitude? Fear? It was gone before Zhao Feng could name it.

"I know how to run," Xiao Chen said, and disappeared into the night.

---

The Drunken Crane Tavern squatted in the center of the merchant district like a fat toad. Its windows were grimy, its sign hung crooked, and the smell of cheap wine and cheaper meat drifted through its cracks. But the back rooms were private, the owner asked no questions, and the walls were thick enough to swallow secrets.

Zhao Feng arrived early, taking a seat at the far end of the common room. He ordered a cup of tea—bad tea, bitter and thin—and settled in to wait.

The tavern filled slowly. Merchants, laborers, the occasional cultivator in patched robes. No one looked at him twice. That was the advantage of being forgettable. In his past life, he'd cultivated an aura of power that made men bow before they knew his name. This time, he embraced invisibility.

Midnight came and went.

At half past, the door to the back room opened. A man stepped out—tall, broad-shouldered, wearing the blue-gray robes of a minor sect messenger. His qi was steady, mid-level Foundation Establishment. Strong enough to crush a mortal without thinking.

Zhao Feng's tea cup froze halfway to his lips.

He knew that face.

In his past life, that man had been a nothing—a messenger who delivered contracts and collected payments. But in this life, that man was something else entirely.

He was the right hand of the Lin family.

The Lin family. The first house to betray Zhao Feng when his fortune crumbled. They'd smiled to his face, called him brother, then stolen his shipping routes and left him bleeding in the mud.

And here was one of their agents, meeting with Wu Chen in a back room at midnight.

Zhao Feng set down his cup. His hands were steady. His heart, however, beat a cold, measured rhythm.

*They're already moving.*

He hadn't even built his empire yet, and the pieces were sliding into place against him. But that was impossible. The Lin family had no reason to notice him—he was a nobody, a street merchant selling qi stones from a leaking warehouse.

Unless someone had told them.

Wu Chen emerged moments later, his face pale, his hands trembling slightly. He didn't look at anyone. He walked straight to the bar, ordered a bottle of the strongest spirit, and drank it in three gulps.

*Bad nervous,* Xiao Chen had said.

*No,* Zhao Feng thought. *Terrified.*

He finished his tea, left a few copper coins on the table, and slipped out into the night. The streets were empty, the moon hidden behind clouds. He walked quickly, his mind racing.

The Lin family. They were fishing, casting lines into the dark waters of the merchant district, looking for something. But what? And why Wu Chen?

He reached his warehouse and stopped.

The lock was broken.

Not forced—broken, as if someone had simply reached out and crushed it with qi-enhanced strength. The door hung slightly ajar, darkness pooling in the gap.

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

He'd left the ledger on the table. The ledger with all his contacts, his plans, his calculations. If someone had taken it—

He pushed the door open.

The warehouse was empty.

Not ransacked. Not looted. *Empty.* The crates of qi stones were gone. The stacks of spirit grass had vanished. The scale, the mortar, the pestle—all disappeared. Even the table was missing.

Only his ledger remained, sitting in the center of the floor, open to a page he hadn't written.

A single message, written in bold, elegant script:

*We know who you are, Zhao Feng.*

*Come to the Lin Estate tomorrow at sunset.*

*Alone.*

*Or next time, we take more than your merchandise.*

Zhao Feng stared at the words. His reflection stared back from the ink, a young man with old eyes and a cold heart.

*How?*

He'd been careful. He'd hidden his tracks, buried his identity, moved through shadows. No one should have connected the street merchant to the man who'd once ruled the underworld of commerce.

But the Lins knew. They'd found him.

And they'd left a message, not a blade. That meant they didn't want him dead. Not yet. They wanted something else.

He picked up the ledger, closed it, and tucked it into his robes. His mind was already spinning, weaving contingencies, calculating odds.

The Lins had resources, cultivators, and connections. He had a street boy, a burned temple, and forty years of bitter knowledge.

Unequal odds. But he'd faced worse.

He walked to the back of the warehouse, where a loose floorboard hid a small compartment. Inside lay a single item—a jade token, no bigger than his thumb, carved with the symbol of a coiled serpent.

He'd found it years ago, in his past life, buried in the ruins of an ancient temple. He'd never figured out what it did, but he'd kept it close. In this life, he'd retrieved it first, hidden it away for a moment like this.

The token hummed against his fingers, warm and alive.

*Not yet,* he thought. *Not unless I have to.*

He tucked the token into his sleeve and left the empty warehouse. The night air was cold, carrying the smell of rain. Somewhere in the distance, a dog barked. The city slept, unaware of the storm gathering in its heart.

Zhao Feng walked toward the burned temple, his footsteps steady, his eyes fixed on the darkness ahead.

The Lins thought they had him cornered. Thought they could summon him like a servant, demand his secrets, break him to their will.

They didn't know who they were dealing with.

They didn't know the wolf had teeth.

---

Xiao Chen found him at the temple, sitting cross-legged in the moonlight, the jade token resting in his palm.

"The messenger," the boy said, breathless. "Lin family. I followed him to their estate. They've got guards everywhere. Cultivators. Five, maybe six."

"Any formations?"

"Couldn't tell. But their walls have marks. Symbols. I didn't recognize them."

Zhao Feng nodded. "You did well."

"What now?" Xiao Chen's voice was tight. "They know where you live. They took everything."

"Now we adapt." Zhao Feng stood, brushing dust from his robes. "They made a mistake tonight."

"What mistake? They stole your whole stock!"

"They left me alive."

The boy's brow furrowed. "That's not a mistake. That's a threat."

"No." Zhao Feng's smile was thin and sharp. "A threat is a warning. A mistake is an opportunity. They could have killed me. They didn't. That means they want something they can only get from a living man."

"What could they want?"

Zhao Feng looked down at the jade token. It pulsed once, warm and insistent, as if answering a question he hadn't asked.

"Tomorrow," he said, "we find out."

The wind picked up, rattling through the temple's broken walls. Somewhere in the darkness, a night bird called, its cry sharp and urgent.

Xiao Chen watched Zhao Feng's face, searching for fear, for doubt. He found neither.

"Are you really going to the Lin Estate?" the boy asked.

"Yes."

"Alone?"

Zhao Feng's smile widened, cold and predatory.

"I said I'd go alone. I didn't say I'd go unarmed."

He closed his fist around the jade token. For a moment, the air around him shimmered, and Xiao Chen could have sworn he saw something move in the shadows behind his master—something old, something hungry, something that had been waiting a very long time to be unleashed.

Then the moment passed, and Zhao Feng was just a young man in worn robes, standing in the ruins of a temple, staring at a future only he could see.

"Get some rest," he said. "Tomorrow's going to be a long day."

He walked into the darkness, and the darkness welcomed him like an old friend.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Debt Collector's Secret

The lantern cast long shadows across the warehouse as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger's columns. Ten thousand spirit coins. A respectable start, but nowhere near sufficient. The Immortal Ascension Tournament would arrive in six months, and with it, an opportunity that would never come again.

A knock at the door interrupted his calculations.

Three rapid taps, a pause, then two more. The signal he'd arranged with Xiao Chen.

Zhao Feng crossed the room and slid the bolt aside. The boy slipped through the opening like smoke, his thin frame barely disturbing the air. His eyes were wide, his breathing shallow.

"He's not coming tonight," Xiao Chen said without preamble. "Wu Chen. He's meeting with someone else. A woman."

Zhao Feng's hand froze on the door bolt. "Where?"

"The Lotus Pavilion. Back room. I followed him from his teahouse after he changed his route. He kept looking over his shoulder. He's nervous about something."

Nervous. Wu Chen was a mid-level merchant who specialized in medicinal herbs. He'd approached Zhao Feng three days ago, offering to buy his entire stock of spirit grass at double the market rate. The deal seemed too good to be true—which meant it almost certainly was.

"Did you see the woman's face?"

Xiao Chen shook his head. "She wore a veil. But she moved like a cultivator. Light steps. No wasted motion. And she had a sword—short blade, worn at her hip like she knew how to use it."

A cultivator meeting with a herb merchant in secret. The pieces clicked together in Zhao Feng's mind like tumblers in a lock. Someone was trying to corner the spirit grass market before the tournament. And they were using Wu Chen as their proxy.

"Good work." Zhao Feng reached into his pouch and tossed Xiao Chen a small bag of coins. "Get something to eat. Real food, not scraps from the market. Then find out everything you can about Wu Chen's supplier. Who sells him his herbs. Where they come from."

Xiao Chen caught the bag with practiced ease. "And you?"

"I'm going to pay Wu Chen a visit. But not tonight. Tonight, I'm going to the Night Market."

---

The Night Market operated in the shadows of the outer city, a sprawling collection of tents and carts that appeared after sunset and vanished before dawn. Official patrols never ventured here. The city guard had learned long ago that the merchants of the Night Market paid better bribes than they earned in salary.

Zhao Feng navigated the winding paths with the confidence of someone who'd walked them a thousand times. In his past life, he'd owned three stalls here. Now he was just another customer, his face hidden beneath a wide-brimmed hat and his cultivation suppressed to match the surrounding mortals.

He found what he was looking for at the market's edge: a small tent with a faded red banner bearing the symbol of a coiled serpent. Inside, an old woman sat cross-legged on a cushion, her fingers counting prayer beads with mechanical precision.

"I need information," Zhao Feng said, sliding into the seat across from her.

The woman didn't look up. "Everyone needs information. The question is what they're willing to pay."

"About a merchant named Wu Chen. His suppliers. His debts. His secrets."

The beads stopped moving. The woman's eyes, milky with age, fixed on Zhao Feng with unsettling clarity. "Wu Chen. The herb merchant. You're not the first to ask about him tonight."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. "Who else?"

"A woman. Cultivator. She paid me twice what you're about to offer, then told me to forget I'd ever seen her." The old woman smiled, revealing gaps where teeth should have been. "I told her the same thing I'm telling you. Some information can't be bought. It has to be earned."

"I don't have time for riddles."

"Then you should have come earlier." The woman reached into her robe and produced a folded piece of paper. "Wu Chen has been buying from a single supplier for the past three months. A man named Huo Jin. Used to be a hunter in the Spirit Wolf Mountains. Now he sells herbs to anyone with coin."

Zhao Feng took the paper. "Why would a cultivator care about a herb merchant's supplier?"

"Because Huo Jin isn't just a hunter. He's the one who found the Lightning Lotus."

The words hit Zhao Feng like a physical blow. The Lightning Lotus. The key to the technique that would win the Immortal Ascension Tournament. In his past life, it had been discovered by a nobody, sold to the young woman who would become the tournament champion, and changed the course of cultivation history.

But in this life, Zhao Feng had planned to find it first.

"How does Wu Chen fit into this?"

"Huo Jin doesn't trust merchants. He trusts Wu Chen. They grew up together in the same village. Wu Chen is the only one who knows where the lotus was found." The old woman leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. "The cultivator who came here? She's going to kill Wu Chen tonight. Take his memories. Find the lotus herself."

The paper crumpled in Zhao Feng's fist. "When?"

"The woman left ten minutes ago. If you run, you might make it in time to watch him die."

Zhao Feng was already on his feet, throwing a pouch of coins onto the cushion. He burst from the tent and sprinted through the Night Market, shoving past merchants and customers alike. His mind raced faster than his feet.

Wu Chen's teahouse was twenty minutes away on foot. If the cultivator had already left, he had maybe fifteen minutes before she arrived. Maybe less.

He couldn't fight a cultivator. Not yet. His cultivation was still at the third level of Qi Condensation, barely enough to light a candle with his spiritual energy. A trained sword cultivator would cut him down in seconds.

But he didn't need to fight. He needed to warn Wu Chen.

Or better yet, he needed to get to Wu Chen first.

---

The teahouse sat on the corner of Silk Street and Jade Lane, a modest two-story building with red pillars and paper lanterns. Most of the lanterns were dark now, the establishment closed for the night. But a single candle flickered in the upstairs window.

Zhao Feng didn't bother with the front door. He circled to the alley behind the building, found a drainage pipe, and climbed. His hands slipped on the rain-slicked metal, but his fingers found purchase in the gaps between bricks. Years of street survival had made him as nimble as any acrobat.

He reached the second-floor window and peered through a crack in the paper screen.

Wu Chen sat at a low table, a cup of tea steaming before him. Across from him sat a woman in a gray robe, her face obscured by a veil. A short sword rested on the table between them, its blade catching the candlelight.

Zhao Feng's heart hammered against his ribs. He was too late.

"—understand your concerns," the woman was saying, her voice smooth as silk. "But the price I'm offering is more than fair. Five thousand spirit coins for the location. You'll never have to work again."

Wu Chen's hands trembled around his teacup. "I told you. I don't know where Huo Jin found the lotus. He never told me."

"Liar." The woman's hand drifted toward her sword. "We can do this the easy way, merchant. Or we can do it the hard way. I assure you, the hard way is considerably less pleasant."

"I swear! On my mother's grave, I don't know!"

The woman sighed. "Pity."

She moved faster than Zhao Feng could track. One moment she was seated, the next she was standing, her sword pressed against Wu Chen's throat. A single drop of blood welled up where the blade touched skin.

"I'll ask one more time. Where is the Lightning Lotus?"

Zhao Feng made his decision.

He kicked the window frame with all his strength. The paper screen tore, and he tumbled through the opening, rolling as he hit the floor. The woman spun, her sword flashing toward him, but he was already moving, throwing a handful of powdered qi stones into her face.

The powder wasn't deadly. But it was blinding.

The woman screamed, clawing at her eyes. Zhao Feng grabbed Wu Chen by the collar and dragged him toward the door. "Move! Now!"

They tumbled down the stairs, Wu Chen stumbling and cursing, Zhao Feng pulling him along. Behind them, he heard the woman's voice rising in fury, felt the surge of her spiritual energy as she prepared to unleash something devastating.

They burst through the teahouse's back door and into the alley. Zhao Feng didn't stop. He pulled Wu Chen through the maze of backstreets, turning corner after corner until his lungs burned and his legs screamed for mercy.

Finally, they collapsed behind a stack of rotting crates, gasping for air.

Wu Chen's face was ashen. "Who—who was that? What does she want with me?"

"She wants the Lightning Lotus." Zhao Feng wiped sweat from his brow. "And she's going to keep coming until she gets it. Or until someone kills her."

"I don't know where it is! I told her the truth!"

"I know." Zhao Feng met Wu Chen's terrified gaze. "But you know who does. Huo Jin. And I'm going to need you to take me to him."

Wu Chen's eyes widened. "I can't. He'll kill me if I bring strangers to his door."

"Then I'll give you a better offer." Zhao Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a jade token, the symbol of his merchant guild etched into its surface. "Protection. A place to hide. Enough money to start a new life in another city. All I need is the location."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then that woman will find you again. And next time, she won't give you a chance to run."

Wu Chen stared at the token for a long moment. His hands were still shaking, but something in his eyes shifted—fear giving way to calculation.

"There's a village three days east of here," he said slowly. "Hidden in the Spirit Wolf Mountains. Huo Jin lives there with his family. He's the only one who knows where the lotus grows."

"Take me there."

"Impossible. I have debts. Obligations. I can't just disappear."

"Then give me a map. Write down the path. I'll find it myself."

Wu Chen laughed bitterly. "You think I'm stupid? If I give you the location, you'll have no reason to keep me alive."

Zhao Feng almost smiled. "Smart man. Then we have a problem. Because I can't trust you to lead me there, and you can't trust me to keep my word."

A crash echoed from somewhere nearby. The woman was getting closer.

Wu Chen's face paled. "Fine. I'll take you. But we leave tonight. And you swear on your cultivation that you'll protect me until I'm safe."

"I swear."

They rose from behind the crates and disappeared into the darkness of the alley, leaving the teahouse and the screaming cultivator behind.

Neither of them noticed the shadow that followed them, silent as death, its eyes gleaming with cold amusement.

---

Three hours later, Zhao Feng stood at the city's eastern gate, a pack on his back and Wu Chen at his side. The guards were asleep, their lanterns dimmed against the pre-dawn chill. Beyond the gate, the road stretched into the mist-shrouded mountains.

"Once we leave," Wu Chen whispered, "there's no turning back. Huo Jin doesn't trust outsiders. If he thinks you're a threat, he'll

title: The Hidden Blade
content:
The lantern cast long shadows across the warehouse as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger's columns. Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. In his past life, he'd been a spectator, watching from the cheap seats as the lightning-wielding nobody shattered expectations.

This time, he wouldn't just watch. He'd own the arena.

But first, he needed capital. Real capital. The kind that made cultivation clans sit up and take notice.

A knock at the door shattered his concentration. Three quick raps, a pause, then two more. Xiao Chen's signal.

Zhao Feng slid the ledger into a hidden compartment beneath the table. "Enter."

The door creaked open. Xiao Chen slipped inside, his movements silent despite his worn boots. The boy looked thinner than he had three days ago, dark circles shadowing his eyes. But there was a sharpness there, too. A hunger that hadn't existed before.

"Wu Chen's meeting with someone tonight," Xiao Chen said without preamble. "Not at the Flying Crane Inn. At a teahouse in the southern district. The Jade Leaf."

Zhao Feng's eyebrows rose. The Jade Leaf was expensive. The kind of place where merchants conducted business they didn't want overheard because the walls were lined with sound-dampening spirit bamboo.

"Who's he meeting?"

"Couldn't get close enough to see his face." Xiao Chen's jaw tightened. "But I heard part of their conversation. They mentioned your name. And they mentioned something else. Something called the 'Black Market Auction.'"

The air left Zhao Feng's lungs.

The Black Market Auction. He remembered it vividly. In his past life, it had been where the underworld's elite traded in secrets, forbidden techniques, and cultivation resources that the major families had declared illegal. He'd never attended—too risky for a man building a legitimate empire.

But he knew what was being sold there this year. A map. A map to a hidden immortal's tomb, untouched for three thousand years.

Every major power in the region would kill for that map.

"Did they say when?"

"Seven days from now. The location changes every time." Xiao Chen's eyes searched Zhao Feng's face. "What is it? What's so important about this auction?"

Zhao Feng didn't answer immediately. His mind was racing, connecting dots that shouldn't have been connected. Wu Chen, a minor merchant with connections to the Zhao family, meeting with someone about the Black Market Auction. That meant the Zhao family knew about the map.

That meant they were planning to acquire it.

And if they acquired it, they'd become unstoppable. The tomb's treasures would fund their rise for generations. His father, the man who'd thrown him out like garbage, would use those resources to crush anyone who opposed them.

Including him.

"We need to change our plans," Zhao Feng said slowly. "The auction changes everything."

"What does it change? You don't even know what they're selling."

"The map to the Eternal Flame Immortal's tomb." Zhao Feng watched Xiao Chen's face go pale. "You've heard of it?"

"The street kids talk. They say the Eternal Flame was one of the strongest immortals before the Heaven's Tribulation took him. That his tomb holds techniques that could make a mortal into a god." Xiao Chen swallowed. "They say the major families have been searching for it for centuries."

"And now it's going to be sold to the highest bidder." Zhao Feng began pacing, his footsteps echoing through the empty warehouse. "If the Zhao family gets that map, they'll have everything they need to crush me before I've even begun to build. I need to stop them."

"Stop them how? You've got maybe ten thousand spirit coins. The major families will bring millions."

Zhao Feng stopped pacing. A slow smile spread across his face—the smile of a merchant who'd just seen an opportunity where others saw only obstacles.

"I don't need to buy the map," he said. "I just need to make sure they don't get it either."

"You're going to steal it?"

"I'm going to ensure it falls into the right hands." Zhao Feng's eyes glittered in the lantern light. "And if those hands happen to belong to someone who owes me a favor, well. That's just good business."

---

The next three days passed in a blur of activity.

Zhao Feng liquidated everything. Every qi stone, every bundle of spirit grass, every minor talisman he'd accumulated. He sold them at a loss, taking whatever coin he could get. By the end of the third day, he had twenty-three thousand spirit coins.

It wasn't enough. But it was a start.

He sent Xiao Chen to gather information on everyone who might attend the auction. The list was short but terrifying: the Zhao family, of course, represented by his eldest brother Zhao Wei. The Lin family, rivals to the Zhao family for generations. The Jiang family, who controlled the region's spirit stone mines. And a mysterious figure known only as "The Collector," a man who'd reportedly bought and sold entire cities in his pursuit of rare artifacts.

The Collector. In his past life, Zhao Feng had done business with him once. The man was ruthless, brilliant, and utterly unafraid of the major families. He was also the only person at the auction who might be willing to work with a nobody from the outer districts.

"Find me a way to contact The Collector," Zhao Feng told Xiao Chen. "Before the auction."

The boy nodded and disappeared into the night.

---

The message came two days later, delivered by a street urchin with a missing tooth and a note sealed with black wax.

*The Old Bridge. Midnight. Come alone.*

Zhao Feng arrived at the appointed time, his cloak pulled tight against the cold. The Old Bridge spanned the river that divided the merchant district from the slums, its ancient stones worn smooth by centuries of footsteps. In the moonlight, it looked almost beautiful.

A figure stood at the bridge's center, silhouetted against the stars.

"You're early," the figure said. The voice was female, low and musical. "I appreciate punctuality."

Zhao Feng stopped ten feet away. "You're not The Collector."

"Sharp." The woman turned, and Zhao Feng got his first look at her face. She was young—maybe twenty-five—with sharp features and eyes that seemed to catch the moonlight. Her robes were simple but well-made, and she wore no jewelry. "I'm his agent. He doesn't meet with just anyone."

"Then why did he agree to meet with me?"

"Because you mentioned the Black Market Auction." The woman stepped closer, her eyes never leaving his face. "He wants to know how a street merchant with no backing heard about something that's supposed to be secret."

"I have my sources."

"Everyone has sources. Few have accurate ones." She stopped three feet away, close enough that Zhao Feng could smell jasmine and something metallic beneath it. "Tell me what you want, and I'll decide if it's worth his time."

"I want to partner with him." Zhao Feng kept his voice steady. "The Eternal Flame's tomb contains treasures beyond imagination. I know where the key is hidden. The key that opens the tomb's inner sanctum."

The woman's mask slipped for just a moment—surprise, quickly hidden. "That's a bold claim."

"It's a true one."

"If it were true, why wouldn't you keep it for yourself?"

"Because I don't have the resources to reach the tomb. The Collector does." Zhao Feng spread his hands. "I offer the key. He offers the means to use it. We split the proceeds sixty-forty."

"Sixty-forty in whose favor?"

"Mine."

The woman laughed—a genuine sound, warm and unexpected. "You've got balls, I'll give you that. No one asks for sixty percent from The Collector."

"I'm not asking. I'm offering a fair deal."

"Fair." She shook her head, still smiling. "I'll deliver your message. He'll decide if he wants to meet you in person." She turned to leave, then paused. "One piece of advice, merchant. Don't try to play games with him. He's been in this business for three hundred years. He's seen every trick."

"Then he'll recognize that I'm not playing any."

She was gone before he finished speaking, swallowed by the shadows as if she'd never existed.

---

The night of the auction arrived with a cold wind and a sky choked with clouds.

Zhao Feng stood in the warehouse, dressed in his finest robes—still cheap compared to what the major families would be wearing, but presentable. Xiao Chen had returned an hour ago with the location: an abandoned temple in the eastern hills, ten miles outside the city.

"The Collector's agent sent word," Xiao Chen said. "He'll meet you after the auction. She said to look for the man in the red mask."

"Good." Zhao Feng checked the dagger hidden in his sleeve, the talismans tucked into his belt, the poison capsule sewn into his collar. He'd prepared for every possibility he could imagine. "You know what to do?"

"Stay hidden. Watch everything. If you don't come out by dawn, I go to the city guard and tell them about the massacre."

"Not a massacre. An auction."

"Same thing, isn't it?" Xiao Chen's voice was flat. "Rich people killing each other over shiny rocks."

Zhao Feng couldn't argue with that.

They left the warehouse together, Xiao Chen melting into the shadows as they reached the city gates. Zhao Feng continued alone, following the winding road into the hills.

The temple appeared at the end of a narrow valley, its ancient walls crumbling but intact. Lanterns hung from the eaves, casting pools of colored light across the courtyard. Figures moved in the darkness—guards, servants, attendees arriving in sedan chairs or on horseback.

Zhao Feng approached on foot, his heart pounding but his face calm.

A masked attendant stopped him at the gate. "Name and affiliation."

"No name. No affiliation. I have an invitation from The Collector."

The attendant's eyes widened behind his mask. He stepped aside. "This way, sir."

Zhao Feng followed him into the temple, his mind racing.

The main hall had been transformed. Silk tapestries covered the walls, hiding the cracked stone beneath. A raised platform stood at the center, surrounded by seats arranged in a semicircle. Perhaps fifty people were already seated, their faces hidden behind masks or hoods.

Zhao Feng took a seat at the back, where he could see everyone who entered.

He counted the Zhao family representatives as they arrived: his brother Zhao Wei, looking smug and confident; two of his cousins, both minor cultivators; and a man Zhao Feng didn't recognize, dressed in robes so dark they seemed to drink the light.

The Lin family sat across from them, their patriarch glaring openly at the Zhao delegation.

The Jiang family arrived last, taking the seats closest to the platform.

And then, as the auction was about to begin, a final figure entered.

He was tall, with broad shoulders and a mask of deep crimson that covered his entire face. His robes were simple black silk, unadorned, but he moved with the confidence of a man who'd never needed to prove anything.

The Collector.

He took a seat in the front row, directly across from the Zhao family delegation.

And then he turned, just slightly, and looked directly at Zhao Feng.

Their eyes met through the mask's narrow eyeholes.

The Collector nodded once.

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold.

*He knew I'd be here. He knew where I'd sit.*

The auctioneer stepped onto the platform, raising his hands for silence.

"Welcome," he said, his voice carrying through the hall, "to the Black Market Auction. Tonight, we offer treasures that will reshape the balance of power in this region. Bid wisely. Bid generously. And

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Snake in the Grass

The knock came at midnight.

Zhao Feng looked up from his ledger, a single oil lamp casting long shadows across the warehouse. He'd been calculating profit margins on mid-grade qi stones, cross-referencing prices from three different markets. The numbers didn't lie. Someone was buying up supply, artificially inflating prices.

But that wasn't what made his blood run cold.

It was the knock. Three raps, a pause, then two more.

The signal he'd arranged with Xiao Chen for emergencies.

He moved to the door, sliding the iron bolt aside without a sound. The boy slipped through, eyes wide, breath coming in short gasps. His thin chest heaved beneath his patched robes.

"Wu Chen," Xiao Chen said, the name falling from his lips like a curse. "He's not alone."

Zhao Feng closed the door, relocked it. "Tell me everything."

The boy pressed his back against a crate of spirit grass, his small hands shaking. "I followed him like you said. He meets a man every third night at the Drunken Crane tavern. But tonight—" He swallowed hard. "Tonight there were five of them. Cultivators. Real ones."

"How do you know they were cultivators?"

"Because I saw one of them punch a hole through a brick wall." Xiao Chen's voice cracked. "Just to prove a point. Wu Chen was begging. On his knees. He owes them money. A lot of it."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. In his past life, Wu Chen had been a minor thorn, a debt collector for the Zhao family who'd grown too ambitious. But this was different. This was a man drowning, grasping at anything to stay afloat.

And men who drown drag others down with them.

"Did they see you?"

"No. I was in the rafters." A flicker of pride crossed the boy's face. "They never look up."

"Good." Zhao Feng reached into his pouch and tossed Xiao Chen a spirit coin. "Get yourself something hot to eat. And a blanket. You'll need your strength tomorrow."

The boy caught the coin, his eyes widening. "This is too much—"

"Consider it a bonus." Zhao Feng's voice softened. "You did well. Now rest. I'll need you at dawn."

After Xiao Chen left, Zhao Feng sat in the silence of his warehouse, the oil lamp casting dancing shadows across his face. He pulled out a blank parchment and began writing.

Names. Dates. Debts.

Wu Chen owed the Cloud Serpent Sect thirty thousand spirit coins. He'd borrowed to finance a gambling habit, hoping to win enough to pay back his original loans. Instead, he'd dug himself deeper.

Now the sect had sent enforcers.

And Wu Chen had two choices: pay up, or disappear.

But there was a third option, one that men like Wu Chen always considered when backed into corners. Betrayal. Sell information about a rising merchant with a growing stockpile of qi stones. Give the enforcers a new target.

Zhao Feng set down his brush.

He needed to move faster.

---

Dawn arrived gray and cold, mist curling through the merchant district like fingers through a corpse. Zhao Feng stood outside the Drunken Crane tavern, watching the door.

He didn't have to wait long.

Wu Chen stumbled out, his robes disheveled, his face a mask of desperation. Dark circles ringed his eyes. His hands trembled as he adjusted his collar.

"Wu Chen."

The man froze. Slowly, he turned. His eyes widened when he recognized Zhao Feng.

"Young Master Zhao." A nervous laugh. "What brings you here at this hour?"

"The same thing that brings you here." Zhao Feng stepped closer, his voice low. "Opportunity."

Wu Chen's face went through a series of emotions—fear, confusion, then a desperate, grasping hope. "I don't know what you mean."

"Don't you?" Zhao Feng stopped an arm's length away. He could smell the cheap wine on Wu Chen's breath, could see the frantic pulse beating in his throat. "You owe the Cloud Serpent Sect thirty thousand spirit coins. The enforcers gave you one week to pay up. But you don't have the money, do you?"

Wu Chen's face drained of color. "How did you—"

"I know a lot of things." Zhao Feng's voice dropped to a whisper. "I know you've been reporting my activities to the Zhao family. I know you've been skimming from our deals. And I know you were planning to sell me out to the Cloud Serpent Sect as payment."

"I—I would never—"

"Don't." The word cut through Wu Chen's protests like a blade. "Don't insult me by lying."

For a long moment, Wu Chen said nothing. Then his shoulders sagged. The fight drained out of him, leaving only a hollow, broken man.

"What do you want?" he asked, his voice barely audible.

"Your loyalty."

"My loyalty?" Wu Chen laughed, a bitter, broken sound. "You want loyalty from a man who's already betrayed you?"

"I want loyalty from a man who has nothing left to lose." Zhao Feng's eyes bored into Wu Chen's. "The Cloud Serpent Sect isn't going to let you go. Even if you paid them, they'd find another reason to squeeze you dry. You're a mark to them. A resource to be exploited."

"And you're different?"

"Yes." Zhao Feng didn't blink. "Because I don't need your money. I need your connections. The Zhao family trusted you once. They'll trust you again if I tell them you've turned over a new leaf."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because I need someone inside their operation." Zhao Feng stepped closer, his voice a silken whisper. "Someone who can feed me information. Someone who can warn me when they move against me."

Wu Chen's eyes darted left and right, checking for eavesdroppers. "You're asking me to spy on the Zhao family. On your own blood."

"My blood." Zhao Feng's voice hardened. "My blood that left me to rot in the outer disciples. My blood that stole everything from me. My blood that—" He stopped himself. Too much. He was showing too much.

But Wu Chen had seen it. The flash of genuine hatred in those young eyes.

"You really do hate them, don't you?"

"Let's just say I have a long memory." Zhao Feng reached into his robes and pulled out a pouch. It clinked with the sound of spirit coins. "Here. Ten thousand. A down payment on your debt. I'll give you the rest when you've proven yourself."

Wu Chen took the pouch with trembling hands. "You'd trust me with this?"

"No. But I trust your desperation." Zhao Feng turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing. The Cloud Serpent Sect. They're not just debt collectors. They're also information brokers. I need you to find out who's been buying up all the mid-grade qi stones in the region."

Wu Chen's eyes narrowed. "You think the sect is involved?"

"I think everyone is involved." Zhao Feng's smile didn't reach his eyes. "Including the Zhao family. Including the cultivators who killed my father. Including—" He stopped again.

Including my sons. In another life.

"I'll see what I can find out," Wu Chen said, clutching the pouch to his chest.

"Do that." Zhao Feng walked away, his footsteps echoing through the empty street.

---

Three days passed.

Xiao Chen reported that Wu Chen had paid off a portion of his debt and had been seen meeting with various minor officials. The information network was being rebuilt.

But Zhao Feng's instincts screamed that something was wrong.

It came to a head on the fourth night.

He was in the warehouse, sorting through a shipment of spirit grass, when he heard it. The crunch of gravel outside. The soft whisper of footsteps.

Multiple footsteps.

He doused the lantern immediately, plunging the warehouse into darkness. His hand found the knife hidden beneath his robes. His other hand reached for the emergency pouch he always kept by the door.

The door splintered inward.

Three figures entered, their cultivation auras blazing like bonfires in the darkness. Zhao Feng's heart sank. These weren't debt collectors. These weren't minor enforcers.

These were killing cultivators. Qi Condensation level, at least. Maybe higher.

"Young Master Zhao," a voice called out, smooth and mocking. "We know you're in here. Come out, and we'll make this quick."

Zhao Feng pressed himself against the wall, calculating. The door was blocked. The windows were too small to escape through. The only other exit was a trapdoor in the floor, leading to a drainage tunnel.

But he'd never make it in time.

"I said come out." The voice hardened. "Or we burn this place down with you inside."

A flame flickered to life in the speaker's palm, casting dancing shadows across the warehouse. Zhao Feng saw them now. Three men, each wearing the gray robes of the Cloud Serpent Sect.

Wu Chen's debt collectors.

Or perhaps, Wu Chen's buyers.

"I see you met with Wu Chen," Zhao Feng said, stepping out of the shadows. He kept his hands visible, the knife hidden. "How much did he sell me for?"

The lead cultivator smiled. It wasn't a pleasant expression. "Fifty thousand spirit coins. A generous price for a dead merchant."

"Generous indeed." Zhao Feng's mind raced. "But I have a counter offer."

"Save your breath." The cultivator's flame grew brighter. "Your information is already paid for. Now we just need to collect."

"Information?" Zhao Feng raised an eyebrow. "What information could I possibly have that's worth fifty thousand spirit coins?"

The lead cultivator laughed. "Don't play dumb, boy. We know about the hidden cultivation technique. The one your father discovered before he died. The one that can break through to the Foundation Establishment realm without a heavenly tribulation."

Zhao Feng's blood turned to ice.

In his past life, that technique had never existed. It was a legend, a myth whispered among desperate cultivators who couldn't breakthrough through normal means. In this life, he'd never mentioned it to anyone.

Except—

Except in his fever dream. The night he'd woken up in this body. The night he'd screamed his past-life secrets into the darkness, thinking no one was listening.

But someone had been listening.

Someone had been waiting.

"You're wondering how we know," the lead cultivator said, enjoying Zhao Feng's shock. "Let's just say you have a very attentive neighbor. An old woman who lives next door. She thought you were possessed by a demon. She came to us for help."

Zhao Feng's jaw tightened. The old woman who sold herbs. The one who always smiled at him when he passed.

She'd been listening. All this time.

"The technique doesn't exist," he said flatly. "It's a myth."

"Maybe. But the Cloud Serpent Sect is willing to pay to find out." The cultivator raised his flaming hand. "Last chance, boy. Come quietly, or we burn."

Zhao Feng's hand found the emergency pouch. Inside, a single talisman. A firecracker charm, meant for signaling Xiao Chen.

But he wasn't going to signal the boy.

He was going to signal the entire district.

"I have one question," Zhao Feng said, stalling for time.

"What?"

"The old woman. Did she tell you anything else?" His fingers inched toward the pouch. "Anything about my plans?"

The lead cultivator frowned. "She said you kept talking about someone named Xiao Chen. That you kept apologizing to a ghost."

Zhao Feng's heart stopped.

He knew.

She'd heard everything. Every name. Every secret. Every plan.

In

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Hidden Vein

...He knew where she'd gotten those techniques.

Zhao Feng's brush stopped mid-stroke. The memory surfaced with startling clarity—the year of the tournament, the commotion at Thunderclap Peak, the discovery that had shaken the entire sect.

The Lightning Empress, they'd called her later. But before she was an empress, she'd been Lin Xue, an outer disciple so poor she'd sold her own blood to spirit doctors just to afford basic cultivation resources. She'd stumbled upon something in the mountains east of the city. Something that had been hidden for three thousand years.

An ancient cultivator's tomb.

Zhao Feng set down his brush. In his past life, he'd heard about the tomb's discovery from a merchant who'd supplied excavation tools to the sects. The location had been kept secret, but merchants always learned secrets. A price tag could always be attached to information.

He began sketching the mountain ranges from memory. The merchant who'd told him had been drunk, boastful, flashing spirit coins he'd made from the deal. "East of the city," the man had slurred, "past the three peaks, at the base of the waterfall that sounds like thunder."

Thunderclap Peak. Named for the constant roar of its waterfall.

The tomb hadn't been discovered until the tournament was already underway. Lin Xue had been training there, hiding from bullies, when she'd fallen through a hidden entrance. She'd emerged three days later with techniques that would make her a legend.

But the tomb wasn't empty when she'd found it. There had been guardians. Traps. A sealed chamber that she'd never been able to open.

Zhao Feng's fingers traced the map he was drawing. The sealed chamber had contained the real treasure—not the techniques she'd taken, but the source of them. A vein of lightning-aspected qi stones, pure enough to fuel a breakthrough to the Core Formation realm.

The major families had spent months trying to crack that chamber. They'd failed. In the end, they'd collapsed the entrance, sealing it forever.

But Zhao Feng knew something they hadn't. The chamber wasn't sealed by a formation or a lock. It was sealed by blood. Ancient cultivator blood, keyed to a specific lineage.

And he knew whose lineage it belonged to.

A knock at the warehouse door shattered his concentration.

Zhao Feng's hand moved instinctively to the dagger hidden beneath his robes. "Who is it?"

"Wu Chen." The voice was smooth, cultured. "You invited me to discuss business, young merchant Zhao."

Right. The meeting he'd arranged three days ago. He'd almost forgotten.

Zhao Feng rolled up his map and tucked it into his sleeve. "The door's unlocked."

The man who entered was everything Zhao Feng remembered—and everything he'd learned to hate. Wu Chen moved with the casual grace of someone who'd never known hunger. His robes were silk, embroidered with gold thread. His fingers carried three rings, each worth more than this warehouse.

In his past life, Wu Chen had been the first to betray him. The man had smiled while signing contracts that stripped Zhao Feng of his shipping routes. Had toasted his health while bleeding him dry.

"Your warehouse is... humble," Wu Chen said, looking around with barely concealed disdain. "Though I suppose that's fitting for a beginner."

"Modest beginnings build strong foundations." Zhao Feng poured two cups of tea from a clay pot. "Please, sit."

Wu Chen didn't sit. He walked to the crates of qi stones, lifted the lid of one, and examined the contents with practiced eyes. "Low-grade. Mixed quality. You're selling to outer disciples and street cultivators."

"The market exists."

"The market exists for garbage." Wu Chen let the lid fall. "I came because I heard interesting things about you, Zhao Feng. A nobody who appeared from nowhere, making deals that shouldn't work. Buying low, selling high. Cornering the market on spirit grass in the southern district."

Zhao Feng sipped his tea. "I've been fortunate."

"Fortunate." Wu Chen laughed. "Fortune has nothing to do with it. You have information you shouldn't have. You knew the harvest timing for the Chen family's spirit fields. You knew the price of qi stones would spike after the sect examinations. You knew—" He stopped, turning to face Zhao Feng directly. "How?"

This was the moment. The pivot point. In his past life, Zhao Feng had been flattered by Wu Chen's attention, had shared his secrets, had trusted the man who would destroy him.

Not this time.

"I pay attention," Zhao Feng said. "I watch. I listen. The world tells you everything if you know how to hear it."

"Pretty words. But they don't answer my question."

"They answer it well enough." Zhao Feng set down his cup. "You're not here to learn my methods, Wu Chen. You're here to decide if I'm useful to you or a threat to eliminate."

The silence stretched. Wu Chen's eyes narrowed.

"You speak boldly for a boy with nothing."

"I speak honestly for a man who knows his worth." Zhao Feng stood, matching Wu Chen's height. "I don't want to be your partner. I don't want to be your subordinate. I want to offer you a trade."

"A trade." Wu Chen's lips curled. "What could you possibly have that I want?"

"Information about the Immortal Ascension Tournament."

Now he had the man's attention. Wu Chen's casual posture stiffened. The rings on his fingers caught the lantern light as he folded his arms.

"Speak."

"In six months, an outer disciple will win the tournament. A nobody. A girl named Lin Xue. She'll defeat everyone—inner disciples, family prodigies, even the sect master's own son."

"Ridiculous."

"It happened." Zhao Feng let the words hang. "She won because she found something. An ancient tomb east of the city, at the base of Thunderclap Peak's waterfall. She took lightning techniques from it. But she missed the real prize."

Wu Chen's face had gone still. The mask of arrogance had cracked, revealing cold calculation beneath.

"What prize?"

"A vein of lightning-aspected qi stones. Pure enough to fuel Core Formation. The tomb's sealed chamber contains enough wealth to make the major families look like beggars."

"You're lying."

"I'm not." Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and pulled out the map he'd drawn. "This shows the exact location. I'll give it to you in exchange for three things."

Wu Chen's hand shot out, but Zhao Feng pulled the map back.

"First: you leave me alone. No interference with my business. No attempts to recruit or eliminate me. Second: you provide me with a letter of introduction to the Heavenly Treasure Pavilion in the capital. Third: you give me your word that when you open that sealed chamber, you'll remember who told you about it."

"Those are small demands for such valuable information."

"Because I'm not greedy." Zhao Feng met his eyes. "I'm patient. I plant seeds. I wait for them to grow."

Wu Chen studied him for a long moment. Then he laughed—a real laugh, surprised and genuine.

"You're interesting, Zhao Feng. Truly interesting." He reached into his own sleeve and produced a jade token, which he tossed across the room. "That's my seal. It'll get you into the Heavenly Treasure Pavilion. As for the rest—you have my word."

Did he? Zhao Feng caught the token and examined it. Authentic. Wu Chen's personal seal, marked with his family crest.

"One more thing," Zhao Feng said. "Lin Xue will find that tomb in three months. She'll emerge changed. When she does, don't try to take the techniques from her. She'll be stronger than you expect."

Wu Chen's smile turned predatory. "Are you warning me or threatening me?"

"Giving you advice. Take it or leave it."

They stood there, two predators circling each other. Then Wu Chen extended his hand.

"The map?"

Zhao Feng handed it over.

Wu Chen unrolled it, studied the markings, and nodded slowly. "If this is accurate, you've just made yourself very useful to me."

"I'm not useful to anyone. I'm useful to myself." Zhao Feng picked up his tea and took a long drink. "The tomb is yours. Go claim it."

Wu Chen left without another word. The door closed behind him, and Zhao Feng was alone again.

He counted to sixty. Then he moved.

The map he'd given Wu Chen was accurate—but incomplete. It showed the tomb's entrance, the outer chambers, even the sealed chamber's location. What it didn't show were the guardian formation keyed to bloodlines. What it didn't show were the traps that would activate if the wrong person tried to open the chamber.

Wu Chen would break his way in. He'd lose men. He'd waste resources. And when he reached the sealed chamber and couldn't open it, he'd come back to Zhao Feng for the answer.

By then, it would be too late.

Zhao Feng grabbed his bag and headed for the door. He had three months to prepare. Three months to find Lin Xue before she found the tomb. Three months to build enough capital to claim the real prize.

He stepped out into the night.

The streets were quiet. Most merchants had closed their stalls. The only light came from the pleasure houses and the occasional lantern carried by night patrols.

Xiao Chen appeared from the shadows, moving like a ghost.

"Wu Chen went east," the boy reported. "He's got four bodyguards with him. They're heading toward the sect district."

"Good. Did he seem suspicious?"

"Suspicious of what? He thinks you're a nobody who got lucky." Xiao Chen's voice held a note of contempt. "He didn't even check the warehouse for spies."

"Because he's arrogant. That's his weakness." Zhao Feng started walking. "Come. We have work to do."

"Where are we going?"

"To find a woman who doesn't know she's about to become an immortal."

They moved through the city like shadows. Xiao Chen knew every alley, every hidden passage, every shortcut that the wealthy merchants never bothered to learn. Within twenty minutes, they'd crossed from the merchant district into the outer sect territory.

The sect's outer disciples lived in cramped dormitories at the base of the mountain. Poor cultivators, rejects from major families, those who hadn't passed the inner sect examinations. They worked menial jobs, took dangerous missions, and hoped for a chance at advancement.

Lin Xue lived among them.

Zhao Feng stopped at the edge of the outer disciple village. Lanterns glowed in windows. A few disciples still practiced forms in the training yard, their movements clumsy and unrefined.

"Wait here," he told Xiao Chen. "If anyone asks, you're looking for a lost pet."

"And you?"

"I'm about to change someone's life."

He walked into the village, past the training yard, past the communal kitchens, until he reached a small hut at the far edge of the settlement. A single candle burned inside.

He knocked.

The door opened a crack. A woman's face appeared—young, tired, with dark circles under her eyes and a bruise on her cheek.

"What do you want?"

"Lin Xue?" Zhao Feng kept his voice low. "My name is Zhao Feng. I'm a merchant. I have an offer for you."

"I don't have money for anything you're selling."

"I'm not selling anything. I'm giving something away." He reached into his bag and pulled out a small pouch. "Inside this pouch is a map to a place that will change your life. Take it. Use it. But remember one thing."

She stared at the pouch, suspicion warring with curiosity.

"When you find what's hidden there, don't tell anyone where you

title: The Enemy's Shadow
content:
The lantern flame flickered as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger's columns. Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. In his past life, he'd watched from the stands, a mid-tier merchant with no cultivation to speak of, cheering alongside the crowds as lightning arced across the arena and a young woman in tattered robes brought down a foundation establishment elder.

Lin Xue. The name burned in his memory.

She'd been an outer disciple of the Azure Cloud Sect, barely worthy of notice. Her family had been slaughtered by demon beasts when she was twelve. She had no connections, no backing, no resources. Just raw talent and a desperate hunger to rise.

The major families had courted her after her victory. The Zhao family had been among them, offering land, marriage alliances, everything short of the family secrets. She'd refused them all.

But Zhao Feng had seen something the others missed. A few months before the tournament, a traveling merchant had passed through the city selling ancient cultivation manuals. Most were fakes, worthless scribbles meant to fool the desperate. But one of them had been real—a fragment of a lightning cultivation technique from the Thunder Emperor's era.

That merchant had been murdered on the road three days after selling it. The technique had vanished.

In his past life, Zhao Feng had assumed Lin Xue had bought it from someone else. But now, with forty years of hindsight, he saw the truth. The merchant who'd sold it had been part of a network—a shadow organization that traded in forbidden techniques and stolen cultivation resources. And Lin Xue had been their test subject.

If he could find that merchant before he died, before the technique fell into the wrong hands, he could control the most valuable asset in the upcoming tournament.

But first, he needed money. A lot of it.

He dipped his brush in ink and began writing a list.
