"Work for me." She leaned forward, her violet eyes boring into his. "I've been watching you, Zhao Feng. The deals you've made. The connections you've built. The way you predicted market shifts that even veteran merchants missed. You have talent. More than talent—you have vision."

"And Wu Chen?"

"He's a pawn. Useful, but replaceable." She waved her hand dismissively. "If you agree to work for the Bureau, I'll make his complaint disappear. I'll even give you access to resources that will accelerate your cultivation tenfold. All I ask in return is your loyalty."

Zhao Feng sat in silence, his mind racing. In his past life, Lin Xue had been his enemy. She'd uncovered his schemes, exposed his manipulations, and nearly destroyed everything he'd built. But that had been later, after he'd become powerful enough to threaten the established order.

Now she was offering him a shortcut. A way to skip years of struggle and jump straight into the halls of power.

But at what cost?

"If I refuse?" he asked.

"Then the investigation proceeds. Your assets are seized. Your operation is destroyed." She smiled again, that cold, predatory smile. "And Wu Chen gets everything you've built."

"Because you'll give it to him."

"Because I'll take it from you and give it to whoever I choose." She stood, brushing imaginary dust from her robes. "You have three days to decide. Find me at the Bureau headquarters when you've made your choice."

She walked past him, her silk robes whispering against the floor. At the door, she paused.

"Oh, and Zhao Feng? That little street rat you've been training—Xiao Chen, isn't it? He's clever. But clever children have a habit of getting hurt when adults play dangerous games."

The door closed behind her, leaving Zhao Feng alone with the cooling tea and the weight of an impossible choice.

---

He didn't go back to the warehouse. Instead, he walked through the city's winding streets, his mind churning. Lin Xue's offer was a trap, but refusing it was a death sentence. Unless he could find another way.

The sun was setting when he found himself at the Temple of the Broken Sword, the same ruined shrine where he'd recruited Xiao Chen. The boy was there, sitting on the broken altar, practicing the characters Zhao Feng had taught him by lantern light.

"You're late," Xiao Chen said without looking up. "I was starting to worry."

"Something came up." Zhao Feng sat beside him, the cold stone pressing through his robes. "I need to ask you something, and I need you to answer honestly."

Xiao Chen put down his brush. "Always."

"If I gave you a choice—a comfortable life, safety, enough money to never worry again—would you take it?"

The boy's dark eyes studied him. "Is that a real choice, or are you testing me?"

"Real."

"Then no." Xiao Chen picked up his brush again. "Comfortable lives are for people who've given up. I haven't. Not yet."

Zhao Feng felt something shift in his chest. In his past life, he'd surrounded himself with people who wanted safety, stability, security. And they'd all abandoned him the moment his power began to crumble.

This time, he'd found someone who wanted more.

"Good," he said. "Because we have three days to destroy Wu Chen, outmaneuver a Senior Inspector, and build an empire that no one can touch."

Xiao Chen's brush stopped. "That's... ambitious."

"I've been called worse." Zhao Feng stood and looked up at the stars beginning to appear in the darkening sky. "Let me tell you about a woman named Lin Xue. About the weakness she doesn't know she has. And about the one thing that can bring down even the most powerful cultivator."

"What's that?"

Zhao Feng smiled, and for the first time in weeks, it reached his eyes.

"Debt."

---

*Three hours later, in a hidden room beneath the Jade Pavilion, Wu Chen received a message sealed with golden wax. He broke the seal and read the single line of text.*

*"The boy is the key. Take him, and Zhao Feng will break."*

*Wu Chen smiled and began making preparations.*

title: The Price of Secrets
content:
The night air carried the scent of rain and rotting wood as Zhao Feng made his way through the winding alleys of the merchant district. His lantern cast dancing shadows against the cracked walls, and each step echoed in the silence like a drumbeat announcing his approach.

He'd sent Xiao Chen to watch Wu Chen three days ago. Three days with no word. No signal. No sign that the boy was even still alive.

The warehouse door stood before him, its lock intact. He pressed his palm against the wood and felt the faint pulse of his formation seal—still active, undisturbed. Good. If Wu Chen had tried anything, it would have triggered—

A sound. Soft. Deliberate.

Zhao Feng froze.

Behind him, maybe twenty feet away, someone had shifted their weight. The faintest scrape of leather on stone.

He didn't turn around. Didn't reach for his knife. Instead, he spoke to the darkness. "If you wanted me dead, I'd already be bleeding out on this street."

A long pause. Then footsteps approached.

"You're harder to kill than I expected, Merchant."

The voice was female. Low. Familiar.

Zhao Feng turned slowly, and the lantern light caught the face of a young woman dressed in worn traveler's clothes. Her dark hair was pulled back in a severe ponytail, and her eyes held the sharp, assessing look of someone who'd survived things she shouldn't have.

Lin Xue.

In his past life, she'd been the champion of the Immortal Ascension Tournament. The lightning cultivator who'd risen from nothing. The one everyone wanted to recruit.

In this life, she was standing outside his warehouse, watching him with undisguised curiosity.

"You've been following me," Zhao Feng said. Not a question.

"For two weeks." She stepped closer, her movements fluid and unhurried. "I wanted to see what kind of man buys cheap land, sells overpriced qi stones to desperate cultivators, and somehow manages to avoid the city guards even when he's breaking half a dozen trade laws."

"I don't break laws. I reinterpret them."

A ghost of a smile crossed her lips. "That's what lawbreakers always say."

Zhao Feng studied her. In the tournament, she'd been a whirlwind of destruction. Her lightning techniques had come from a hidden sect, one that had been crushed by the major families decades ago. She'd been their last disciple, carrying forbidden techniques that no one else knew existed.

But in this life, she was still unknown. Still unnoticed. Still hunting for something.

"What do you want?" he asked.

"Information." She crossed her arms. "You know things you shouldn't. I've watched you trade with cultivators twice your cultivation level. You always get what you want. You always walk away unscathed. That's not luck—that's knowledge."

"And you think I'll share that knowledge with a stranger who's been stalking me?"

She reached into her robe and pulled out a small cloth pouch. Tossed it to him. He caught it one-handed and felt the weight—heavy, dense, humming with energy.

High-grade qi stones. At least fifty of them.

"A deposit," she said. "I need to find someone. Someone who trained in the Stormcloud Sect before it was destroyed. I've been searching for three years, and every lead has been a dead end."

The Stormcloud Sect. The lightning cultivators. The ones who'd been wiped out by the three major families in a single night, forty years ago.

In his past life, Zhao Feng had known their last elder. A broken man who'd lived in obscurity, selling talismans on the street to survive. He'd died of a wasting sickness, taking the sect's remaining techniques with him.

Except he hadn't died. Not then.

"I know where you won't find him," Zhao Feng said slowly. "But I don't know where you will."

Lin Xue's eyes narrowed. "That's not what I want to hear."

"That's all I can give you for free." He tossed the pouch back to her. "Keep your stones. I don't take payment for incomplete information."

She caught it, her expression unreadable. "Then what do you want?"

"Answers." He stepped closer, lowering his voice. "The Immortal Ascension Tournament is six months away. You're going to compete. You're going to win. And when you do, every major family in the city is going to come knocking on your door, offering fortunes for your allegiance."

Her face went still. "How do you know I'm competing?"

"Because I know who you are, Lin Xue. I know why you're looking for the Stormcloud Sect. I know what you're carrying in your storage ring right now."

Her hand twitched toward her waist. "If you've told anyone—"

"I haven't. And I won't." He held her gaze. "I'm offering you a deal. You help me with a problem I have, and I'll give you the location of the last Stormcloud elder."

"He's alive?"

"He was, last I checked. He might be dead now. He might have moved. But I can give you a starting point that's better than anything you've found in three years."

Lin Xue was silent for a long moment. The wind picked up, carrying the scent of rain closer. Distant thunder rumbled.

"What's the problem?" she finally asked.

Zhao Feng allowed himself a small smile. "Wu Chen. He's a merchant from the Southern Trade Alliance. He came to me offering a partnership, but his real interest is in my supply chain. He wants to know where I'm getting my qi stones."

"And you want me to kill him?"

"No." Zhao Feng shook his head. "I want you to discredit him. He's invited me to a trade meeting tomorrow night at the Jade Phoenix Pavilion. I need someone to cause a distraction that will expose his true intentions to the other merchants in attendance."

"What kind of distraction?"

"The kind that involves lightning techniques and a very dramatic entrance."

Understanding flickered in her eyes. "You want me to show them what I can do. You want them to see a powerful cultivator backing you."

"I want them to see that anyone who tries to move against me has to answer to someone who commands lightning." Zhao Feng met her gaze. "You need resources for the tournament. I need leverage. We both get what we want."

Lin Xue considered this. Her fingers drummed against her arm as she weighed the offer.

"One condition," she said. "After the tournament, you owe me a favor. No limits. No questions."

Zhao Feng's instincts screamed at him to refuse. Favors in the cultivation world were dangerous things—they could be used to force someone into impossible situations, to bind them with oaths that couldn't be broken.

But he knew Lin Xue. In his past life, she'd been ruthless but honorable. She kept her word. She protected those who helped her.

"Agreed," he said.

They shook hands, and he felt the faint pulse of a qi oath seal into his palm—a binding promise that neither of them could break without suffering severe backlash.

"I'll be there tomorrow night," she said. "Don't make me regret this, Merchant."

"I never do."

She turned and vanished into the shadows, her footsteps silent against the stone. Zhao Feng watched her go, then finally unlocked his warehouse door and stepped inside.

The moment he crossed the threshold, he knew something was wrong.

The air was too still. The seals on his storage crates were intact, but the energy in the room felt... disturbed. Like someone had been here, touched everything, and then carefully put it back in place.

He held up his lantern and scanned the room. Crates of qi stones. Bundles of spirit grass. His scale and ledger on the table.

Nothing missing. Nothing moved.

But there was a piece of paper on his table that hadn't been there before.

He walked over and picked it up. The handwriting was elegant, precise. The ink was still wet.

*You've been busy, Feng.*

*I've been watching your progress with great interest. You're smarter than you were before. Quicker. More careful.*

*But you've made a mistake.*

*You think you're the only one who remembers how things ended.*

*I'll be in touch.*

*—Your loving father*

Zhao Feng's blood turned to ice.

That couldn't be right. His father had died when he was twenty-three. A heart failure, the physicians said. Zhao Feng had been there when they lowered his body into the ground. He'd watched the dirt cover the coffin.

But this handwriting was unmistakable. The phrasing, the cadence, the way his father always signed his notes with that same mocking endearment—*Your loving father*.

He read the note three times. Four times. Each time, the words didn't change.

Someone was playing games with him. Someone who knew about his past life. Someone who wanted him to know that they knew.

Or—

No. He refused to consider the alternative.

He crumpled the note and shoved it into his storage ring. His hand was shaking. He made it stop.

Tomorrow night. The Jade Phoenix Pavilion. He'd deal with Wu Chen first. Then he'd find out who wrote this note and burn their entire operation to the ground.

But even as he lit the candles and began organizing his supplies for the meeting, he couldn't shake the feeling that someone was watching him.

And that they were smiling.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Debt Collector Calls

The morning sun cast long shadows across the merchant district as Zhao Feng made his way through the crowded streets. Three days had passed since his meeting with Xiao Chen, and the boy had proven himself worthy of the trust placed in him.

Every evening, Xiao Chen appeared at the warehouse with reports. Wu Chen visited the same teahouse every afternoon. He met with the same three men. He asked questions about a young merchant who'd been buying qi stones in unusual quantities.

Zhao Feng had expected as much. Wu Chen worked for the Zhang family, and the Zhang family had their fingers in every pie in the outer district. They wouldn't ignore a newcomer cutting into their territory.

But Xiao Chen had also discovered something unexpected. Wu Chen was hiding something. He paid for his tea with silver, not spirit coins. He walked with a slight limp that suggested an old injury. And he always, always checked over his shoulder before entering any building.

A man afraid of being followed was a man with secrets.

Zhao Feng turned down a narrow alley, his destination a small apothecary shop that specialized in medicinal herbs. The shopkeeper, Old Man Hu, had been a valuable source of information in his past life. He knew everything that happened in the lower districts, and he owed favors to everyone who mattered.

The bell above the door chimed as Zhao Feng entered. The shop was cluttered with jars and bottles, the air thick with the smell of ginseng and dried mushrooms. Old Man Hu sat behind the counter, his wizened face half-hidden in shadow.

"Ah, the young merchant returns," Hu said, his voice crackling like old parchment. "I was wondering when you'd come back."

"You knew I would."

"Of course. You're too smart not to." Hu gestured to a stool. "Sit. We have much to discuss."

Zhao Feng sat, keeping his posture relaxed but alert. Old Man Hu wasn't a cultivator—not anymore. His cultivation had been crippled years ago, leaving him a mortal with a network that spanned the entire city. In this life, as in the last, he was a man to be respected.

"I heard you've been making waves," Hu said, pouring two cups of weak tea. "Buying up qi stones. Making deals with the outer disciples. The Zhang family is not pleased."

"The Zhang family doesn't own the qi stone market."

"No, but they act like they do." Hu slid a cup toward Zhao Feng. "They've been controlling prices in this district for twenty years. A newcomer who disrupts that control is a threat."

"I'm not a threat. I'm a businessman."

"To them, there's no difference." Hu took a sip of his tea, his eyes never leaving Zhao Feng's face. "I have information that might interest you. About Wu Chen."

"I'm listening."

"Wu Chen is no mere servant. He's a debt collector for the Zhang family's underground operations. Loans. Protection money. The kind of business that leaves bodies in the river."

Zhao Feng's expression didn't change, but his mind was racing. A debt collector. That explained the limp, the paranoia. Men in that line of work made enemies.

"His real target isn't your qi stones," Hu continued. "It's you. The Zhang family wants to know who you are, where you came from, and why you have so much capital to throw around."

"And what will they do when they find out?"

Hu's lips curled into a humorless smile. "If you're useful, they'll offer you a partnership. If you're a threat, they'll offer you a knife in the back."

Zhao Feng nodded slowly. He'd expected this. In his past life, he'd crushed the Zhang family's operations after they'd tried to extort him. But that was fifty years from now, when he had armies of cultivators at his command.

This time, he had nothing but his wits and a handful of qi stones.

"There's something else," Hu said, leaning forward. "The Immortal Ascension Tournament has been moved up. Three months from now, not six."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. "Impossible. The tournament is always held at the winter solstice."

"The major families petitioned the sect elders. They want to capitalize on the recent discovery of a spirit vein in the eastern mountains. The tournament will determine who controls the mining rights."

A spirit vein. A source of endless qi stones, the foundation of any cultivation empire. In his past life, the vein had been discovered three years from now, and the battle for control had sparked a decade-long conflict.

But now it was happening sooner. Everything was happening sooner.

"Who else knows about this?" Zhao Feng asked.

"Everyone who matters. The major families are already positioning their candidates. The betting houses are taking wagers on the winners." Hu paused. "And there are rumors that a dark horse has emerged. A nobody from the outer disciples who's been training in secret."

Zhao Feng's ears perked up. "A woman? Using lightning techniques?"

Hu's eyes widened slightly. "How did you know?"

Because in his past life, that woman had been his greatest regret. He'd had the chance to recruit her before the tournament, but he'd been too focused on other deals. She'd joined the Xiao family instead, and her lightning techniques had made them untouchable for years.

"I have my sources," Zhao Feng said. "What's her name?"

"Lin Mei. She's been buying supplies from the apothecary on Jade Street. High-grade stamina pills. Lightning-attuned spirit stones. She's preparing for something big."

Lin Mei. The name echoed in Zhao Feng's memory. In his past life, she'd become a legend. The Lightning Empress, they'd called her. She'd single-handedly turned the Xiao family from minor players into major powers.

But that was then. This time, he would find her first.

"I need to meet her," Zhao Feng said. "Can you arrange it?"

Hu stroked his chin thoughtfully. "It will cost you."

"Name your price."

"Three bottles of mid-grade recovery pills. And a favor to be named later."

Zhao Feng didn't hesitate. "Done."

Hu smiled, revealing yellowed teeth. "I'll send word when she's available. But I warn you, she's not easy to convince. She trusts no one, and she has reason not to."

"Everyone has a price."

"Some prices aren't measured in money."

Zhao Feng left the apothecary with his mind churning. The timeline had shifted. The spirit vein, the tournament, Lin Mei—everything was happening faster than he'd anticipated. He needed to accelerate his plans.

He returned to the warehouse to find Xiao Chen waiting for him, the boy's face pale and his hands trembling.

"Boss," Xiao Chen said, his voice barely a whisper. "Wu Chen is here. Inside."

Zhao Feng's hand moved instinctively to the dagger hidden in his sleeve. "How long?"

"Ten minutes. He said he wanted to wait for you."

"Alone?"

"He has two men with him. Big men. Cultivators."

Zhao Feng's mind raced through his options. He could run, but that would confirm his weakness. He could fight, but two cultivators against a mortal with a dagger was suicide.

Or he could walk in and face them like a merchant who had nothing to fear.

"Stay here," Zhao Feng said. "If you hear fighting, run to the apothecary and tell Old Man Hu everything."

"But boss—"

"Do as I say."

Zhao Feng pushed open the warehouse door and stepped inside.

The scene was exactly as Xiao Chen had described. Wu Chen sat on a crate near the back wall, his legs crossed and his hands folded in his lap. Two men stood behind him, their arms crossed and their eyes hard. All three wore the gray robes of the Zhang family's enforcers.

"Ah, the young merchant returns," Wu Chen said, his voice smooth as oil. "I was beginning to think you'd run away."

"Why would I run?" Zhao Feng said, closing the door behind him. "This is my warehouse."

"Your warehouse." Wu Chen laughed, a cold, hollow sound. "You bought it with borrowed money, didn't you? Money you got from selling qi stones to the outer disciples."

"Last I checked, there's no law against selling qi stones."

"There's no law against it, no." Wu Chen stood, brushing dust from his robes. "But there are consequences. The Zhang family has controlled this district's qi stone trade for two decades. We don't take kindly to interlopers."

"I'm not an interloper. I'm a businessman."

"Then you understand business." Wu Chen reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll. "This is a contract. The Zhang family is offering you a partnership. You give us sixty percent of your profits, and we allow you to continue operating in this district."

"And if I refuse?"

Wu Chen's smile didn't reach his eyes. "Then we take everything. Your warehouse. Your stock. Your life."

Zhao Feng looked at the contract, then at Wu Chen, then at the two enforcers. He could feel the weight of the dagger against his wrist, but he knew it wouldn't be enough.

But he also knew something Wu Chen didn't.

"You're afraid," Zhao Feng said quietly.

Wu Chen's smile faltered. "What?"

"You're afraid of me. That's why you're here with two enforcers. That's why the Zhang family wants a partnership instead of just taking what they want." Zhao Feng stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "You don't know who I am. You don't know where I came from. And that terrifies you."

Wu Chen's face twisted with anger. "You're a nobody. A gutter rat who got lucky."

"Am I?" Zhao Feng reached into his pocket and pulled out a single black spirit stone, holding it up so the light caught its surface. "Do you know what this is?"

Wu Chen's eyes widened. "That's... that's a shadow stone. They're illegal."

"This is a communication stone from the Shadow Market," Zhao Feng said. "And I have fifty more just like it. Do you know what happens when I crush this stone?"

Wu Chen took a step back. "You're bluffing."

"The Shadow Guild owes me a favor. A very large favor. If I crush this stone, they'll send a team to collect. And they won't stop until every Zhang family member in this district is dead."

The two enforcers exchanged nervous glances. Even Wu Chen's composure cracked.

"You're lying," he said, but his voice lacked conviction.

"Try me."

The silence stretched for an eternity. Zhao Feng's heart pounded in his chest, but he kept his hand steady, his eyes locked on Wu Chen's.

Finally, Wu Chen let out a breath. "This isn't over."

"It never is."

Wu Chen gestured to his men, and they filed out of the warehouse without another word. When the door closed behind them, Zhao Feng let out a shaky exhale and slumped against the wall.

The shadow stone was a fake. A polished piece of obsidian he'd bought for three spirit coins.

But Wu Chen didn't know that.

Xiao Chen appeared in the doorway, his face a mixture of awe and terror. "Boss... that was incredible."

"Don't praise me yet," Zhao Feng said, his voice hoarse. "That bought us time, nothing more. The Zhang family will come back, and next time, they won't be so easy to bluff."

"What do we do?"

Zhao Feng's mind was already working, calculating, planning. The timeline had changed. The stakes had risen. He needed allies, and he needed them fast.

"We find Lin Mei," he said. "Before anyone else does."

title: The Tournament's Hidden Blade
content:
The night air carried the scent of rain and rotting wood as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger's columns. Ten thousand spirit coins. Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. Not enough. Never enough.

He needed capital. Real capital. The kind that made elders sweat and clan heads reconsider their alliances.

A knock came at the warehouse door. Three rapid taps, a pause, then two more.

Xiao Chen's signal.

"Enter."

The boy slipped through the gap like smoke, his bare feet silent on the warped floorboards. He'd cleaned himself up over the past week—no more dirt caked under his nails, no more matted hair. But his eyes remained the same. Sharp. Watchful. Hungry.

"He's coming tonight," Xiao Chen said. "Wu Chen. He left his compound an hour ago with two guards."

"Where is he now?"

"The Drunken Crane. Meeting with a man called Iron Hand Zhang."

Zhao Feng's hand stilled on the ledger. Iron Hand Zhang. In his past life, that name had belonged to a mid-tier thug who'd risen to control the black market for spirit beast parts. A man with no loyalty, no scruples, and a talent for finding people's weaknesses.

Exactly the kind of person Wu Chen would seek out.

"Did you hear what they discussed?"

Xiao Chen shook his head. "Couldn't get close enough. But I saw Iron Hand pass something to Wu Chen. A scroll. Sealed with red wax."

Red wax. Official business. Or official bribery.

"Good work." Zhao Feng reached into his pouch and tossed Xiao Chen a small bag. Twenty spirit coins—more than most laborers made in a month. "There's a woman at the market named Auntie Lin. Tell her I sent you. She'll teach you to read for half that price."

Xiao Chen caught the bag, his fingers wrapping around it like a lifeline. "You trust her?"

"I trust that she fears me more than she loves money." Zhao Feng closed the ledger. "Stay with her for two hours, then come back. I'll have more work for you tonight."

The boy nodded and disappeared into the darkness.

Zhao Feng stood alone in the warehouse, the lantern casting long shadows across his face. Wu Chen was moving faster than expected. If he'd already contacted Iron Hand Zhang, that meant he was preparing for something violent. Something that required deniable muscle.

The question was: who was the target?

The answer came three hours later, when Xiao Chen returned with blood on his shirt and fire in his eyes.

"They tried to burn the warehouse," the boy said, his voice flat. "Three men. Iron Hand's men. I followed them from the Drunken Crane."

Zhao Feng felt a cold calm settle over him. Of course. Wu Chen wasn't going to negotiate. He wasn't going to threaten. He was going to erase Zhao Feng's operation before it could grow, then pick through the ashes for whatever survived.

"Where are they now?"

"Alley behind the tannery. Waiting for midnight."

"How many?"

"Three. But one of them has a cultivation technique. I saw him shatter a brick with his bare hand."

Qi Condensation, first or second layer. Not a master, but dangerous enough to kill a mortal with a single blow.

"Show me."

Xiao Chen led him through the maze of back alleys, moving with the practiced silence of someone who'd learned to survive by not being seen. They stopped at the corner of a crumbling wall, where a gap in the bricks offered a view of the alley beyond.

Three men crouched in the shadows, their faces half-hidden by hooded cloaks. One of them was bigger than the others, his shoulders broad, his hands wrapped in leather bands. The cultivator. The other two carried short blades and the coiled tension of men who'd killed before.

Zhao Feng studied them for a long moment. Then he smiled.

"Xiao Chen. Do you know what makes a merchant different from a thief?"

The boy frowned. "Money?"

"Risk." Zhao Feng pulled a small pouch from his inner robe. Inside were a dozen spirit stones, each one a deep, vibrant green. High-grade. Worth more than everything in the warehouse combined. "A thief risks his life for small gains. A merchant risks his fortune for empire."

He handed the pouch to Xiao Chen. "Take these to the Drunken Crane. Find a man named Old Qian—he's a gambler who owes me a favor. Tell him to spread a rumor. Say that Zhao Feng has discovered a vein of high-grade spirit stones in the abandoned mine north of the city. Say he's leaving at dawn to secure the claim."

Xiao Chen's eyes widened. "But that's—"

"A lie. Yes." Zhao Feng's smile sharpened. "But Wu Chen doesn't know that. And neither do these three. By the time they realize the truth, I'll have already moved."

"What do I do after?"

"Come back here. Wait for me at the temple. If I'm not there by sunrise, take the coins I gave you and leave the city. Don't look back."

The boy's jaw tightened. For a moment, Zhao Feng saw the ghost of the man Xiao Chen would become—the one who'd thrown himself in front of a blade to buy Zhao Feng an extra breath. The one who'd died with a smile on his face, whispering "I paid my debt."

"Be careful," Xiao Chen said.

"I'm always careful." Zhao Feng turned and walked toward the alley. "That's why I'm still alive."

---

The confrontation happened faster than Zhao Feng had anticipated.

He was halfway across the merchant district when the cultivator stepped out of the shadows, his leather-wrapped hands glowing with faint qi. The other two fanned out behind him, blocking the street in both directions.

"Zhao Feng," the cultivator said. "You're a hard man to find."

"I wasn't hiding." Zhao Feng stopped, keeping his hands visible. "I assume Wu Chen sent you?"

The cultivator's eyes narrowed. "You assume correctly. The master wants to have a word with you. About your recent... acquisitions."

"Tell him I'm busy. I have a mine to inspect at dawn."

"A mine." The cultivator laughed, a harsh sound that echoed off the buildings. "You expect me to believe that?"

"I expect you to believe whatever you want." Zhao Feng reached into his robe, slowly. "But if you kill me, you'll never find out where the real vein is."

The cultivator's confidence flickered. Just for a moment. Just enough.

In that moment, Zhao Feng moved.

He didn't attack. He didn't reach for a weapon. Instead, he threw a handful of spirit coins into the air—dozens of them, glittering in the moonlight, scattering across the street like falling stars.

The two thugs lunged for them without thinking. Instinct. Greed. The same weakness that had always made low-level criminals predictable.

The cultivator didn't move. His eyes stayed locked on Zhao Feng, his qi flaring brighter. "Clever. But I'm not as stupid as they are."

"No. You're worse." Zhao Feng met his gaze. "You're desperate. I can see it in your stance, in the way you hold your qi. You're pushing for the third layer, but you're stuck. You need resources. Techniques. A breakthrough that Wu Chen can't provide."

The cultivator's fist clenched. "You don't know anything about me."

"I know that you're wasting your potential working for a second-rate merchant who'll discard you the moment you stop being useful." Zhao Feng held out his hand. In his palm lay a single spirit stone, pure white with veins of gold running through it. A high-grade qi stone, rare enough to fund a cultivator's breakthrough for months.

"I know that I can give you what he can't."

The cultivator stared at the stone. His breathing quickened. His qi wavered.

"One condition," Zhao Feng said. "Walk away. Tell Wu Chen you couldn't find me. Take this stone and go start a new life somewhere else."

"And if I take the stone and kill you anyway?"

"Then you'll never know about the other nine I have hidden." Zhao Feng smiled. "I'm a merchant, not a fool. I never put all my wealth in one place."

A long silence stretched between them. The two thugs had stopped grabbing coins, watching the standoff with growing unease.

Finally, the cultivator reached out and took the stone. His fingers brushed Zhao Feng's, and for a moment, Zhao Feng felt the rough calluses of a man who'd fought his way up from nothing.

"The other nine," the cultivator said. "Where?"

"Come find me in a month. If you're still alive, I might tell you."

The cultivator's lips twisted into something like a smile. "You're either the bravest mortal I've ever met, or the stupidest."

"I prefer 'most practical.'" Zhao Feng stepped past him, his heart pounding but his face calm. "Good luck with your breakthrough."

He walked away without looking back. Behind him, he heard the cultivator order the thugs to their feet, heard their footsteps retreating in the opposite direction.

The trap had been set. Now he just had to wait for Wu Chen to spring it.

---

Xiao Chen was waiting at the temple, his small frame silhouetted against the broken windows. When he saw Zhao Feng approach, he let out a breath he'd been holding.

"You're alive."

"Disappointed?"

"Relieved." The boy's voice cracked. "Old Qian spread the rumor. It's all over the Drunken Crane now. People are already talking about the mine."

"Good." Zhao Feng sat down on a broken stone bench, exhaustion finally catching up with him. "Now we wait. By morning, Wu Chen will have sent men to the mine. By noon, he'll realize it's a dead end. And by nightfall, he'll be desperate enough to make a mistake."

"What if he doesn't?"

"Then I'll make him one." Zhao Feng closed his eyes. "Wake me in two hours. We have work to do."

The hours passed in fragments of restless sleep and sharp-edged dreams. In one, he saw his past-life sons standing over his dying body, their faces cold and empty. In another, he saw Xiao Chen grown, a young man with a cultivator's bearing and a merchant's cunning.

When he opened his eyes, the moon had shifted and the air had grown cold.

Xiao Chen stood at the temple entrance, his body tense, his hand gripping a rusty dagger.

"Someone's coming," the boy whispered. "Fast. And they're not alone."

Zhao Feng rose, his joints aching, his mind already calculating. If Wu Chen had found them, the plan had failed. If the cultivator had betrayed him, he was dead.

But when the figure emerged from the shadows, it wasn't Wu Chen. It wasn't Iron Hand Zhang.

It was a woman.

She wore the robes of an inner disciple from the Azure Cloud Sect, her hair bound in a topknot, her eyes glowing with a faint silver light. In her hand, she held a jade slip—the kind used to transmit messages across long distances.

"Zhao Feng," she said, her voice carrying the weight of authority. "I bring word from the sect master. Your name has been submitted for consideration in the Immortal Ascension Tournament."

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

"There's been an error," he said carefully. "I'm not a cultivator. I have no cultivation base."

The woman smiled, and it was not a kind smile. "The sect master is aware. That is precisely why he's interested."

She tossed the jade slip to him. He

title: The Shadow in the Market
content:
The morning sun cast long shadows through the merchant district as Zhao Feng made his way toward the central market. Three days had passed since Xiao Chen had taken the pouch. Three days of watching, waiting, and preparing.

The boy had proven useful. More than useful.

Each night, Xiao Chen would slip through the crack in the warehouse wall—a gap so narrow that Zhao Feng himself couldn't fit through—and report what he'd learned. Wu Chen's schedule. His contacts. The way he lingered too long at the teahouse across from the auction house, watching the flow of qi stones with hungry eyes.

But last night's report had been different.

"There's another man," Xiao Chen had said, his voice barely a whisper in the darkness. "Dressed in silk. Comes to see Wu Chen after midnight. They argue."

"What about?"

"Couldn't get close enough. But I heard one name. 'Lin.'"

Zhao Feng's blood had frozen. Lin. The same family that had backed Wu Chen in his past life. The same family that had bought Zhao Feng's first warehouse out from under him, leaving him scrambling for scraps.

They were moving early.

Now, as he walked through the market, Zhao Feng kept his eyes open and his expression neutral. The stalls were busy with morning trade—vegetables, meats, cheap talismans for warding off evil spirits. Nothing of real value. Nothing worth his attention.

He was looking for something else.

The Immortal Ascension Tournament was six months away, but the preparations had already begun. In his past life, he'd learned about the hidden markets that operated beneath the city's surface. Places where forbidden techniques were traded, where cultivators desperate for power sold their services, where information could be bought for the right price.

The entrance was supposed to be in the back of a noodle shop.

He found it easily enough. A faded wooden sign, a stooped old man stirring a pot of broth, the smell of cheap noodles wafting through the air. Nothing remarkable.

"Bowl of noodles," Zhao Feng said, sliding onto a worn stool.

The old man didn't look up. "Five copper coins."

Zhao Feng placed the coins on the counter, but when the old man reached for them, Zhao Feng's hand moved faster. He pressed a second coin—a qi stone, low-grade but real—on top of the coppers.

"I'm looking for the back room," he said quietly.

The old man's eyes flickered up. His gaze was sharp, assessing. "Who sent you?"

"No one. I found my own way."

A long silence. The old man's hand closed around the coins, including the qi stone. "Eat your noodles first. Then walk to the back. Don't look at anyone. Don't stop."

Zhao Feng nodded and ate the bland noodles in silence. When he finished, he stood and walked toward the back of the shop, pushing through a curtain of beads into a narrow hallway.

The hallway led to a door. Behind the door, stairs that descended into darkness.

He counted twenty-seven steps before the stairs ended and a new world opened before him.

The underground market was massive. Torches lined the walls, casting flickering shadows across stalls piled high with spirit herbs, beast cores, and cultivation manuals. The air was thick with the smell of incense and something metallic—blood, maybe, or qi-rich minerals. People moved through the aisles with their heads down, their faces hidden behind hoods and masks.

This was where the real business happened.

Zhao Feng moved slowly, cataloging everything he saw. A stall selling maps of spirit beast territories. Another offering "guaranteed breakthrough" pills that were probably poison. A third displaying spirit swords that gleamed with barely contained power.

He stopped at the fourth stall.

It was unremarkable—just a wooden table covered in a black cloth, with a few scattered manuals on display. But the manuals caught his attention. Not because of their quality, but because of their titles.

*Foundations of Lightning Cultivation.*
*The Thunder Heart Method.*
*Conducting the Heavenly Current.*

In his past life, these exact manuals had been sold to a young woman six months before the tournament. She'd used them to develop techniques that had stunned the entire cultivation world. The major families had fought over her, offering fortunes, land, marriage alliances.

But she'd refused them all. She'd disappeared after the tournament, taking her secrets with her.

Zhao Feng had never learned her name. He'd only heard rumors—a girl from the outer disciples, nobody special, nobody important. Until she'd stepped onto that stage and changed everything.

He reached for the first manual.

"Those aren't for sale."

The voice came from behind the stall. A woman emerged from the shadows, her face partially hidden by a wide-brimmed hat. She was young—maybe twenty-five—with sharp features and eyes that seemed to glow faintly in the torchlight.

"I'm just looking," Zhao Feng said.

"Looking costs nothing. Touching costs your hand."

He pulled his hand back slowly. "Then let's talk. I'm interested in buying."

The woman's lips curved into a thin smile. "You can't afford them."

"Name your price."

"Fifty thousand spirit coins. Each."

Zhao Feng kept his expression neutral, but his mind was racing. Fifty thousand was more than he'd made in the past month combined. More than he had in total assets. But he knew what those manuals were worth. In the right hands, with the right student, they could change the balance of power in the entire region.

"I don't have that kind of money," he said. "But I can offer you something better."

"And what's that?"

"Information."

The woman laughed. It was a cold sound, like ice cracking. "Everyone has information. Few have fifty thousand spirit coins."

"Everyone has information," Zhao Feng agreed. "But not everyone knows that the Lin family is already moving on the Immortal Ascension Tournament. Not everyone knows that they've been buying up the lightning-type techniques for the past three months, trying to corner the market."

The woman's smile faded.

"You're lying."

"Am I? Check with your contacts. See who's been asking questions about lightning cultivation. See who's been visiting the auction houses with Lin family tokens."

He turned to leave.

"Wait."

He stopped.

"Who are you?" the woman asked.

"Someone who wants to make a deal. I can't buy your manuals today. But I can give you something else. Protection."

"Protection from what?"

"From the people who will come looking for you once they know what you have."

The woman studied him for a long moment. Then she reached under the table and pulled out a small jade slip—a storage device, used for transferring information.

"Take this," she said. "It contains the first chapter of the Thunder Heart Method. Proof that I have what I claim."

Zhao Feng took the slip. It was warm in his hand, pulsing with qi.

"Come back when you have something real to offer," the woman said. "Not just words."

He nodded and turned away, slipping the jade into his sleeve. Behind him, he heard the woman's voice again.

"And boy? If you're lying about the Lin family, I'll find you. And I'll make you wish you'd never been born."

Zhao Feng didn't look back.

---

He emerged from the noodle shop into the bright morning light, blinking against the sudden glare. The jade slip was a heavy weight in his sleeve, a promise of power that was still out of reach.

But he had a lead. A connection. A path forward.

He was halfway back to the warehouse when Xiao Chen appeared at his side, materializing from the crowd like a ghost.

"Wu Chen is moving," the boy said, his voice urgent. "He's got men. They're heading for your warehouse."

Zhao Feng's stride didn't falter. "How many?"

"Six. Maybe seven. They're carrying weapons."

"Where is the warehouse guard?"

"Dead. Found him this morning. Throat cut."

Zhao Feng's jaw tightened. He'd hired the guard three days ago—a retired cultivator with decent skills. Not a friend. Not someone he cared about. But dead meant someone was sending a message.

"Take me there. But stay hidden."

They moved through the alleys, keeping to the shadows. When they reached the edge of the merchant district, Zhao Feng stopped and surveyed the warehouse from a distance.

Xiao Chen was right. Seven men surrounded the building, their postures tense, their hands resting on weapons. They weren't trying to be subtle. They wanted to be seen.

And standing in the middle of them, arms crossed, was Wu Chen.

Zhao Feng's old enemy looked younger in this life—fresher, less worn down by years of scheming. But the eyes were the same. Cold. Calculating. Hungry.

"Zhao Feng!" Wu Chen called out, his voice carrying across the empty street. "I know you're watching. Come out. Let's talk."

Zhao Feng considered his options. He could run. Let Wu Chen take the warehouse, the qi stones, everything. Start over somewhere else.

But that wasn't who he was anymore.

He stepped out of the shadows.

"Wu Chen. I wasn't expecting a visit."

Wu Chen smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "I heard you've been doing well for yourself. Making deals. Buying property. Very impressive for a nobody from the outer districts."

"I've had good teachers."

"I'm sure you have." Wu Chen's smile widened. "Which is why I'm here with an offer. A generous one."

"Let's hear it."

"Join me. Work for the Lin family. You've got talent, boy. I can see it. With our resources behind you, you could go far."

"And if I refuse?"

Wu Chen's hand moved, and one of the men pulled out a small leather pouch. He tossed it at Zhao Feng's feet. It landed with a wet thud, and a red stain spread across the dirt.

Inside was a severed finger. A woman's finger, judging by the size. A jade ring still circled the middle knuckle.

Zhao Feng recognized the ring.

It belonged to Mei Ling—the herbalist who had supplied him with his first batch of spirit grass. The only merchant in the district who had treated him fairly.

His hands began to shake.

"That's just a warning," Wu Chen said. "The next one won't be a finger."

Zhao Feng looked up, and in that moment, all pretense of calm fell away. His eyes went cold, his voice dropping to a whisper that carried more threat than any scream.

"Where is she?"

Wu Chen's grin never faltered. "Safe. For now. You have until sunset to make your decision."

He turned and walked away, his men falling into formation around him. They disappeared into the maze of alleys, leaving Zhao Feng alone with a pouch full of blood and a dead guard's body.

Xiao Chen crept up beside him. "What do we do?"

Zhao Feng looked at the severed finger. At the warehouse. At the space where Wu Chen had stood.

"We burn everything," he said quietly. "And then we make him pay."

title: The Serpent Beneath the Floorboards
content:
The lantern light flickered across Zhao Feng's ledger, casting dancing shadows that seemed to writhe like living things. He dipped his brush in ink, the familiar scent of grinding stone and pressed herbs filling the small warehouse.

*The Immortal Ascension Tournament,* he wrote in careful characters. *Six months. Lightning techniques. The woman from the western hills.*

He paused, remembering her face. Lin Xue. She'd been nothing—a servant's daughter who'd scraped together enough merit points to enter the tournament as a lark. When she'd unleashed those first lightning strikes, the elders had nearly fallen off their platforms. By the time she'd summoned the Sixfold Heaven's Wrath technique, they were already drafting contracts.

The major families had fought over her like dogs over meat. But in the end, it was Zhao Feng's own son—his second, Zhao Ming—who had secured her allegiance. He'd promised her resources, protection, a place in the family. And when she'd delivered the location of the Thunder God's Remains, Zhao Ming had taken everything and left her crippled in a ditch.

That was how the Zhao family operated. Take, consume, discard.

The brush snapped in Zhao Feng's grip.

He set down the pieces and took a breath. *Different life. Different path. This time, I find her first.*

A knock at the door pulled him from his thoughts. Three quick raps, a pause, then two more. The signal he'd arranged with Xiao Chen.

"Come."

The boy slipped inside like a shadow, his movements silent despite the creaking floorboards. His eyes were sharp, scanning the room before they settled on Zhao Feng.

"He's coming," Xiao Chen said. "Wu Chen. I followed him from the teahouse. He met with someone—a woman in a green veil. They talked for an hour."

"A woman?"

"I couldn't hear what they said, but he looked nervous when he left. Wiped his hands on his robe like he was sweating." The boy's nose wrinkled. "Rich people sweat different than poor people. Smells worse."

Zhao Feng almost smiled. "Anything else?"

"He's bringing two men. Big ones. They're waiting outside the merchant district now, pretending to browse a weapons stall." Xiao Chen's eyes narrowed. "I don't think this is a friendly visit."

*No. No, it isn't.*

Zhao Feng had known this day would come. Wu Chen was connected—not to a major family, but to the Snake Sect, a minor criminal organization that controlled the city's black market. In his past life, Zhao Feng had crushed them without a second thought. But that was after he'd built his fortune, after he had fifty enforcers at his call.

Now he had a leaking warehouse and a street kid.

"Stay hidden," Zhao Feng said. "Watch through the cracks in the wall. If things go wrong, don't intervene. Run to the East Market and find a man named Iron Ho. Tell him I sent you, that I need the favor repaid."

"What favor?"

"Just tell him. He'll know."

Xiao Chen's jaw tightened, but he nodded and vanished into the shadows behind a stack of crates.

Zhao Feng straightened his robe, checked the small knife hidden in his sleeve, and waited.

---

The door burst open twenty minutes later.

Wu Chen entered first, his round face slick with sweat despite the cool evening air. He wore silk robes that strained at the seams, his fingers heavy with jade rings. Behind him, two men ducked through the doorway—bruisers with scarred knuckles and dead eyes.

"Brother Zhao!" Wu Chen's voice boomed, too loud for the small space. "I hope I'm not interrupting your... what would you call this? Inventory?"

"Business," Zhao Feng said calmly. "Just as you're conducting business by bringing guests without notice."

Wu Chen's smile didn't reach his eyes. "Ah, but these are my associates. Very interested in what you've been selling. You see, I've heard rumors that you've been moving qi stones at prices that don't match the market average. I told them, 'That can't be right. Brother Zhao is a newcomer, but he's not stupid.' But they insisted on seeing for themselves."

The bigger of the two bruisers stepped forward, cracking his knuckles. "Open the crates."

Zhao Feng didn't move. "I don't recall offering you tea."

The bruiser's face reddened. "Listen, you little—"

"Patience, patience." Wu Chen waved a hand, still smiling. "Brother Zhao, I'm not here to cause trouble. I'm here to offer you a partnership. The Snake Sect has been very generous to merchants who show... flexibility. We provide protection, access to exclusive markets, and we ask only for a small percentage of your earnings."

"How small?"

"Thirty percent."

Zhao Feng laughed.

It wasn't a polite laugh. It was the laugh of a man who had heard something genuinely absurd. It echoed off the warehouse walls, and for a moment, even the bruisers looked uncertain.

Wu Chen's smile hardened. "Something funny?"

"Thirty percent," Zhao Feng repeated, wiping his eyes. "You want thirty percent of my earnings, and in return, you'll protect me from the same thugs you're threatening me with right now. That's a beautiful arrangement. Truly. I almost admire the simplicity of it."

"Are you refusing?"

"I'm saying your offer is insulting." Zhao Feng stepped around the table, moving closer to Wu Chen. The bruisers tensed, but he stopped just out of arm's reach. "Let me make you a counteroffer. You leave now. You tell your superiors that Zhao Feng is not interested in partnerships. And you forget you ever saw this warehouse."

Wu Chen's smile vanished entirely. "You think you can threaten me? I've been doing business in this city since before you were born, boy. I have connections you can't imagine. I can have your license revoked. I can have your stock seized. I can have you found floating in the river with your throat cut."

"Three things," Zhao Feng said, his voice dropping low. "First, I'm not a boy. Second, if my stock is seized, I know exactly which officials to bribe to get it back—and I know exactly how much they charge. Third, and most importantly..." He reached into his robe and pulled out a folded piece of paper. "I know about the girl in the Green Lotus Brothel. The one with the birthmark on her shoulder. The one you've been hiding from your wife."

Wu Chen went pale.

"She's pregnant," Zhao Feng continued. "Your wife finds out, she'll take you for everything you have in the divorce. Your father-in-law is on the city council. He'll make sure you never work in this district again. And the Snake Sect? They'll cut you loose the moment you're not useful."

"How—" Wu Chen's voice cracked. "How do you know about that?"

"I know a lot of things." Zhao Feng folded the paper and tucked it away. "I know that your third cousin is in debt to the Zhao family's loan sharks. I know that you've been skimming from the Snake Sect's accounts for the past six months. And I know that if you don't walk out that door right now and never come back, I will make sure every piece of information I have finds its way to the right ears."

The bruisers looked at Wu Chen, waiting for orders.

Wu Chen's hands trembled. For a long moment, he just stood there, his face cycling through colors—red, white, purple. Then, slowly, he turned.

"Let's go."

"Boss—"

"I said let's go!"

The bruisers followed him out, shooting dark looks at Zhao Feng as they left. The door slammed shut, and then there was silence.

Zhao Feng let out a breath he hadn't realized he was holding.

Xiao Chen emerged from the shadows, his eyes wide. "That was... that was incredible. How did you know all that?"

"Research." Zhao Feng walked to the table and poured himself a cup of cold tea. His hand only shook slightly. "Information is the most valuable commodity in the world, Xiao Chen. Money comes and goes. Power rises and falls. But knowing things that other people don't want you to know? That lasts forever."

"But how did you find out? You've only been in the city for a month."

Zhao Feng didn't answer. He couldn't explain that he'd known Wu Chen in his past life—that the man had been a minor nuisance, easily crushed, but that Zhao Feng had made it a habit to learn everyone's secrets. Just in case.

"Wu Chen won't stay scared forever," Zhao Feng said instead. "He'll go back to the Snake Sect, and they'll send someone smarter. Someone who won't be so easy to intimidate."

"Then what do we do?"

"We accelerate the timeline." Zhao Feng opened his ledger and flipped to a page marked with a red seal. "The Immortal Ascension Tournament is in six months. But there's another event happening sooner. A gathering of rogue cultivators at the Black Market Auction, three weeks from now."

"Auction?"

"A rogue cultivator found a map to an ancient tomb. He's selling copies. The major families don't know about it yet—they think it's just a rumor." Zhao Feng's eyes gleamed. "But I know it's real. And I know what's inside."

Xiao Chen leaned forward. "What?"

"Seeds," Zhao Feng said. "The seeds of the Lightning Bamboo, guarded by a remnant formation. The same bamboo that, when properly cultivated, produces the techniques that Lin Xue used to win the tournament."

He closed the ledger. "We need to get there first. We need to buy those map copies before anyone else knows they exist."

"How much will they cost?"

"Everything we have. And more."

Xiao Chen's face fell. "But we don't have more."

"No. We don't." Zhao Feng stared at the lantern flame, watching it dance and flicker. "Which means we need to find another way. A way to get money that doesn't involve selling our stock."

"What kind of way?"

Zhao Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a small, carved token—a serpent coiled around a flame, the symbol of the Snake Sect. He'd lifted it from Wu Chen during their confrontation, a trick he'd perfected over forty years of dealing with untrustworthy men.

"The Snake Sect has a vault," he said slowly. "Hidden beneath the gambling den on Serpent Street. And I know the combination."

"You want to rob them?"

"No." Zhao Feng's smile was cold, sharp, and utterly without mercy. "I want to make them an offer they can't refuse. One that ends with them paying me to take their money."

The lantern sputtered.

Outside, the night grew dark, and somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Weight of Whispers

The night air carried the stench of fish and brine from the nearby docks. Zhao Feng sat cross-legged on a crate of low-grade qi stones, the lantern casting long shadows across his ledger. The numbers danced before his eyes—figures that told a story of modest beginnings, but not yet of empire.

A knock came at the warehouse door. Three sharp raps, then two soft ones. The signal.

Zhao Feng rose, his muscles tense. He opened the door to find Xiao Chen slipping through the gap like a shadow, his thin frame barely visible in the darkness. The boy's eyes were wide, his breath coming in short gasps.

"He's not alone," Xiao Chen whispered.

Zhao Feng's hand drifted to the dagger hidden beneath his robes. "Wu Chen?"

"No. Someone else. A cultivator." Xiao Chen's voice trembled. "I followed Wu Chen from the Jade Pavilion. He met with a man wearing blue silk—the kind with silver thread woven through it. They talked for an hour in a private room."

Blue silk with silver thread. The uniform of the Cloud Serpent Sect's outer disciples.

Zhao Feng's mind raced. The Cloud Serpent Sect was one of the minor sects in the region, but even a minor sect cultivator could crush a mortal merchant like an ant. If Wu Chen had aligned himself with them, this was worse than Zhao Feng had anticipated.

"Did you hear what they said?"

Xiao Chen nodded, his face pale. "They know about your qi stones. The cultivator said you were 'an interesting variable' that needed to be 'removed before the tournament.' Wu Chen agreed. He said he'd handle it tonight."

Tonight.

Zhao Feng looked around the warehouse. His stockpile. His future. Everything he had built in the past two months was sitting in these crates. And now, a Cloud Serpent cultivator wanted him dead.

"How long do we have?"

"Wu Chen was gathering men when I left. Maybe an hour. Maybe less."

An hour. Not enough time to move everything. Not enough time to find help. Not enough time to do anything except make a choice.

Zhao Feng's mind flashed to his past life. He'd faced worse odds. He'd lost everything and rebuilt it a dozen times. But back then, he'd had connections, money, power. Now he had a leaking warehouse, a street rat, and a pile of rocks.

"Help me move the ledger and the high-grade stones," he said, his voice calm. "Everything else stays."

Xiao Chen stared at him. "But—"

"If we try to move everything, we'll be caught. Better to save what matters and live to fight another day." Zhao Feng grabbed a bag and began stuffing it with the highest quality qi stones. "There's a safe house two streets over. An old apothecary shop that's been closed for years. I bought it last week."

"You planned for this?"

"I plan for everything."

They worked in silence, the minutes ticking by like drops of blood from a wound. Xiao Chen moved with surprising speed, his small hands deftly packing the spirit grass and the most valuable stones. Within twenty minutes, they had two bags filled with the core of Zhao Feng's wealth.

Then the door splintered.

Wood exploded inward as a figure in blue silk stepped through the gap. He was young, maybe twenty, with a sharp face and eyes that held the cold arrogance of someone who had never known failure. In his hand, a sword glowed with pale blue light.

"Zhao Feng," the cultivator said, his voice smooth as oil. "I was hoping to catch you here."

Zhao Feng straightened, placing himself between the cultivator and Xiao Chen. "And you are?"

"Senior Disciple Liu of the Cloud Serpent Sect." The man smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I've heard interesting things about you, merchant. A nobody who suddenly appears with a fortune in qi stones. A mortal who deals in cultivation resources as if he has a thousand years of experience."

"I'm flattered."

"Don't be." Liu stepped forward, his sword still glowing. "I'm here to offer you a choice. Give me everything you have, and I'll let you live. Refuse, and I'll take it anyway, then burn this place to the ground with you inside."

Zhao Feng's hand tightened on the dagger. He was no match for a cultivator. Even a low-level one like Liu could kill him with a flick of his wrist. But Zhao Feng had something Liu didn't have: forty years of understanding how the cultivation world truly worked.

"Before I decide," Zhao Feng said, his voice steady, "I have a question. Who told you about me? Was it Wu Chen? Or someone higher up?"

Liu's smile widened. "Why does it matter?"

"Because if Wu Chen was the one who brought you here, then you're working for a desperate man. And desperate men make mistakes." Zhao Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade slip, holding it up. "Did Wu Chen tell you about this?"

Liu's eyes narrowed. "What is that?"

"A record. Every transaction I've made since I started. Every person I've dealt with. And a list of names—including yours, Senior Disciple Liu, and the exact amount you owe to a certain gambling den in the eastern district."

The cultivator's face went pale.

"You see," Zhao Feng continued, "I make it a point to know my enemies before they know me. You owe fifty thousand spirit coins to the Golden Lotus Gambling House. Your sect doesn't know. Your master doesn't know. But I do."

Liu's sword trembled. "That's a lie."

"Is it? Then why are you shaking?" Zhao Feng took a step forward, his voice dropping. "I'm offering you a different deal, Senior Disciple. Walk away. Tell Wu Chen you couldn't find me. And in return, I'll forget I ever saw your name on that list."

For a long moment, the only sound was the creaking of the warehouse walls and the distant cry of gulls. Liu's face cycled through rage, fear, and calculation. His sword flickered, the light dimming.

"You're bluffing," he finally said.

"I'm really not." Zhao Feng held up the jade slip. "This is going to a very curious sect elder if anything happens to me tonight. You know how the Cloud Serpent Sect feels about gambling debts. They'd expel you. Maybe worse."

Liu's jaw clenched. He looked at the bags of qi stones, then at Zhao Feng, then back at the bags. The greed in his eyes warred with the fear.

"Fine," he spat. "You live tonight. But if that information ever surfaces—"

"It won't, as long as you keep your end of the bargain." Zhao Feng stepped aside, gesturing toward the broken door. "Now, if you'll excuse me, I have business to attend to."

Liu glared at him, then turned and stalked out into the night. His footsteps faded into the darkness, leaving only the sound of Xiao Chen's ragged breathing.

The boy let out a shaky exhale. "That was... close."

"Too close." Zhao Feng grabbed the bags. "We need to move. Now."

They slipped out the back entrance, into the maze of alleys that wound through the merchant district. The moon was hidden behind clouds, casting the streets into deep shadow. Zhao Feng led the way, his feet remembering paths he'd walked in another life.

The old apothecary shop stood at the end of a dead-end alley, its windows boarded up and its sign hanging crooked. Zhao Feng produced a key and unlocked the door, ushering Xiao Chen inside.

The interior smelled of dust and dried herbs. Shelves lined the walls, empty except for a few cracked jars. But in the back room, hidden behind a false wall, was a small chamber with a bed, a table, and a locking chest.

Zhao Feng set down the bags and lit a candle. "We'll stay here until things cool down. Then I need to find out who else is pulling Wu Chen's strings."

Xiao Chen sat on the bed, his legs dangling over the edge. "You think there's someone else?"

"I know there is." Zhao Feng opened the chest and began transferring the qi stones inside. "Wu Chen doesn't have the authority to hire a Cloud Serpent cultivator. Someone with real power is backing him. Someone who wants me dead before the tournament."

"Who?"

Zhao Feng paused, a single qi stone in his hand. In his past life, there had been a man who'd tried to destroy him during the Immortal Ascension Tournament. A man from the Zhao family—his own nephew, Zhao Lei, who'd seen him as a threat to the family's power.

But that was in another life. In this one, Zhao Lei was still a child, barely ten years old. It couldn't be him.

"I don't know yet," he admitted. "But I will."

A knock came from the front of the shop.

Both of them froze. Zhao Feng's hand went to his dagger again. Xiao Chen slid off the bed, his eyes wide.

"Stay here," Zhao Feng whispered. "Don't make a sound."

He crept through the dark shop, his heart pounding. The knock came again—three sharp raps, then two soft ones. The signal.

But only two people knew that signal. Him and Xiao Chen.

He reached the door and peered through a crack in the boards. A figure stood in the alley, wrapped in a dark cloak. The face was hidden, but the posture was familiar. Confident. Waiting.

Zhao Feng unlocked the door and opened it a crack.

"Who are you?"

The figure lowered the hood, revealing a woman's face—sharp features, dark eyes, and a scar that ran from her temple to her jaw. She was young, maybe twenty-five, but her eyes held the weight of someone much older.

"Someone who wants to make you an offer," she said, her voice low and rough. "I know what you're planning, Zhao Feng. The tournament. The hidden techniques. The girl who can control lightning."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"You will." She reached into her cloak and pulled out a jade slip, identical to the one he'd shown Liu. "I have information you need. In exchange, I want your help. There's a sect that needs to fall, and you're the only one who can make it happen."

"Which sect?"

Her smile was cold. "The Cloud Serpent Sect."

The candle behind him flickered and died, plunging them both into darkness. When Zhao Feng's eyes adjusted, the woman was gone, leaving only the jade slip lying on the ground where she'd stood.

He picked it up, his hands trembling. Inside the slip, he could sense a single message, pressed into the jade with spiritual energy:

*"They killed my family. Help me destroy them, and I'll give you the tournament."*

Beneath the words, a name: *Lin Xue.*

Zhao Feng's breath caught. Lin Xue. The girl who'd won the Immortal Ascension Tournament. The nobody who'd shocked the world with her lightning techniques.

She was standing in his alley, asking for his help.

And she knew exactly who he was.

title: The Snake in the Garden
content:
The ledger blurred before Zhao Feng's eyes. He'd been calculating for three hours, cross-referencing prices, mapping supply routes, and estimating the capital he'd need to corner the low-grade qi stone market before the Immortal Ascension Tournament.

His plan was solid. In six months, when the tournament began, demand for qi stones would skyrocket. Every outer disciple would be scrambling to buy cultivation resources. Every merchant would raise prices. But Zhao Feng intended to own the supply chain before any of them realized what was happening.

The problem was capital.

His current stockpile was worth ten thousand spirit coins. He needed at least fifty thousand to execute his plan. And he needed it within three months.

The creak of the warehouse door made him look up.

Wu Chen entered with the practiced silence of a man who'd spent years moving through shadows. He was shorter than Zhao Feng remembered from his past life, with a weasel-like face and eyes that never stopped moving. Behind him, two enforcers waited in the darkness, their cultivation auras carefully suppressed.

"Brother Zhao," Wu Chen said, his voice oily and warm. "I hope I'm not disturbing your evening calculations."

"You're always disturbing something," Zhao Feng replied, closing his ledger. "What do you want, Wu Chen?"

"Straight to business. I appreciate that." Wu Chen pulled up a crate and sat, crossing his legs with the ease of a man who believed he had all the time in the world. "I've been watching your operation with great interest. You've moved a surprising amount of product in a very short time. Impressive, considering you started with nothing."

"I had a good teacher."

"Did you?" Wu Chen's smile didn't reach his eyes. "I heard you were just a porter at the Azure Pavilion. Then suddenly you have capital, you have connections, and you're buying up low-grade qi stones like a man who knows something the rest of us don't."

Zhao Feng kept his face neutral. Inside, his mind raced. Wu Chen was fishing. But how much did he know? In his past life, Wu Chen had been a minor player, a fence who dealt in stolen goods and information. He'd been useful precisely because he was forgettable.

But forgettable men didn't track new merchants to their warehouses at night.

"I'm flattered by your attention," Zhao Feng said. "But I'm just a small trader. Trying to make a living."

"Small traders don't buy three hundred qi stones in a single week." Wu Chen leaned forward. "Small traders don't know the exact moment to buy when prices bottom out. Small traders don't move like they've seen the future."

Ice crawled down Zhao Feng's spine.

This was wrong. In his past life, no one had noticed him this early. He'd been beneath notice, a nobody trading in scraps. But his actions had been different this time. He'd been bolder, faster, more aggressive. And the wolves had taken notice.

"I have good instincts," Zhao Feng said carefully. "Nothing more."

"Instincts." Wu Chen laughed, a sound like grinding stones. "Let me tell you what I think, Brother Zhao. I think you have information. I think someone is feeding you intelligence about the markets. And I think that intelligence is going to make someone very rich."

He paused, letting the silence stretch.

"I want in."

Zhao Feng didn't answer immediately. He reached for the kettle he'd left heating on a small brazier and poured two cups of tea. The gesture gave him time to think.

Wu Chen wasn't offering a partnership. He was making a threat disguised as an invitation. If Zhao Feng refused, the information about his sudden rise would spread to the major families. They would investigate. They would find questions that had no good answers.

"I don't need a partner," Zhao Feng said finally. "I have everything I need."

"Everyone needs partners. Even the strong need allies." Wu Chen accepted the tea but didn't drink. "Think of it as insurance. You let me take a small percentage of your profits, and I make sure certain... questions don't get asked."

"And if I refuse your generous offer?"

Wu Chen's smile vanished. "Then I start asking those questions myself. To the Shen family. To the Li family. To everyone who might be interested in a nobody merchant who suddenly knows too much."

The threat hung in the air like smoke.

Zhao Feng's hand moved to his storage ring, where he kept a talisman for emergency defense. But fighting was the last resort. He couldn't kill Wu Chen without drawing attention, and he couldn't let him leave without an answer.

"I'll give you an answer in three days," Zhao Feng said.

"Three days?"

"I need to review my books. Calculate what percentage I can afford to give without destroying my operation." Zhao Feng stood, signaling the meeting was over. "Come back at midnight. Three days from now. I'll have an answer ready."

Wu Chen studied him for a long moment. Then he rose, brushing imaginary dust from his robes.

"Three days," he agreed. "But Brother Zhao? Don't try to run. I have eyes everywhere in this district. And those eyes will find you long before you reach the city gates."

He left without drinking his tea.

Zhao Feng waited until the footsteps faded before he let out the breath he'd been holding. His hands were shaking. Not from fear, but from rage.

In his past life, men like Wu Chen had been nothing. Ants beneath his notice. But now, this ant had the power to destroy everything he was building.

He needed to deal with Wu Chen. Permanently.

But killing him would bring the city guard. It would bring the major families. It would bring questions he couldn't answer.

He needed a different solution.

---

Xiao Chen found him an hour later, slipping through a crack in the warehouse wall that Zhao Feng hadn't noticed. The boy moved like a ghost, his bare feet making no sound on the dusty floor.

"The weasel came to see you," Xiao Chen said. It wasn't a question.

"You were watching?"

"I followed him for two days, like you said. He's not just a fence." The boy's voice dropped. "He works for someone. A woman. I couldn't get close enough to hear her name, but she's important. Big house. Lots of guards. And she's been meeting with the Shen family."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold.

The Shen family. His first target for the qi stone market corner. The family he'd planned to bankrupt in the tournament's aftermath.

If Wu Chen was connected to them, then everything he'd said tonight was a test. A probe to see how Zhao Feng would react.

"Did you see her face?"

"No. She wears a veil. But I saw her hands." Xiao Chen held up his own, mimicking the gesture. "She has a scar. Three lines, like claw marks, across her right wrist."

Zhao Feng's mind exploded with recognition.

The Three-Clawed Woman. A legendary figure from his past life, a cultivator who'd been crippled during a failed breakthrough and turned to information brokering. She'd been one of the most dangerous people in the underworld, controlling a network of spies that stretched across three provinces.

And she was interested in him.

"Why?" he muttered, pacing the warehouse floor. "Why would she care about a small-time merchant?"

"The same reason everyone cares," Xiao Chen said. "You're doing things you shouldn't be able to do. People notice."

The boy was right. Zhao Feng had been too aggressive. Too knowledgeable. He'd moved like a man who'd already won, and the predators had taken notice.

He stopped pacing.

"I need to change my approach."

"What do you mean?"

"I mean I've been acting like a merchant prince when I'm still a beggar with a few coins." He turned to face Xiao Chen. "I need to become invisible again. Let them think I'm nothing. Let them forget about me."

"How?"

Zhao Feng's eyes drifted to the crates of qi stones. In his past life, he'd learned a technique that could hide one's cultivation level, making a cultivator appear weaker than they truly were. But that wasn't what he needed.

He needed to hide his success. Make it look like failure.

"We're going to lose money," he said. "Publicly. Dramatically. We're going to make it look like I made a series of terrible investments and lost everything."

"And then?"

"And then I disappear. Let the Three-Clawed Woman think I'm broken. Let Wu Chen think there's nothing left to squeeze." Zhao Feng's voice hardened. "And while they're laughing at my failure, I'll be building something they can't see coming."

Xiao Chen nodded slowly. "When do we start?"

"Tomorrow. I need you to spread a rumor. Tell the street kids that you saw me crying. That I was drinking alone. That I'm losing everything."

The boy's eyes widened. "You want me to lie about you?"

"I want you to tell the truth about a lie." Zhao Feng pulled out a pouch of coins and tossed it to him. "Make it convincing. Your life depends on it."

Xiao Chen caught the pouch and slipped it into his rags. "And if someone asks why I'm helping you?"

"Because I paid you. That's all they need to know." Zhao Feng walked to the door and opened it, letting the cold night air rush in. "Go. Start the whisper. By dawn, I want the entire district to think Zhao Feng is finished."

The boy vanished into the darkness without another word.

Zhao Feng stood in the doorway, watching the stars wheel overhead. In his past life, he'd learned that the greatest weapon wasn't strength or wealth.

It was what people believed.

Let them believe he was weak. Let them believe he was broken. Let them write him off as another failed merchant who reached too high.

When the Immortal Ascension Tournament came, they would learn the truth.

But first, he had to survive the night.

He closed the door and returned to his calculations, but his mind kept drifting to the scarred woman. The Three-Clawed Woman. In his past life, she'd died during a power struggle between the major families, her network scattered to the winds. But that was thirty years from now.

In the present, she was alive. Connected. Dangerous.

And she had her eyes on him.

Zhao Feng sharpened his quill and began writing a new plan. A darker plan. One that involved blood and fire and the careful destruction of everyone who threatened what he was building.

He wrote until his hand cramped, until the lantern oil ran low, until the first gray light of dawn crept through the cracks in the walls.

Then he burned the pages and watched the ashes scatter in the wind.

The game had changed.

And Zhao Feng intended to win.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Snake in the Grass

Three days passed in a blur of negotiations and deliveries. Zhao Feng had established a rhythm—mornings at the warehouse, afternoons cultivating in the abandoned temple, evenings reviewing Xiao Chen's reports. The boy had proven himself invaluable, his street instincts sharp enough to spot a lie from three blocks away.

Wu Chen, the merchant who'd approached Zhao Feng about a "partnership," had been easy enough to read. He wanted the qi stones. He wanted them cheap. And he wanted Zhao Feng out of the way afterward. Xiao Chen had followed him to a teahouse in the inner district, where Wu Chen had met with three men from the Liu family—one of the eight major merchant houses that controlled the city's trade.

The Liu family. In his past life, they had been minor players, content to let others fight for scraps while they quietly consolidated power. By the time Zhao Feng had reached the height of his wealth, the Liu family had become his most dangerous competitor. Their patriarch, Liu Wei, had been the one to suggest Zhao Feng's sons turn against him.

*That snake is already moving,* Zhao Feng thought as he arranged crates in the warehouse. *He's testing me, seeing if I'll fold.*

He wouldn't.

The warehouse door creaked open. Zhao Feng's hand moved instinctively to the dagger hidden in his sleeve.

"It's me," Xiao Chen said, slipping through the gap. The boy's face was pale, his eyes wide. "Something's happened."

"Speak."

"There's a girl. In the alley behind the temple. She's hurt."

Zhao Feng's jaw tightened. "That's not our concern."

"She's wearing a sect robe. Azure Sky Sect."

The words hit him like a physical blow. The Azure Sky Sect was one of the major cultivation sects in the region, their influence stretching across three provinces. In his past life, they had been the ones to sponsor the Immortal Ascension Tournament.

And the young woman who'd won that tournament—the nobody who'd shocked everyone—had been an Azure Sky disciple.

"Take me to her."

---

The alley behind the temple was dark, the only light coming from a sliver of moon overhead. The girl lay crumpled against the wall, her white robe stained with blood and mud. Even in the dim light, Zhao Feng could see the injuries—a gash across her ribs, a bruise swelling on her temple, her left arm bent at an unnatural angle.

She was barely breathing.

Xiao Chen stood guard at the mouth of the alley, his eyes scanning the shadows. "No one followed her. I made sure."

Zhao Feng crouched beside the girl. Her face was young—seventeen, maybe eighteen—with delicate features that would have been pretty if not for the blood. Her cultivation base was weak, barely at the second stage of Qi Condensation.

*This isn't her,* he realized. *The tournament winner was a lightning cultivator at the peak of Foundation Establishment. This girl is nothing.*

But she wore the same sect robe. She had the same dark hair, the same build.

"Help me lift her," Zhao Feng said.

Xiao Chen hesitated. "She could be a trap."

"She could be. But she's also a chance."
