They carried her to the temple, laying her on a pile of straw near the altar. Zhao Feng worked quickly, cleaning her wounds with water from his canteen, setting her arm with a splint made from broken pews. His hands moved with practiced efficiency, muscle memory from a lifetime of treating injuries—both his own and others'.

The girl stirred as he finished binding her ribs. Her eyes fluttered open, unfocused and terrified.

"Where—" She coughed, blood flecking her lips. "Who are you?"

"Someone who found you in an alley. What happened?"

Her face contorted with pain. "They came at night. The sect—they destroyed everything. Elder Lin—he betrayed us. He opened the gates for the demon cultists."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold. "The Azure Sky Sect was attacked?"

"Wiped out." Tears streamed down her face. "I ran. I ran for three days. They're still hunting the survivors."

*Impossible.* In his past life, the Azure Sky Sect had been one of the pillars of the cultivation world. They had survived wars, plagues, and internal purges. They were supposed to be unshakeable.

But his past life had also been a lie. His sons had hidden their betrayal for years before striking. What else had he missed?

"How many survivors?" he asked.

"I don't know. A few dozen, maybe. They scattered." She grabbed his arm, her grip surprisingly strong. "Please. I need to get to the capital. I have to warn the Grand Council."

"The capital is two weeks away. You won't make it in your condition."

"Then I'll die trying." Her eyes blazed with desperate determination. "The demon cult isn't finished. They're coming for the other sects. They're coming for everyone."

Xiao Chen stepped forward. "Boss, we should leave. If the demon cult is hunting her, they'll track her here."

"Not yet." Zhao Feng studied the girl's face, searching for any sign of deception. Her aura was steady, her fear genuine. But in a world built on lies, even genuine fear could be a weapon. "What's your name?"

"Lin Xue."

*Lin.* The same surname as the elder who'd betrayed her. A coincidence, or something more?

"I'm going to help you," Zhao Feng said slowly. "But I need something in return."

Her eyes narrowed. "What could a street merchant possibly want from me?"

"Information. Everything you know about the Azure Sky Sect—their techniques, their treasures, their secrets. And when you recover, you'll owe me a favor."

"That's extortion."

"That's business."

She stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a bitter, broken sound that echoed through the empty temple. "Fine. I accept. It's not like I have anything left to lose anyway."

---

The next morning, Zhao Feng woke to find Xiao Chen gone.

*No. Not again.*

He searched the temple, the surrounding streets, the warehouse. Nothing. The boy had vanished without a trace.

Lin Xue was still unconscious, her breathing shallow but steady. Zhao Feng left her with water and a note, then set out to find his informant.

The underworld of Greencloud City had its own rules, its own hierarchy. In his past life, Zhao Feng had mastered them all. He knew which beggars were actually spies, which merchants ran illegal gambling rings, which teahouses served as information exchanges.

He started at the Drunken Dragon, a tavern in the slums where the dregs of society gathered to drink away their sorrows. The owner, a one-eyed man named Iron Ho, recognized him immediately.

"Zhao Feng. Heard you've been making moves." Iron Ho wiped a glass with a rag that had never been clean. "Looking for something?"

"A boy. Dark hair, thin, about this tall." Zhao Feng held his hand at chest level. "He was with me last night. Now he's gone."

Iron Ho's eye narrowed. "Heard about a kid matching that description. Saw him being dragged into the Liu compound an hour ago."

The Liu family. They'd taken Xiao Chen.

"Why?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" Iron Ho leaned forward. "Word is, the Lius are consolidating. They've been buying up debts, calling in favors, squeezing out the smaller merchants. You've been making noise, Zhao Feng. Noise attracts attention."

*I've been too careful,* Zhao Feng realized. *But not careful enough.*

He'd underestimated how quickly the major families would notice him. In his past life, he'd been invisible for years before anyone paid attention. But that was because he'd started with nothing, worked his way up slowly. This time, he'd moved too fast, accumulated too much too quickly.

The Lius had seen a threat and decided to remove it.

"Where in the compound?"

"Eastern wing. They have a holding cell for debtors." Iron Ho's eye flickered with something that might have been sympathy. "I wouldn't try to get him back if I were you. The Lius have cultivators on their payroll. You're just a merchant."

*A merchant with forty years of knowledge,* Zhao Feng thought. *And a burning desire for vengeance.*

He turned and walked out of the tavern, his mind racing through possibilities. Direct confrontation was suicide. Bribery would take too long. He needed leverage—something the Lius valued more than Xiao Chen's life.

Then he remembered.

In his past life, the Liu family had risen to power through a single deal: a secret trade route through the Demon Wastes, connecting the human kingdoms to the spirit beast markets of the eastern plains. It had made them a fortune, but it had also made them enemies. The other major families had never discovered the route's location.

But Zhao Feng had.

He'd stumbled upon it by accident, chasing a debtor who'd fled into the wastes. The path was treacherous, guarded by ancient formations and territorial beasts. But it existed.

And if the Lius found out he knew about it, they'd do anything to keep it secret.

*Including returning Xiao Chen alive.*

Zhao Feng smiled, the expression cold and sharp as a blade.

*Time to play the snake's game.*

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Thread That Binds

The night air carried the scent of rain and rotting wood. Zhao Feng stood in the doorway of his warehouse, watching the shadows pool between the buildings. His fingers drummed against the doorframe—a habit he'd picked up in his past life, when waiting meant money lost or lives ruined.

Wu Chen was late.

Not by much. Fifteen minutes. But in Zhao Feng's experience, men who were late to first meetings were either testing you or planning to betray you. Sometimes both.

He turned back inside and checked his ledger one more time. The numbers were clean. His stockpile was organized. Every qi stone, every bundle of spirit grass, every vial of low-grade elixir had been accounted for. If Wu Chen wanted to do business, Zhao Feng was ready.

If Wu Chen wanted something else...

His hand drifted to the dagger hidden in his sleeve. Another lesson from his past life: never meet a new contact unarmed.

The footsteps came at last—soft, deliberate, the walk of a man who didn't want to be heard but knew he would be anyway. Zhao Feng looked up as a silhouette filled the doorway.

Wu Chen was shorter than he remembered. In his past life, the man had seemed larger, more imposing. But that was before Zhao Feng had learned to see through appearances. Now he noticed the slight hunch in Wu Chen's shoulders, the way his eyes darted left and right before settling on Zhao Feng.

"Brother Zhao," Wu Chen said, his voice oily and smooth. "I apologize for the delay. Business kept me."

"Business always keeps us," Zhao Feng replied, gesturing to a chair he'd set up near the table. "Please. Sit."

Wu Chen didn't sit immediately. He walked around the warehouse, examining the crates, running his fingers along the bundles of spirit grass. His eyes lingered on the scale, the ledger, the organized stacks of qi stones.

"You've done well for yourself," Wu Chen said, finally taking the chair. "They say you started with nothing. A few spirit coins and a dream."

"They say many things," Zhao Feng said, taking the seat across from him. "I prefer to let my work speak."

"Wise. Very wise." Wu Chen leaned forward, his hands clasped together. "I'll be direct, Brother Zhao. I represent certain... interests. Interests that could be very beneficial to someone in your position."

"What kind of interests?"

"The kind that require discretion. And loyalty." Wu Chen's smile didn't reach his eyes. "The merchant guild has grown stagnant. The old families control everything. But there are those of us who believe the future belongs to those bold enough to seize it."

Zhao Feng kept his expression neutral. In his past life, he'd heard similar speeches a hundred times. They always ended the same way—with someone dead, someone betrayed, and the speaker walking away with the spoils.

"I'm listening," he said.

"Good. We need a supplier. Someone who can move goods without attracting attention. Someone who understands the value of... unconventional resources." Wu Chen reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade slip. He placed it on the table between them. "This contains a list. Study it. If you can procure what's on that list, you'll be well compensated."

Zhao Feng didn't touch the slip. "What kind of resources?"

"Cultivation aids. Spiritual herbs. Certain... tools that the guild has deemed restricted." Wu Chen's smile widened. "Nothing illegal, of course. Merely... unregulated."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

Zhao Feng knew what he was being asked. In his past life, he'd refused similar offers, and he'd watched those who accepted them rise to power while he struggled. But he'd also watched them fall—their empires crumbling when the sect investigators finally caught up.

"I'll consider it," Zhao Feng said.

"Consider quickly. The opportunity won't last." Wu Chen stood, his movements fluid and practiced. "I'll return in three days for your answer. And Brother Zhao?"

"Yes?"

"Don't keep me waiting."

He left without another word, his footsteps fading into the night. Zhao Feng sat motionless, his eyes fixed on the jade slip.

The trap was obvious. Wu Chen was testing him, seeing if he was desperate enough to take the bait. But what wasn't obvious was who Wu Chen really worked for. In his past life, the man had been a minor player, a messenger for larger powers. But which power? The merchant guild's black market faction? One of the major families? Or someone else entirely?

Zhao Feng picked up the jade slip and pressed it to his forehead. The information flowed into his mind: a list of herbs, minerals, and artifacts, each one more restricted than the last. Blood Orchid. Shadow Root. A fragment of what appeared to be a formation array for suppressing spiritual energy.

His blood ran cold.

The last item wasn't just restricted. It was forbidden. The kind of thing that got you executed by sect enforcers, no trial, no appeal.

Wu Chen wasn't testing him. He was setting him up.

The door creaked. Zhao Feng's hand shot to his dagger, but it was only Xiao Chen, slipping through the gap like a shadow. The boy's face was pale, his clothes damp with night dew.

"He's gone," Xiao Chen said. "I followed him to the south gate. He met someone there."

"Who?"

"I don't know. Tall man, dark robes. They only spoke for a moment, but Wu Chen gave him something. A pouch, like the one you gave me." Xiao Chen's eyes were sharp, calculating. "I couldn't get close enough to hear what they said. The tall man had a guard with him. Cultivator. Qi condensation stage, maybe higher."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. A cultivator guard. That meant the tall man was either wealthy, powerful, or both. And Wu Chen was passing information to him.

"Did you see which direction they went?"

"East. Toward the noble district." Xiao Chen hesitated. "There's something else. Wu Chen had a mark on his hand. A tattoo, I think. I only saw it when he reached into his robe."

"What kind of mark?"

"A serpent. Wrapped around a sword."

Zhao Feng's blood turned to ice.

The Serpent Sword. In his past life, that symbol had belonged to a secret society within the merchant guild—a faction that had tried to recruit him several times. He'd refused, and they'd made his life difficult. But he'd never learned who led them.

Until now.

"You did well," Zhao Feng said, his voice steady despite the cold dread settling in his chest. "I need you to do something else. Find out everything you can about the Serpent Sword. Who uses that symbol. What they control. Be careful—if anyone catches you asking questions, you run. You don't fight. You don't try to explain. You run."

Xiao Chen nodded, his face serious beyond his years. "And you?"

"I have a meeting to prepare for." Zhao Feng stood, tucking the jade slip into his robe. "And a trap to avoid."

---

The next morning, Zhao Feng visited the public archives.

The building was old, its walls stained with centuries of smoke and dust. Rows of shelves stretched into the shadows, filled with scrolls and books that no one had touched in decades. The archivist, a wizened old man with clouded eyes, barely looked up as Zhao Feng entered.

"I need records of merchant guild transactions from the past five years," Zhao Feng said. "Specifically, anything related to restricted resources."

The archivist snorted. "Those records aren't public."

"I know. But I also know you keep copies of everything that passes through the guild's hands. And I know you have a granddaughter who needs medicine for her cough." Zhao Feng placed a pouch of spirit coins on the counter. "This should cover a year's supply."

The old man's hands trembled as he took the pouch. He didn't count the coins. He didn't need to. "Back room. Third shelf from the left. Don't take anything out of the building."

Zhao Feng found what he was looking for within an hour.

The pattern was subtle, buried in a maze of transaction records and shipping manifests. But once he saw it, he couldn't unsee it. Over the past three years, a network of buyers and sellers had been quietly moving restricted resources through the merchant guild's channels. The transactions were small, never enough to trigger an audit, but they were consistent. Growing.

And they all led back to one name: Lin Wei.

The name hit him like a physical blow.

Lin Wei. His fourth son. In his past life, the quiet one. The scholar. The one who'd spent hours in the family library, studying ancient texts and cultivation manuals. Zhao Feng had thought he was preparing for the civil exams. He'd been proud of his son's dedication.

Now he wondered what Lin Wei had really been studying.

Because in this life, Lin Wei was still a child. Still innocent. Still untouched by the betrayals that would come decades later.

Unless the betrayals had already begun.

Zhao Feng's hands gripped the edge of the table until his knuckles turned white. He forced himself to breathe. To think. To separate the past from the present.

Lin Wei was eight years old. He couldn't be behind this. But the name was there, clear as day, woven into the fabric of the conspiracy.

Someone was using his son's name.

Or his son was already being groomed for a role he didn't understand.

Either way, the game had changed.

Zhao Feng copied the relevant records onto a blank scroll, then slipped out of the archives as quietly as he'd entered. The morning sun was bright, harsh against his eyes. He blinked, adjusting, and nearly walked into a woman standing in the middle of the street.

She was young, maybe twenty, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to see through everything. Her robes were plain, but the quality was unmistakable—silk, with subtle embroidery that only a cultivator would recognize.

"You're Zhao Feng," she said. It wasn't a question.

"Who's asking?"

"My name is Li Mei. I'm an investigator for the Azure Sky Sect." She held up a badge, its surface gleaming with spiritual energy. "I need to ask you some questions."

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Rat in the Walls

The numbers in Zhao Feng's ledger told a story that made his blood run cold.

He ran the calculations three times, each time arriving at the same conclusion. His qi stone reserves were draining faster than they should. Not from theft—the seals on his crates remained undisturbed, the locks intact. But the stones themselves were losing potency. A slow degradation that shouldn't be possible with proper storage.

Someone was siphoning Qi from his warehouse.

Zhao Feng set down his brush and pressed his fingers to his temples. In his past life, he'd heard rumors of such techniques—parasitic cultivation methods that could drain energy through barriers, through distance, through the very fabric of storage arrays. But those were lost arts, buried with the ancient sects that had been destroyed during the Great Purge.

Unless someone had rediscovered them.

He extinguished his lantern and sat in the darkness, letting his eyes adjust. The warehouse had four walls, a ceiling of rotting timber, and a dirt floor packed hard by decades of use. If someone had planted a siphon array, it would be hidden. Buried. Possibly activated by a trigger word or a specific time of day.

He needed to find it before his entire stockpile turned to dust.

The knock came at midnight.

Zhao Feng rose from his crouch, brushing dirt from his robes. He'd spent the last three hours running his hands over every surface, feeling for the faint pulse of active Qi that would betray a hidden formation. Nothing. Either the siphoning was being done by an expert, or the array was buried deeper than he could sense.

"Enter," he called.

The door creaked open, and Wu Chen stepped inside, his round face gleaming with sweat despite the cool night air. Behind him came two men Zhao Feng didn't recognize—cultivators, by the look of their bearing. One wore a blue silk robe and carried a sword at his hip. The other was older, his face weathered, his eyes sharp.

"Brother Zhao," Wu Chen said, spreading his arms in a gesture of welcome that didn't reach his eyes. "I've brought associates. Important ones."

Zhao Feng didn't move from his position by the table. "You said nothing about bringing guests."

"Circumstances change." Wu Chen gestured to the blue-robed man. "This is Young Master Lin from the Lin family of Green Cloud City. He's interested in your... operation."

The Lin family. Zhao Feng kept his expression neutral, but his mind raced. The Lin family was a mid-tier clan, influential but not dominant. In his past life, they'd aligned themselves with his third son, providing military support in exchange for trade routes. They were wolves dressed in merchant's clothing.

"I wasn't aware I had an operation worth a Young Master's attention," Zhao Feng said carefully.

Young Master Lin stepped forward, his sword clinking against his hip. He was young—early twenties, with the arrogant posture of someone who'd never been told no. "Don't play games with me, merchant. I know what you're doing. Buying up low-grade qi stones, cornering the market in the outer districts. You're planning something."

"I'm planning to make a living."

"You're planning to disrupt the established order." Lin's eyes narrowed. "My family holds contracts with half the mining operations in the eastern provinces. If you think you can undercut us, you're either very brave or very stupid."

Zhao Feng felt Xiao Chen's presence before he saw him—a flicker of movement in the shadows near the roof. The boy had followed him after all. Good.

"I'm neither," Zhao Feng said. "I'm a merchant. I buy low, I sell high, and I pay my taxes. If your family's prices are too high for the common cultivator, that's not my concern."

Wu Chen laughed nervously. "Gentlemen, gentlemen. There's no need for hostility. Brother Zhao, Young Master Lin isn't here to threaten you. He's here to offer you a partnership."

"A partnership."

"Fifty percent of your operation," Lin said flatly. "In exchange for protection and access to our supply chains. You'll be a subsidiary of the Lin family. You'll answer to us."

Zhao Feng studied the young master's face. The arrogance was there, yes, but beneath it he saw something else. Desperation. The Lin family was feeling pressure from somewhere. They needed his supply network, his methods, his growing influence.

In his past life, he'd learned that desperation was the most dangerous currency. It made men do foolish things.

"I'll consider your offer," Zhao Feng said.

Lin's hand moved to his sword. "That's not an answer."

"It's the only one you're getting tonight." Zhao Feng met his gaze steadily. "I'm a simple merchant. I need time to review contracts, consult with my associates, ensure the terms are favorable. Surely a man of your standing understands the importance of due diligence."

For a long moment, the warehouse was silent. Then Lin laughed—a sharp, humorless sound.

"You've got balls, merchant. I'll give you that." He turned toward the door. "You have three days. After that, my offer becomes less... generous. Wu Chen will be in touch."

The door slammed shut behind them.

Zhao Feng waited until their footsteps faded before allowing himself to exhale. He turned to the shadows near the roof. "You can come down now."

Xiao Chen dropped silently to the floor, landing in a crouch. The boy moved like a ghost, his bare feet making no sound on the packed earth.

"He's lying," Xiao Chen said.

"Which part?"

"All of it. I followed him before he came here. He met with someone else first. A woman. She gave him orders."

Zhao Feng's heart rate spiked. "Describe her."

"Tall. Black hair with a white streak. Wore a red robe with gold embroidery. She had a scar on her left cheek, shaped like a crescent moon."

Zhao Feng felt the world tilt beneath him.

He knew that scar. He knew that woman.

In his past life, she'd been known as the Crimson Viper—an assassin and information broker who'd served his eldest son. She'd been the one who'd orchestrated the betrayal. The one who'd found his hidden vaults, his secret accounts, every detail of his operation.

And she was here. Now. Six months before she should have appeared.

"You're sure about the scar?" he asked, his voice carefully controlled.

"I don't forget faces," Xiao Chen said. "She's the one who killed Old Zhang last month. Cut his throat in broad daylight and walked away like nothing happened."

Old Zhang. Zhao Feng remembered the name—a minor merchant who'd tried to undercut the local gangs. His death had been written off as a random robbery.

But if the Crimson Viper was active, if she was giving orders to Wu Chen and the Lin family, then someone was already moving against him. Someone who knew what he was planning. Someone who'd seen the same future he had.

"Xiao Chen," he said slowly, "I need you to do something for me. Something dangerous."

The boy's dark eyes didn't waver. "What?"

"I need you to find out where that woman is staying. Where she meets people. Who she reports to." Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small jade token. "Take this. If you're caught, show it to them. Tell them you work for a merchant named Zhao Feng who wants to buy information. They'll believe you."

"And if they don't?"

"Then you run. You hide. And you find me."

Xiao Chen took the token, his fingers brushing against Zhao Feng's. The boy's hands were cold, calloused from years of survival.

"I'll do it," he said. "But I need to know something first."

"Ask."

"Who is she really? You recognized her. I saw it in your face."

Zhao Feng considered lying. Considered weaving a story about old enemies and past debts. But Xiao Chen was too sharp. The boy would see through it.

"She's a ghost from a life I used to live," Zhao Feng said. "A life I thought I'd left behind. She's here to make sure I don't succeed."

"Why?"

"Because I'm a threat to someone very powerful. Someone who doesn't want me to change the world."

Xiao Chen stared at him for a long moment. Then, slowly, he nodded.

"I'll be back by dawn," he said, and vanished into the night.

---

Zhao Feng didn't sleep.

He spent the remaining hours before daylight reinforcing the seals on his warehouse, adding layers of protective formations he'd learned in his past life. They weren't perfect—he lacked the cultivation base to power truly effective arrays—but they would slow down any thief long enough for him to respond.

As the first light of dawn crept through the cracks in the walls, he heard footsteps outside.

Not Xiao Chen's. These were heavier. Deliberate.

The door swung open, and a woman stepped inside.

She was tall, as Xiao Chen had described, with black hair that fell past her shoulders and a single white streak running through it like a bolt of lightning. Her red robe was immaculate, the gold embroidery catching the morning light. And on her left cheek, a crescent-shaped scar that seemed to glisten in the shadows.

"Cultivator Zhao," she said, her voice smooth as silk. "I've heard so much about you."

Zhao Feng's hand moved toward the dagger hidden in his sleeve.

"I wish I could say the same," he replied. "But you have me at a disadvantage."

The woman smiled, and it was the smile of a predator who'd already cornered her prey.

"Call me Lin Mei. I represent interests that would very much like to see you succeed." She stepped closer, her movements fluid, graceful. "Or fail. Depending on how our conversation goes."

"Then let's talk."

"Oh, we will." She reached into her robe and pulled out a folded piece of paper, tossing it onto the table between them. "But first, I wanted to deliver a message. From an old friend of yours."

Zhao Feng didn't touch the paper. "I don't have old friends."

"You do. You just don't remember them yet." She turned toward the door, pausing with her hand on the frame. "Read the message, Cultivator Zhao. And then decide whether you want to keep playing this game, or whether you'd rather walk away while you still can."

She left without another word.

Zhao Feng stood motionless for a long moment, the paper lying on the table like a coiled snake. Finally, he reached out and unfolded it.

The handwriting made his blood run cold.

It was his own.

*Zhao Feng—*

*If you're reading this, then time has moved differently than I expected. I don't know when this will reach you, but I know you'll remember.*

*Don't trust the woman in red. She serves the same master she always has.*

*And he knows you're here.*

*He's coming.*

*—Zhao Feng*

The signature was in his own hand. The same loops and curves he'd used for forty years in his past life. The same ink. The same paper.

But he'd never written this letter. He'd never sent it. And yet here it was, a warning from a version of himself that shouldn't exist.

He read it again, and felt the walls of his carefully constructed plan begin to crumble.

Someone else had been reborn.

And they were already three steps ahead.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Obsidian Token

The calculations blurred before Zhao Feng's eyes. He blinked, forcing his focus back to the ledger. Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. In his past life, the lightning techniques that propelled the unknown outer disciple to victory had come from the Obsidian Pavilion, a black-market auction house that operated in the shadows of Green Maple City.

But the Obsidian Pavilion didn't accept just anyone. They required an invitation, and those invitations were reserved for cultivators of the fifth rank or higher, or merchants with a net worth exceeding one hundred thousand spirit coins.

Zhao Feng had neither.

He set down his brush and rubbed his temples. The candle had burned down to a stub, casting dancing shadows across the warehouse walls. Outside, the night sounds of the merchant district had gone quiet. Even the dogs had stopped barking.

A soft tap came at the door.

Three knocks. Pause. Two knocks.

Xiao Chen's signal.

Zhao Feng rose, his muscles stiff from hours of hunching over the ledger. He unlatched the door and pulled it open. The boy slipped inside like a shadow, his thin frame barely making a sound. His eyes were bright, alert, and he carried the smell of smoke and cheap wine.

"He's here," Xiao Chen said, his voice barely above a whisper. "Wu Chen. He came to the district about an hour ago with three men. They're drinking at the Lotus Petal Tavern."

Zhao Feng's pulse quickened. "What are they discussing?"

"Business. He's bragging about a big shipment coming in three days. Something about 'settling accounts' with a merchant who cheated him." Xiao Chen paused, his young face serious. "He described you. Not by name, but he said 'the young fool who bought the tannery warehouse.' He knows you're operating here."

That was faster than Zhao Feng had expected. Wu Chen had connections throughout the city, tentacles that reached into every market and alley. But this soon? Someone had talked.

"Did he mention any names? Anyone who might have told him about me?"

Xiao Chen shook his head. "Just general threats. But there was something else." He reached into his tattered sleeve and pulled out a small object, holding it out to Zhao Feng. "One of his men dropped this when they left the tavern."

Zhao Feng took it. His fingers recognized the shape before his eyes did. A token, carved from black jade, with a single character etched into its surface: 影. Shadow.

The Obsidian Pavilion.

His hands trembled slightly. This wasn't just any token. This was a merchant's pass, a symbol that allowed the bearer to access the Pavilion's trade floors and bid on their most exclusive items. In his past life, such tokens had been worth more than most merchants earned in a decade.

"How did you get this?"

"I followed them after they left the tavern. One of the men stopped to relieve himself behind a teahouse. The token fell from his pocket. I waited until he was gone, then picked it up."

Zhao Feng stared at the boy. In his past life, Xiao Chen had died protecting him, but he'd never known the boy's full capabilities. This level of stealth, of quick thinking—it was exceptional.

"You did well," Zhao Feng said, his voice quiet. "Very well."

Xiao Chen's chest puffed slightly, but he kept his expression neutral. "What now?"

Now. Zhao Feng's mind raced. Wu Chen had a shipment coming in three days. He had a token to the Obsidian Pavilion. But more importantly, he had a connection to the auction house that Zhao Feng desperately needed.

"Now, we change our plans."

---

The next morning, Zhao Feng woke before dawn. He dressed in his finest clothes—still modest by merchant standards, but clean and presentable—and made his way to the Lotus Petal Tavern.

The establishment sat at the intersection of two main roads, its red lanterns still glowing in the gray morning light. The smell of rice wine and grilled meat clung to the air. A few early drinkers sat at the bar, nursing their cups with bloodshot eyes.

Zhao Feng took a seat in the corner, his back to the wall. He ordered a pot of tea and waited.

He didn't have to wait long.

Wu Chen descended the stairs at the hour of the dragon, his robes rumpled, his hair unbound. He looked like a man who had slept in his clothes, but his eyes were sharp, scanning the room with practiced efficiency. They landed on Zhao Feng and narrowed.

The big man walked over, his footsteps heavy on the wooden floor. He didn't sit.

"You're the one who bought the tannery warehouse."

It wasn't a question.

"I am," Zhao Feng said, pouring himself a cup of tea. "Join me. The tea is passable."

Wu Chen's lips curled. "I don't drink tea with people who owe me money."

"Owe you?" Zhao Feng raised an eyebrow. "I don't recall any transaction between us."

"No transaction." Wu Chen pulled out a chair and sat down, his weight making the wood groan. "But you're operating in my territory. Every merchant in this district pays me a protection fee. You haven't paid."

"I wasn't aware the merchant district had a landlord."

"It has me." Wu Chen leaned forward, his voice dropping to a growl. "You've been here three weeks. That's three weeks of missed payments. I'm generous, so I'll let you pay for the full month. Five hundred spirit coins. Plus interest."

Five hundred. Zhao Feng nearly laughed. That was more than most small merchants made in a year. Wu Chen wasn't asking for protection. He was asking for everything.

"And if I refuse?"

"Then your warehouse might have an accident. A fire, perhaps. Or a theft." Wu Chen smiled, showing yellowed teeth. "These old buildings, they burn so easily."

Zhao Feng took a slow sip of his tea, buying time. In his past life, he would have been intimidated. He would have paid, hoping to buy peace, only to discover that Wu Chen's demands would never stop.

But this wasn't his past life.

"I have a counteroffer," Zhao Feng said, setting down his cup. "I'll pay you nothing. Instead, I'll give you something worth far more."

Wu Chen's eyes flickered with interest. "What could a boy like you possibly have that I want?"

Zhao Feng reached into his sleeve and pulled out the Obsidian Pavilion token. He placed it on the table, face up, the character 影 catching the morning light.

"I know where you got this," Zhao Feng said. "And I know you're planning to use it to bid on the Lightning Condensation Art at the next auction."

The color drained from Wu Chen's face. His hand shot out, grabbing the token, but Zhao Feng was faster. He swept it off the table and back into his sleeve.

"How—" Wu Chen's voice cracked. "How do you know about that?"

"I know many things, Wu Chen. I know your shipment of spirit herbs coming in three days is worth thirty thousand spirit coins. I know you've mortgaged your wife's jewelry to fund it. And I know that if you don't win that bidding war, you'll lose everything."

Wu Chen's hands gripped the edge of the table, his knuckles white. "Who are you?"

"Someone who can help you." Zhao Feng leaned back, his posture relaxed, his voice calm. "I have information about the auction. I know who else is bidding, what their limits are, and how you can beat them."

"And what do you want in return?"

"Half of the Lightning Condensation Art."

Wu Chen laughed, a harsh, barking sound. "Half? You think I'd share the most valuable technique in the city with some gutter rat playing merchant?"

"I don't think you have a choice." Zhao Feng met his gaze without flinching. "Without my information, you'll bid blind. You'll either overpay and ruin yourself, or you'll lose to someone with deeper pockets. With my help, you have a chance."

The silence stretched between them. Wu Chen's chest heaved with barely contained rage. But beneath the anger, Zhao Feng saw something else: fear.

"One week," Wu Chen finally said, his voice tight. "I'll give you one week to prove your information is real. If it's not..." He let the threat hang in the air.

"Agreed."

Zhao Feng stood, leaving a few copper coins on the table for the tea. As he turned to leave, Wu Chen's voice stopped him.

"Boy. If you're lying to me, I'll make sure you disappear so completely that even the rats won't remember you existed."

Zhao Feng didn't turn around. "I'm not lying, Wu Chen. But you should know something."

"What?"

"The man who dropped this token." Zhao Feng held up the Obsidian Pavilion pass. "You might want to check his loyalties. He dropped it a little too easily."

He walked out of the tavern, the morning sun warm on his face. Behind him, he heard Wu Chen's fist slam against the table.

---

Xiao Chen was waiting for him at the warehouse, his small form tucked into the shadows of the doorway.

"You're alive," the boy said, his voice flat, but his eyes betraying his relief.

"Barely." Zhao Feng unlocked the door and stepped inside. "But we've bought ourselves a week."

"What happens in a week?"

Zhao Feng looked at the crates of qi stones, the bundles of spirit grass, the humble beginnings of what would one day be an empire. A week wasn't much time. But it was enough.

"In a week," he said, "we take the first step toward the Immortal Ascension Tournament. And Wu Chen becomes our unwitting partner."

Xiao Chen frowned. "You're going to betray him."

"Of course." Zhao Feng smiled, and there was no warmth in it. "I'm a merchant. Betrayal is just another transaction."

He turned to his ledger, his mind already racing with calculations. But before he could begin, a knock came at the door.

Three quick raps. Urgent.

Zhao Feng exchanged a glance with Xiao Chen. The boy slid to the side, pressing himself against the wall, a small knife appearing in his hand as if by magic.

Zhao Feng approached the door. "Who is it?"

A woman's voice answered, breathless and trembling. "Zhao Feng? Are you Zhao Feng?"

His blood ran cold. He recognized that voice. He'd heard it in his past life, on the day his sons had betrayed him.

It was his first wife. Yun Mei.

"I need to speak with you," she said, her voice cracking. "Please. It's about your father."

Zhao Feng's hand froze on the latch.

His father had died twenty years ago. In his past life.

But in this life, his father was still alive. Still a poor farmer in the outskirts of Green Maple City. Still breathing.

And Yun Mei had no reason to seek him out unless something had gone terribly wrong.

He pulled open the door.

Yun Mei stood there, her clothes disheveled, her face streaked with tears. She looked nothing like the cold, calculating woman he remembered from his past life. She looked terrified.

"Your father," she gasped. "They've taken him."

"Who?"

"The Wu family. They have him. They're holding him at their compound." She grabbed his arm, her fingers digging into his skin. "They said if you don't give them what they want by sunset tomorrow, they'll kill him."

The warehouse seemed to tilt around Zhao Feng. His mind raced, connecting the dots. Wu Chen. The token. The threat in the tavern.

Wu Chen hadn't believed Zhao Feng's

title: The Serpent's Whisper
content:
The warehouse door groaned open at midnight.

Zhao Feng looked up from his ledger, one hand already reaching for the dagger hidden beneath his sleeve. But the figure that slipped through the crack moved with a familiar gait—small, quick, shadow-like.

Xiao Chen.

The boy slid inside and closed the door behind him, his face pale even in the dim lantern light. He was breathing hard, his clothes wet with sweat despite the cool night air.

"He's not alone," Xiao Chen said without preamble.

Zhao Feng set down his brush. "Wu Chen?"

"Three others. They meet every night at the Drunken Crane Inn, back room." The boy's hands trembled as he spoke. "I followed them for three days. They're not just merchants. They're connected to someone big."

"Connected to who?"

"I don't know the name. But I heard them mention the Sun family."

The air in the room grew cold.

Zhao Feng's mind raced. The Sun family. One of the five great merchant clans of Greencloud City—and in his past life, one of the first to fall. They'd been aggressive, ruthless, and ultimately outmaneuvered by a consortium of smaller traders. But that was years from now. In the present, the Sun family was still a powerhouse, controlling the flow of mid-grade qi stones throughout the eastern provinces.

And they were already interested in him.

"How much do they know?" Zhao Feng asked.

"I don't think they know about you specifically. But they've been asking about the qi stone market. Who's been buying. Who's been selling. They're looking for patterns."

Zhao Feng nodded slowly. Of course. He'd been careful, but not careful enough. His purchases had been too strategic, too perfectly timed. Any experienced trader would notice that someone was manipulating the market.

The question was: had they identified him yet?

"Wu Chen leaves the inn at the third hour," Xiao Chen continued. "He always walks alone. Takes the alley behind the silk market. That's when he's most vulnerable."

"You want to ambush him."

"I want to know what he knows."

Zhao Feng considered the boy. In his past life, Xiao Chen had been a loyal soldier, a protector who threw himself into danger without hesitation. But he'd also been reckless, driven by emotion rather than strategy. That recklessness had gotten him killed.

"We're not going to attack him," Zhao Feng said.

"But—"

"We're going to give him exactly what he wants."

Xiao Chen's brow furrowed. "I don't understand."

Zhao Feng smiled. It wasn't a warm smile. "Wu Chen wants to find the person manipulating the qi stone market. So let's give him a lead. A false one. Someone else for the Sun family to chase while we work in the shadows."

The boy's eyes widened. "You want to set a trap."

"I want to build a wall between us and them. And the first brick of that wall is a believable lie."

---

The Drunken Crane Inn was a three-story establishment in the heart of the merchant district, known for its strong liquor and discreet clientele. Zhao Feng arrived just before the second hour, wearing a plain brown robe and carrying a ledger under his arm. He looked like a minor clerk, unremarkable and forgettable.

He took a table near the back, ordering a cup of tea he had no intention of drinking.

The inn was busy. Merchants haggled over prices. Cultivators flaunted their spirit swords. A pair of courtesans entertained a group of young nobles in the corner. Everyone was looking for something—profit, power, pleasure.

Zhao Feng watched the door.

An hour passed. Then another.

At the third hour, Wu Chen emerged from the back room.

He was a thin man with a weasel's face and restless eyes. His robes were expensive but worn, the mark of a man who'd risen fast and fallen harder. He carried a satchel close to his chest, as if afraid someone might snatch it.

Zhao Feng waited until Wu Chen reached the door, then stood and walked toward him.

They collided in the doorway.

"I'm terribly sorry," Zhao Feng said, bending to pick up his scattered ledger. "I wasn't watching where I—"

But Wu Chen wasn't looking at the ledger. He was staring at the pouch that had fallen from Zhao Feng's sleeve.

A pouch filled with mid-grade qi stones.

The weasel's eyes glinted. "That's quite a fortune for a simple clerk."

Zhao Feng's face went pale—a practiced reaction. "It's... it's not mine. I'm just delivering it."

"Delivering it? To whom?"

"I can't say." Zhao Feng's voice dropped to a whisper. "I've already said too much."

Wu Chen's hand shot out, gripping Zhao Feng's wrist. "You'll tell me, or I'll have the city guards check the origin of those stones. And we both know they weren't acquired through legal channels."

Zhao Feng's shoulders sagged. Defeated. Broken.

"Fine," he muttered. "There's a trader. Goes by the name Old Yang. He's been buying up qi stones for months, stockpiling them in a warehouse near the eastern gate. He's planning something big—an auction, maybe. But he's paranoid. He uses different people to move his goods so no one can track him."

Wu Chen's grip loosened. "Old Yang? I've never heard of him."

"That's the point. He keeps his name off the records. But I've seen him. Old man, white hair, walks with a limp. He always wears a jade ring on his right hand."

The weasel's eyes narrowed. "And why are you telling me this?"

"Because I don't want to go to prison for carrying stones I was paid to transport. I have a family. A daughter. Please, just let me go."

Wu Chen studied him for a long moment. Then, slowly, he released his grip.

"Go," he said. "But if I find out you're lying, I'll make sure you and your daughter disappear."

Zhao Feng nodded frantically, gathered his ledger, and fled into the night.

He didn't stop running until he was three streets away.

Then he slowed, straightened his robes, and allowed himself a small smile.

Old Yang didn't exist. Neither did the daughter. But the warehouse near the eastern gate? That belonged to a minor Sun family rival. And the jade ring? He'd seen a dozen merchants wearing similar ones in the past week.

By tomorrow, Wu Chen would be chasing shadows. And by the time he realized he'd been tricked, Zhao Feng would be long gone.

---

The next morning, Zhao Feng visited the Sun family's main trading post.

It was a bold move, reckless by any standard. But sometimes the best place to hide was in plain sight.

The trading post was a massive stone building at the center of the merchant district, guarded by cultivators in Sun family colors. Merchants lined up outside, hoping to secure contracts for qi stone shipments. The air smelled of incense and greed.

Zhao Feng walked past the line and approached the front door.

A guard blocked his path. "No appointments. Get in line."

"I'm not here for an appointment," Zhao Feng said. "I'm here to deliver a message to Young Master Sun Wei."

The guard's eyes widened. Sun Wei was the family's third son, a cultivation prodigy known for his temper and his appetite for risk. Mentioning his name was a gamble.

"The young master doesn't receive messengers without—"

"He'll receive this one." Zhao Feng pulled a sealed envelope from his robe. "Tell him it's about the eastern warehouse. He'll understand."

The guard hesitated, then took the envelope and disappeared inside.

Zhao Feng waited, his heart pounding.

Five minutes passed. Ten.

Then the guard returned, his expression different. Softer. "Follow me."

---

The interior of the trading post was even more opulent than the exterior. Silk tapestries covered the walls. Spirit lamps cast a warm glow over marble floors. Attendants moved silently, carrying trays of tea and documents.

Sun Wei sat in a private chamber at the back of the building, surrounded by maps and ledgers. He was young—mid-twenties—with sharp features and eyes that missed nothing. A mid-grade spirit sword hung at his waist.

He didn't look up when Zhao Feng entered.

"You have exactly three minutes to explain why I shouldn't have you thrown into the street," Sun Wei said, turning a page in his ledger.

"Because I know who's been manipulating the qi stone market."

Sun Wei's hand stopped. Slowly, he looked up.

"And who would that be?"

"A man named Wu Chen. He works for a rival family, though I haven't identified which one. He's been buying up stones through intermediaries, creating a false shortage to drive up prices."

Sun Wei's eyes narrowed. "That's a serious accusation."

"I have proof." Zhao Feng placed a second envelope on the table. Inside were documents he'd forged the night before—purchase orders, delivery schedules, all pointing to Wu Chen as the mastermind.

Sun Wei picked up the envelope but didn't open it. "Why are you telling me this? What do you want?"

"A position. In your trading operation."

A laugh. Cold and sharp. "You think you can walk in here, drop a name, and walk out with a job?"

"I think I can walk in here, give you the name of the man trying to destabilize your family's business, and prove my value. You need someone who understands the underground market. Someone who can navigate the shadows without getting caught."

"And you're that person?"

"I'm the only person who walked through your door with actionable intelligence instead of begging for scraps."

Sun Wei stared at him for a long moment. Then he opened the envelope and read.

The silence stretched.

Finally, he set the papers down. "Wu Chen works for the Sun family. Has for three years."

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

"Did you think I didn't know?" Sun Wei continued, his voice soft. Dangerous. "Did you think I wouldn't recognize a trap when I saw one?"

"I—"

"You came here to pit me against a loyal subordinate. To create chaos within my ranks. Either you're a fool, or you're working for someone who wants me dead."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. He'd miscalculated. Badly.

But before he could speak, Sun Wei held up a hand.

"However." The young master's lips curled into a thin smile. "Wu Chen has been getting too comfortable lately. Too greedy. And I've been looking for an excuse to replace him."

He leaned forward.

"So I'll give you a chance. Prove that Wu Chen is as corrupt as you claim. Bring me evidence I can use, and I'll give you the position you want."

"And if I fail?"

Sun Wei's smile didn't waver. "Then I'll have you killed. Slowly. In a way that makes everyone remember what happens to those who waste my time."

Zhao Feng forced himself to nod.

"I understand."

"Good. You have one week."

As Zhao Feng turned to leave, Sun Wei's voice stopped him.

"One more thing. What's your name?"

Zhao Feng paused at the door.

"Zhao Feng."

The young master's eyes flickered with something—recognition? Interest? It was impossible to tell.

"Interesting," Sun Wei said. "I look forward to seeing what you bring me."

---

Zhao Feng walked out of the trading post with his heart hammering against his ribs.

He'd survived. Barely.

But now he was trapped between two fires. Expose Wu Chen—and risk the Sun family discovering his true intentions. Or fail—and die.

There was only one way out

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Price of Trust

The lantern flickered as Zhao Feng traced his finger along the ledger's columns. Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. The numbers told him he was ahead of schedule—barely.

*Thirty-seven thousand spirit coins in liquid assets. Fourteen thousand in raw qi stones. A reliable supplier network extending three cities outward.*

In his past life, he'd taken twelve years to build this foundation. This time, he'd done it in three months.

But twelve years had also taught him that foundations crumbled faster than they rose.

A knock came at the warehouse door. Three quick raps, a pause, then two more. The signal he'd arranged with Xiao Chen.

Zhao Feng set down his brush. "Enter."

The door creaked open, and Xiao Chen slipped inside like a shadow. The boy had cleaned up over the past weeks—new clothes, hair tied back, eyes sharp instead of desperate. But tonight, something was wrong. His movements were too quick, too tight.

"The Wu Chen situation has changed," Xiao Chen said without preamble.

"Explain."

"You were right that he's not just a merchant. He's connected to the Black Lotus Society."

Zhao Feng's hand stilled on the ledger. The Black Lotus. He'd known they existed in this era, but he'd hoped to avoid their attention for at least another year. In his past life, they'd tried to recruit him three times. The third time, they'd sent assassins.

"How deep?"

"Deep enough that he reports to someone in the inner city. I followed him to a meeting last night. Three men, all wearing black robes with lotus embroidery. They talked about you."

"Specifics."

Xiao Chen's jaw tightened. "They know about the qi stone shipments. They know about the spirit grass deals. They know you're operating without backing from any major family." He paused. "They called you 'an unclaimed asset.'"

*Unclaimed.* The word grated. In his past life, he'd been claimed by his family, his sons, his so-called partners—each of them taking pieces until nothing remained. This time, he'd sworn to belong to no one.

"They want to recruit me," Zhao Feng said. It wasn't a question.

"Or eliminate you. The man in charge—the one Wu Chen reports to—said if you won't be useful, you'll be a threat. He gave Wu Chen a deadline. Seven days."

Zhao Feng leaned back, his mind racing. The Black Lotus in this era was smaller than he remembered, but no less dangerous. They operated in shadows, controlled information networks that spanned the empire, and had no qualms about removing obstacles.

"How much does Wu Chen know about my operations?"

"Not everything. He knows about the warehouse, but not the secondary location in the bamboo district. He knows you trade in qi stones, but not that you've started refining them."

*Good.* The refinement operation was his ace. Raw stones were common. Refined stones—purified, potent, consistent—were rare. In his past life, he'd cornered the refined stone market by accident, simply because no one else had thought to standardize the process.

"Xiao Chen, I need you to do something dangerous."

The boy straightened. "I've done dangerous before."

"This is different. I need you to let Wu Chen catch you."

Silence. The lantern flame danced, casting shadows across Xiao Chen's face.

"You want me to feed him information," the boy said slowly.

"Carefully selected information. True enough to be believed, false enough to be useless. You'll tell him I'm planning a major deal in three days—a shipment of high-grade qi stones from the northern trade route. You'll tell him the time, the location, the value."

"And when he shows up to steal it, he finds nothing."

"No. When he shows up, he finds exactly what I want him to find."

Xiao Chen's eyes narrowed. "You're baiting a trap for the Black Lotus. That's suicide."

"It's leverage. The Black Lotus respects power. If I can demonstrate that I know their movements better than they know mine, they'll reassess. They'll negotiate instead of attack."

"And if they decide to just kill you faster?"

Zhao Feng smiled, cold and thin. "Then I'll have to make sure they can't."

---

The next three days passed in a blur of preparation. Zhao Feng worked eighteen hours straight, sleeping in the warehouse on a pile of spirit grass sacks. His body ached—the body of an eighteen-year-old wasn't used to this pace—but he pushed through it.

On the second day, he visited the bamboo district location for the first time in a week.

It was a small building tucked between a noodle shop and a fortune teller's stall. The sign outside read "Chen Family Sundries" in faded characters. Inside, the shelves were lined with cheap trinkets and household goods—a perfect cover.

The real business happened in the basement.

Zhao Feng descended the stone stairs, his footsteps echoing in the narrow passage. The basement had been expanded, carved out by his hired hands over the past month. Four rooms now sat underground, each lined with shelves and workbenches.

In the first room, two alchemists—retired outer disciples from the Falling Star Sect—worked over bubbling cauldrons, refining raw qi stones into concentrated essence. The process was simple but tedious. Heat the stones to a precise temperature, extract the vapor, condense it into liquid form, then let it crystallize into refined stones.

The result was worth ten times the raw material.

In the second room, a young woman named Mei Lin sorted spirit herbs by grade. She'd been a beggar when Zhao Feng found her, her cultivation crippled by a failed breakthrough. But her eyes were sharp, and she had an instinct for quality that couldn't be taught.

"Three shipments arrived this morning," she said without looking up. "The cloudroot is excellent. The midnight lotus is mediocre at best."

"Replace the supplier for the lotus."

"Already done."

Zhao Feng allowed himself a small nod. The people he'd gathered weren't loyal yet—not truly. They worked for coin and survival. But they were competent, and competence could grow into loyalty with time.

He was inspecting the third room's stockpile when Xiao Chen appeared at the basement entrance, breathless.

"He took the bait."

Zhao Feng straightened. "Details."

"Wu Chen has assembled a group of enforcers. Twelve men, all qi condensation cultivators. They're planning to hit the northern trade route at midnight tonight. But there's something else."

"What?"

"The man Wu Chen reports to—the one I mentioned before—he's going to be there personally. He wants to watch."

Zhao Feng's heart rate increased, but his face remained stone. An audience. That complicated things. If the Black Lotus leader was present, the trap had to be perfect. One mistake, and he wouldn't just lose the shipment—he'd lose everything.

"Change of plans," Zhao Feng said. "The northern route was always a decoy. The real shipment is going through the eastern route, under cover of a funeral procession. But that doesn't matter anymore."

"What do you mean?"

"I'm not going to let them steal a decoy. I'm going to meet them."

Xiao Chen's face went pale. "That's insane."

"It's calculated. If I can speak directly to the Black Lotus leader, I can end this tonight. A few words of negotiation, and they'll leave me alone for years."

"And if the words don't work?"

Zhao Feng picked up a refined qi stone from the shelf. It pulsed with soft light, warm in his palm. In his past life, he'd learned to fight with words, coin, and contracts. But some battles couldn't be won with merchants' tools.

He still had his past life's cultivation memories. He still knew techniques that shouldn't exist for another hundred years.

"Then I'll use other words."

---

Midnight. The northern trade route stretched through a narrow valley, flanked by rocky outcroppings and ancient pines. The moon was hidden behind clouds, leaving the road in near-total darkness.

Zhao Feng stood in the center of the road, alone.

He wore simple merchant's robes, no armor. A single pouch hung at his belt, containing twenty refined qi stones—a fortune that would make most cultivators weep with envy.

The bait.

He heard them before he saw them. Footsteps, soft and deliberate, coming from both sides of the road. Shadows detached themselves from the trees, forming a loose circle around him.

Twelve men. Just as Xiao Chen had said.

They wore black robes, their faces hidden behind cloth masks. Qi fluctuations rippled around them like heat haze—all of them at the fifth level of qi condensation or higher. Dangerous, but not unbeatable.

"Wu Chen," Zhao Feng called out. "I know you're here. Show yourself."

A figure stepped forward from the circle, pulling down his mask. Wu Chen was a thin man with a weasel's face and nervous eyes. He looked like a merchant, which made him dangerous. Merchants understood that the best lies were wrapped in truth.

"Zhao Feng," Wu Chen said, his voice oily. "I must admit, I didn't expect you to come personally."

"I believe in handling my own affairs."

"A commendable trait. But foolish." Wu Chen gestured, and the circle tightened. "My employer wants to meet you. He's waiting at the ridge."

Zhao Feng followed his gaze. On the eastern ridge, a single figure stood silhouetted against the sky. Tall, draped in black, with the unmistakable aura of a foundation establishment cultivator.

*So the leader is here after all.*

"I'll go willingly," Zhao Feng said. "But call off your dogs. If they touch me, the deal is off."

Wu Chen's eyes flickered with amusement. "What deal? You're in no position to negotiate."

"I'm always in a position to negotiate." Zhao Feng opened the pouch at his belt, letting the refined qi stones catch the faint moonlight. "Do you know what these are?"

Wu Chen leaned forward, his expression shifting from amusement to hunger. "Refined stones. High quality. Where did you get these?"

"I made them. And I can make more. Thousands more. Your employer doesn't want to kill me, Wu Chen. He wants to own me. But I'm not for sale."

The circle of enforcers had stopped moving, their attention fixed on the glowing stones. Greed was a powerful tool—Zhao Feng had learned that lesson early.

"Tell your employer I want to discuss a partnership. Equal terms. I provide refined stones at a discount, and the Black Lotus provides protection and exclusive distribution rights in the inner city."

Wu Chen laughed. "You're asking the Black Lotus to work for you."

"I'm asking them to work *with* me. There's a difference."

The laugh died. Wu Chen's face hardened. "You're either very brave or very stupid."

"Those are often the same thing." Zhao Feng began walking toward the ridge, the enforcers parting before him. "Coming, Wu Chen? Or do you need permission from your master first?"

The weasel-faced merchant's jaw clenched, but he followed.

The walk to the ridge took ten minutes. Zhao Feng used every second to study his surroundings, counting the guards hidden in the shadows, noting the escape routes, memorizing the terrain.

When he reached the top, the figure was waiting.

He was older than Zhao Feng expected—maybe fifty, with graying hair and a face carved from stone. His cultivation aura pressed down like a physical weight, making it hard to breathe.

*Foundation establishment, fifth level. Dangerous, but not untouchable.*

"So," the man said, his voice deep and cold. "You're the merchant who's been causing ripples."

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Price of Knowledge

The lantern flickered as Zhao Feng's brush hovered over the ledger.

Six months until the Immortal Ascension Tournament. In his past life, he'd watched from the cheap seats, a nobody among thousands, while the lightning cultivator named Mei Lin had shattered every expectation. The major families had offered her everything—rank, resources, marriage alliances. She'd refused them all.

Three months later, she was dead.

The official story said a beast attack during a solo hunt. But Zhao Feng had seen the wounds. Clean cuts. Precise. The work of human hands, not claws.

He'd never learned who killed her. But he'd learned where she found her power.

The Forgotten Archives.

A repository of techniques and knowledge buried beneath the old academy, sealed for centuries because the elders deemed them too dangerous. In his past life, Mei Lin had stumbled upon them by accident. This time, Zhao Feng would find them first.

But first, he needed to survive the night.

---

A knock came at the warehouse door. Three sharp raps, then two soft ones.

Wu Chen was early.

Zhao Feng set down his brush and moved to the door, his footsteps deliberately slow. He unlatched the lock and pulled the door open just wide enough to see his visitor.

Wu Chen stood in the darkness, a hooded lantern in one hand and a leather satchel in the other. His face was pleasant, almost friendly, with the kind of smile that made you want to trust him.

Zhao Feng knew better.

"Brother Zhao," Wu Chen said, bowing slightly. "I hope I'm not interrupting."

"Not at all." Zhao Feng opened the door wider. "Please, come in."

Wu Chen stepped inside, his eyes scanning the warehouse with practiced efficiency. He noted the crates, the stacks of spirit grass, the scale and ledger on the table. Nothing escaped his gaze.

"You've been busy," he said, setting his satchel on the table. "I'm impressed. When we first met, I thought you were just another small-time trader trying to flip a few stones. But this..." He gestured at the organized stockpile. "This is the work of someone who knows what he's doing."

"I've had good teachers," Zhao Feng said, keeping his voice neutral.

"Modest. I like that." Wu Chen opened his satchel and pulled out a small jade box. "I brought what you asked for. Mid-grade qi stones, forty of them, as promised."

He placed the box on the table and opened the lid. The stones inside glowed with a soft blue light, their energy palpable even from a distance. Zhao Feng leaned in, examining them closely.

They were real. Genuine mid-grade stones, properly formed, with no cracks or impurities.

But something was wrong.

In his past life, Zhao Feng had handled millions of qi stones. He knew their weight, their texture, their energy signature. These stones had the right appearance, but the energy felt... thin. Like watered-down wine.

He picked one up, holding it between his thumb and forefinger. The stone was warm, which was normal. But when he pressed his cultivation sense into it, the qi resisted. It didn't flow smoothly, like it should. It felt sticky. Clogged.

These stones had been drained and recharged.

Someone had extracted the original qi, then refilled the stones with lower-quality energy. It was an old scam, one that required significant skill to pull off. The stones would work for a few days, maybe a week, before the false qi dissipated and left nothing but useless rock.

"Something wrong?" Wu Chen asked, his smile still in place.

Zhao Feng looked up, meeting the man's eyes. "These stones have been tampered with."

Wu Chen's smile didn't waver. "I don't know what you mean."

"I think you do." Zhao Feng set the stone back in the box. "The energy signature is wrong. Someone drained these stones and refilled them with low-grade qi. They'll last maybe five days before they become worthless."

For a long moment, Wu Chen said nothing. Then his smile disappeared, replaced by something cold and calculating.

"You have a sharp eye, Brother Zhao. Sharper than I expected."

"I've been cheated before. I learned to spot the signs."

"A pity." Wu Chen reached into his satchel again, but this time his hand came out holding a small dagger. The blade caught the lantern light, gleaming with a faint green tint. Poison. "I was hoping we could do this the easy way."

Zhao Feng didn't move. "The easy way?"

"The easy way is you give me everything you have, and I let you walk away with your life." Wu Chen's voice was flat, professional. "The hard way is I take everything and leave your body in the gutter for the rats."

"You're not here to trade."

"No. I'm here to collect." Wu Chen circled the table, the dagger held loosely in his hand. "You've been making waves, Brother Zhao. Buying up qi stones, undercutting established merchants, making deals with people who should know better. The families are starting to notice. And they don't like what they see."

"The families sent you?"

"The families don't dirty their hands with street-level work. They hired someone. I'm that someone." Wu Chen shrugged. "It's nothing personal. You're just in the way."

Zhao Feng's mind raced. His cultivation was still weak, barely at the third level of Qi Condensation. Against a trained assassin, he had no chance in a direct fight. But he hadn't survived two lifetimes by fighting fair.

"I'll make you a counter-offer," Zhao Feng said, his voice calm.

Wu Chen laughed. "You're in no position to negotiate."

"Thirty thousand spirit coins."

The laughter stopped. "What?"

"Thirty thousand spirit coins. That's what I'll pay you to walk away tonight and tell whoever hired you that I'm not worth the trouble."

"And why would I believe you have thirty thousand spirit coins?"

Zhao Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a wooden token. It was plain, unremarkable, with a single character carved into its surface: *Jade*.

Wu Chen's eyes widened.

"You recognize this," Zhao Feng said. "Good. Then you know what it means."

The Jade Token was one of the most valuable items in the city. It granted its bearer access to the Jade Pavilion, the most exclusive auction house in the Eastern Continent. Only a handful of people possessed such tokens, and they were never given lightly.

"Where did you get that?" Wu Chen's voice had lost its professional edge.

"That's not important. What's important is what I can do with it." Zhao Feng held the token up. "The next Jade Pavilion auction is in three months. I have enough stockpiled to secure a seat at the main table. Do you know what happens at the main table?"

Wu Chen's grip on the dagger tightened.

"Deals worth millions," Zhao Feng continued. "Cultivation techniques that can break through bottlenecks. Spirit weapons that can turn a nobody into a master. And the people who sit at that table? They don't forget favors. They also don't forget slights."

"Are you threatening me?"

"I'm offering you a choice." Zhao Feng set the token on the table between them. "Take thirty thousand spirit coins, delivered in three installments, and walk away. Or try to kill me, and spend the rest of your life looking over your shoulder, wondering which of the families I've befriended will come for you."

Wu Chen stared at the token, his jaw working.

"You're bluffing," he finally said.

"Am I?" Zhao Feng smiled. "You've been watching me for weeks, Wu Chen. You know I'm not a fool. You know I've been planning something. Do you really think I'd let myself be caught unprepared?"

From outside the warehouse, a sound drifted in. Soft footsteps. Multiple sets of them.

Wu Chen's head snapped toward the door.

"That's my insurance," Zhao Feng said. "Street kids. Twelve of them. They've been watching this warehouse since you arrived. If I don't walk out of here alive, they'll spread the word about what happened here. They'll describe your face, your clothes, your fighting style. By sunrise, every information broker in the city will know who killed Zhao Feng."

"You're insane."

"No. I'm prepared." Zhao Feng picked up the token and tucked it back into his robe. "Now. Do we have a deal?"

The silence stretched.

Then, slowly, Wu Chen lowered the dagger. "Three installments. First one by tomorrow night."

"Done."

"And I want the names of your street kids. I need to know who's watching me."

"Not possible. But I'll give you something better." Zhao Feng reached into his pocket and pulled out a small slip of paper. "The name of the person who hired me to buy up those qi stones. You can sell that information for twice what I'm paying you."

Wu Chen took the paper, reading it quickly. His eyes narrowed. "This is real?"

"It's real. I've been working for a minor branch of the Liu family. They're trying to corner the qi stone market before the tournament. Your employer probably already knows, but now you have proof."

Wu Chen pocketed the paper. Then, without another word, he turned and walked out of the warehouse, disappearing into the night.

Zhao Feng waited until the footsteps faded completely before he let out a breath he didn't realize he'd been holding.

He hadn't had any street kids watching. He'd gambled everything on a bluff.

But he'd won.

For now.

He looked down at the jade box of fake qi stones, then at his ledger, then at the token hidden in his robe. The pieces were starting to come together, but the game was only beginning.

Someone had sent Wu Chen. Someone with enough money to hire a professional assassin, enough influence to track his movements, and enough paranoia to want him dead before he became a real threat.

The question was: who?

And more importantly: how much did they know?

---

Dawn found Zhao Feng standing on a hill overlooking the city's outer district. Below him, the morning markets were coming to life, merchants setting up stalls, customers haggling over prices, children running through the streets.

In the distance, he could see the Jade Pavilion's golden roof, catching the first rays of sunlight.

Three months until the auction. Six months until the tournament.

He had so much to do, and so little time.

A movement caught his eye. Xiao Chen, climbing the hill toward him, his thin frame silhouetted against the rising sun.

"I followed Wu Chen," the boy said, breathing hard. "Like you asked."

"And?"

"He went to a teahouse in the inner district. Met with a woman. Rich clothes, lots of jewelry." Xiao Chen paused. "She had a tattoo. A silver serpent wrapped around a tree."

Zhao Feng's blood ran cold.

The Silver Serpent Clan. One of the most powerful underground organizations in the Eastern Continent. In his past life, they'd controlled the black market for cultivation resources, ruling through fear and violence.

But they weren't supposed to be here. Not yet. Not for another five years.

"Did you hear anything they said?"

Xiao Chen shook his head. "Too far away. But she gave him something. A bag, heavy. Sounded like coins."

Gold. Payment for a job well done. Or payment for a job still in progress.

"Good work." Zhao Feng reached into his pocket and pulled out a few copper coins, pressing them into the boy's hand. "Get some food. Rest. I'll find you tonight."

"Are you in trouble?" Xiao Chen asked, his young face serious.

"Not yet." Zhao Feng turned back to look at the city. "But I

title: The Poison in the Wine
content:
The lantern flickered as Zhao Feng set down his brush. His calculations covered three pages of the ledger, each line a thread in the web he was weaving. The Immortal Ascension Tournament was his target, but the path there required stepping stones he hadn't yet placed.

A knock at the warehouse door broke his concentration.

Three quick raps. A pause. Two more.

Xiao Chen's signal.

Zhao Feng crossed the room and pulled back the bolt. The boy slipped inside like a shadow, his thin frame barely visible in the dim light. His eyes were wide, his breathing rapid.

"Wu Chen isn't just a merchant," Xiao Chen said without preamble. "He's a spy for the Jin family."

Zhao Feng felt the words land like stones in his stomach. The Jin family. One of the three major merchant clans that dominated Greencloud City. In his past life, they'd been his rivals for decades before he'd crushed them.

"You're certain?"

"I followed him to a teahouse in the eastern district. He met with a man wearing Jin family colors. They spoke for an hour. Wu Chen left with this." Xiao Chen pulled a folded piece of paper from his tattered sleeve. "He dropped it when he paid for his tea. I grabbed it before the waiter could clean up."

Zhao Feng took the paper and unfolded it. His eyes scanned the elegant script, and his blood ran cold.

*The boy has acquired a warehouse on Merchant's Row. Stockpile of low-grade qi stones estimated at ten thousand spirit coins. No known backing from any major family. He operates alone. Recommend we approach with caution or eliminate before he grows. —W.C.*

Below the message, someone had scrawled a response in crimson ink:

*Proceed with elimination. Make it look like a robbery. —J.*

Zhao Feng read the words twice. Then a third time.

In his past life, he'd destroyed the Jin family. Ruined their businesses, bought their debts, scattered their heirs to the winds. They'd never seen it coming until it was too late.

This time, they'd noticed him before he'd even taken his first real step.

"You need to leave," Xiao Chen said, his voice barely above a whisper. "Tonight. They're coming."

"When?"

"I don't know. But the response was fresh. The ink was still wet when I found it."

Zhao Feng looked around the warehouse. His stockpile. His foundation. Everything he'd built in the past two months. If he abandoned it, he'd be back to nothing. If he stayed, he'd be dead.

There had to be another way.

"How did they find me?" he asked, more to himself than to Xiao Chen.

"You bought a warehouse. You've been trading qi stones. You're eighteen years old with no family name and no backing. You stood out." The boy's voice held a wisdom that belied his years. "In this city, being noticed is the same as being targeted."

Zhao Feng closed his eyes. The boy was right. He'd been too aggressive, too visible. In his past life, he'd had decades to build his influence slowly. This time, he'd tried to sprint before learning to walk.

The Jin family had seen him as a weed growing in their garden. Weeds were pulled out before they could spread roots.

"I need to think," Zhao Feng said. "Give me a moment."

He paced the length of the warehouse, his mind racing through possibilities. Flight was the safest option, but it would cost him everything. Fighting was suicide—he was barely at the second level of qi condensation, no match for even a low-tier Jin enforcer.

But what if he didn't fight? What if he changed the game entirely?

He stopped pacing.

"Xiao Chen," he said slowly. "Who is the Jin family's biggest rival?"

"The Zhao family. They've been fighting over the spirit ore trade for three generations."

The Zhao family. His own family name, though no relation. In his past life, the Zhao clan had been his strongest allies before his sons had turned them against him.

"How do I get a meeting with their patriarch?"

Xiao Chen's brow furrowed. "You don't. The Zhao patriarch only meets with established merchants. He won't even talk to street traders."

"Then I need to give him a reason."

Zhao Feng walked to his desk and pulled out a fresh sheet of paper. He dipped his brush in ink and began to write. Not a request for a meeting. An offer.

*To the honorable Zhao patriarch,*

*I have come into possession of information regarding Jin family trade routes through the Spiritwood Forest. I believe this information would be of great value to your house. In exchange, I seek protection and a partnership.*

*I await your response at the warehouse on Merchant's Row.*

*—Zhao Feng*

He sanded the ink and folded the paper. "Take this to the Zhao family compound. Slip it under the gate if you have to. Make sure it reaches the patriarch's hands."

Xiao Chen took the letter, his expression uncertain. "What if they don't respond?"

"Then I'll think of something else. Go."

The boy vanished into the night.

Zhao Feng turned back to his ledger. The Jin family's enforcer would come. Maybe tonight, maybe tomorrow. He had to be ready.

He pulled a small pouch from his robe—a collection of spirit herbs he'd been saving for a breakthrough pill. Instead, he began mixing them into a powder. Not for cultivation. For defense.

The hours passed. The lantern burned low. Zhao Feng worked by feel, his fingers moving with the precision of forty years' experience. When he was done, he had three small packets of powder, each capable of inducing temporary paralysis in anyone who inhaled it.

Not lethal. But effective.

He placed one packet in his sleeve, one under his desk, and one by the door.

Then he waited.

Dawn came, gray and cold. Zhao Feng had just begun to think the Jin family might have postponed their attack when he heard footsteps outside. Not the careful approach of a single assassin. The heavy tread of multiple boots.

He counted. Four. Maybe five.

He blew out the lantern and pressed himself against the wall, the packet of powder ready in his hand.

The lock on the door clicked. Someone had picked it. The door swung open, and a sliver of morning light cut across the floor.

A man's voice, rough and low: "Check the corners. Make sure he's alone."

Shadows moved. Zhao Feng held his breath.

A figure passed within inches of him, close enough that he could smell the cheap wine on the man's breath. He didn't move. Didn't breathe.

The search took three minutes. When it was done, the voice spoke again: "Clear. Where's the target?"

"Patriarch said he sleeps here. Must have left early."

"Doesn't matter. Take the stones. Burn the rest. Make it look like a robbery gone wrong."

Zhao Feng's hand tightened around the powder packet. He could take out one, maybe two of them before they overwhelmed him. That wasn't enough.

But then he heard a new sound. A commotion from outside. Shouts. The clatter of running feet.

"Someone's coming!" one of the enforcers hissed. "A lot of someones."

"Abort. Move, move, move!"

The shadows retreated. The door slammed shut. Boots pounded away into the distance.

Zhao Feng stayed pressed against the wall, his heart hammering. He counted to sixty before he allowed himself to move.

He opened the door and looked outside.

A group of men in blue robes stood in the street, their leader a tall man with graying temples and eyes like chips of ice. He held Zhao Feng's letter in his hand.

"You're the boy who sent this?" the man asked, his voice carrying the weight of absolute authority.

Zhao Feng stepped forward and bowed. "I am."

The man studied him for a long moment. Then he smiled, and there was no warmth in it.

"I am Zhao Wuji, patriarch of the Zhao family. You claim to have information about Jin trade routes." He folded the letter and tucked it into his sleeve. "If you're lying to me, boy, I'll have your tongue."

"I'm not lying," Zhao Feng said.

"Then tell me what you know."

Zhao Feng met the patriarch's gaze. In his past life, he'd negotiated with emperors and sect leaders. A city merchant clan didn't intimidate him.

"Not here," he said. "Inside. And I want something in return before I share anything."

Zhao Wuji's eyes narrowed. "You're in no position to make demands."

"I'm in every position to make demands," Zhao Feng replied. "Because if I don't give you this information, the Jin family will control the Spiritwood Forest trade within a month. And when they do, your family will be finished."

A tense silence fell over the street. Zhao Wuji's men shifted uneasily, their hands moving toward their weapons.

The patriarch's smile faded entirely.

"You speak boldly for a dead man walking."

"I speak boldly because I'm the only one who can save you."

Zhao Wuji stared at him for a long, uncomfortable moment. Then, slowly, he nodded.

"Inside. We talk."

Zhao Feng stepped aside and let the patriarch enter. As the Zhao family men filed past him, he caught a glimpse of movement at the end of the street.

A figure in gray robes, watching.

The figure raised a hand in a casual wave, then turned and disappeared around a corner.

Zhao Feng's blood went cold.

He knew that gesture. He'd seen it a hundred times in his past life.

The Jin family had just learned that the Zhao patriarch had come to visit him personally.

And that meant the game had just escalated far beyond what he'd planned.
